question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether they both received advance personal invitations to the official events in Washington DC during the St. Patrick's Day period.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any departmental budget lines that had an underspend in the 2025-26 financial year.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any resource issues being experienced by her Department's Southern Section Office that are causing delays in responding to MLAs' correspondence.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any assessment that has been made as to whether the presence of the First Minister, alongside the deputy First Minister, would have added value to the visit to Washington DC over the St. Patrick's Day period.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 41692 22-27; to detail (i) whether her Department has a dedicated budget to facilitate expenditure on injury collision cluster sites; (ii) the reason why it was not included in the 2025-26 work programme, given that the junction was identified as having a significant collision history; and (iii) all the injury collision sites in the Southern Division that were recommended in the November 2025 review, to be subject to remedial work in the current financial year.
answer_text: I can advise that our Local Transport Safety Measures (LTSM) budget facilitates expenditure on safety related measures which schemes such as the road safety measures which were recently introduced at the Ballymiscaw Road / Whinney Hill junction.Officials have confirmed that the review of the injury collision cluster sites was completed in November 2025.  The Annual Report to Ards and North Down Council was published on 14 October 2025 and as this was after the publication date, the scheme could not be included.I am advised the Ballymiscaw Road / Whinney Hill junction was a high priority site as part of this collision remedial review, due to the extent of collisions over the last three years. It has been delivered as part of our LTSM programme, this financial year, to enhance driver awareness and improve road safety on this busy part of the road network. Whilst there were other sites identified as part of the collision remedial review, their development and implementation will be considered in a future works programme, dependent on the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 41691/22-27, to detail (i) whether the completion date of spring summer 2026 was a typographical error; (ii) the circumstances of the work instruction being overlooked; (iii) when her officials become aware that it had been overlooked; and (iv) whether any other work instructions had been overlooked at that time.
answer_text: I can advise that the completion date of spring/summer 2026 was a typographical error, as the works were originally planned for completion during spring/summer 2025.As previously outlined in my response to AQW 41691, during the contract transition, the instruction was inadvertently overlooked and the work was not carried out as planned. Officials became aware of this during an inspection of the A48 Gransha Road South which was undertaken in preparation of the response to the Member’s previous question, when it was noted that the planned works had not been completed. Records have been checked and there is no evidence to indicate that any other work instructions were overlooked during the contract changeover.As previously communicated, the instruction has since been reissued and the current contractor has been directed to prioritise completion of the works as soon as weather conditions permit.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any assessment that has been made of the impact on the Northern Ireland economy of not visiting Washington DC during her engagements in the United States of America over the St. Patrick's Day period.
answer_text: In March, I travelled to the US along with officials from Invest NI, Tourism Ireland, NI Screen and Studio Ulster. The focus of this visit was on economic opportunities from the West Coast of the US. I also travelled to New York where I visited the headquarters of iVerify, a company which is expanding their presence here.The visit focused on strengthening relationships with existing investors, accelerating potential expansions and new projects, promoting the North as a location for large-scale TV and movie production and highlighting the region’s unique cultural and natural assets as a must-see tourism destination.  Our key agencies charged with building overseas markets and business including Invest NI and Tourism Ireland took part in St. Partick’s Day engagements across the Americas including Washington DC.  I attended the opening of Invest NI’s new office location in San Francisco that will enhance Invest NI’s presence in the West Coast. The dedicated team will continue to support our local companies with their exports to the US and promote the North as an attractive investment location for US companies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 16519/22-27, for an update on the outcome of this review.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that a desktop site assessment has now been completed. Once the relevant Schedule of Accommodation has been confirmed, the EA will progress a feasibility report on the proposed use of the Cotton Primary School site.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any assessment that has been made of the benefits to Northern Ireland in attending St. Patrick's Day events in Washington DC.
answer_text: We use our engagement with international partners to promote this region as a place where people want to live and work, to visit, to study and invest, and to build the relationships that can help us deliver the Executive’s priorities.Global outreach and inward investment are important factors in helping us to achieve these goals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 27495/22-27, whether she is now in a position to detail a timeline for completion of this work.
answer_text: As I have previously advised, my Department issued an instruction to its lining contractor to refresh all road markings on the A48 Gransha Road South, between the Kerrs Road junction and the approach to Six Road Ends. That work was scheduled to be completed during spring/summer 2026.The instruction was issued under the previous lining contract, which ended on 30 June 2025. Regrettably, during the transition to the new contract that commenced on 1 July 2025, the instruction was overlooked and the work did not proceed as intended.As the completion of road marking works is dependent on suitable weather conditions, it is not possible at this stage to provide a precise completion date. However, I can confirm that the current contractor has been directed to prioritise the completion of this work as soon as conditions allow.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 41158/22-27, to detail (i) when the decision to progress this project was taken; (ii) whether the project was highlighted in detailed terms, with exact location, in the work programme for 2025-26, previously presented to Ards and North Down Borough Council; (iii) any proposals her Department has to improve sight lines for vehicles exiting Ballymiscaw Road onto Whinney Hill, in relation to traffic approaching the junction from Holywood; and (iv) whether any assessment has been made of the slip lane contributing to collisions at this juncti
answer_text: The Department completed a review of injury collision cluster sites in its Southern Division in November 2025, which included the Ballymiscaw Road / Whinney Hill junction. That review identified a significant collision history and concluded that restricted visibility caused by vehicles using the leftturn slip lane was the primary safety issue. The recommended measure was the removal of the slip lane to improve intervisibility and reduce conflict with westbound traffic towards Holywood.Although the scheme was not included in the 2025/26 work programme previously presented to Ards and North Down Borough Council, the findings of the November review and further injury collisions over the Christmas period led officials to accelerate its delivery, with construction beginning in early March 2026. The Department currently has no further plans for additional sightline improvements, but the impact of the scheme will be monitored and further measures considered if appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the release of funding to football clubs that met the criteria under the Northern Ireland Football Fund.
answer_text: A total of 37 applicants applied to The Northern Ireland Football Fund (TNIFF) Performance Programme. All met the eligibility criteria to apply. Only 32 applications met the minimum score required in all application categories. In September 2025, I announced the first cohort of 20 applicants which would be progressed to the next stage of the TNIFF Performance Programme. Last month these 20 clubs received letters of offer for funding to complete outline business cases for their projects. 10 clubs who were previously funded through the Football Ground Business Case Development Fund were offered up to £10k for an updated business case and the remaining 10 clubs were offered up to £30k to prepare business cases. No expenditure decisions have yet been taken in relation to award of capital grant under the TNIFF Performance Programme. These decisions can only be considered when business cases have been received and considered for approval.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission for an update on repairs to the roof of Parliament Buildings.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for her assessment of the road-safety impact of the proposed closure of the left-turn slip road from the Whinney Hill, Craigantlet, onto the Ballymiscaw Road; (ii) for her assessment of the adequacy of the sightlines for traffic turning right out of the Ballymiscaw Road onto Whinney Hill; and (iii) to detail the number of written submissions her Department has received requesting the closure of the left turn slip road, in the last five years.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. In light of the number of injury collisions at this junction, my officials assessed the contributory factors and developed a design proposal to remove the leftturn slip lane. This measure is intended to improve visibility, reduce vehicle movements and conflict points, and directly address the collision risks identified at this location. The removal of the slip lane will also improve sightlines for vehicles turning right out of the Ballymiscaw Road onto Whinney Hill, where visibility is currently restricted by vehicles using the slip lane. I can confirm that my Department has received no written submissions requesting the closure of the leftturn slip road at this location in the last five years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any guarantee period applied to utility companies, for road and footpath reinstatements; (ii) the number of reinstatements that are physically inspected by Departmental officials; (ii) the percentage of these inspections that have resulted in contractors being called back to correct reinstatements, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Street Works (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 forms the legislative framework for street works by statutory undertakers and requires the undertaker to comply with the Specification for the Reinstatement of Openings in Roads.The guarantee period for utility companies on the permanent reinstatement is two years, increased to three years for a deep excavation.My Department applies the procedures for inspection of reinstatements and for dealing with reinstatement failures as set out in the Northern Ireland Road Authority and Utilities Committee (NIRAUC) Code of Practice for Inspections. Where a statutory undertaker fails to carry out reinstatement to an acceptable standard, the undertaker, not the contractor, is duly notified to carry out remedial works to bring the reinstatement to the required standard.The number of reinstatement inspections, reinstatement defects and percentage requiring follow-up until such time as the defect is repaired by the statutory undertaker in each of the last five years are detailed in the table below: 20242023202220212020Reinstatement Inspections 955896341006297058948 Reinstatement Defects62790912591011759Percentage6.6%9.4%12.5%10.4%8.5%

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for her assessment of why consumer choice in Northern Ireland for some electrical goods, such as tumble dryers, is more limited than in the rest of the United Kingdom.
answer_text: Responsibility for energy-related product regulations, including energy labelling and ecodesign, is a reserved matter for the British Government, specifically the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero. However, I recognise the potential impacts on consumers in the North, and officials continue to engage with counterparts in Whitehall while supporting the Consumer Council.From 1 July 2025, new EU ecodesign and energy labelling requirements have applied in the North under the Windsor Framework. These changes have effectively phased out air-vented and condenser models, leaving more energy-efficient heat pump models available. As equivalent requirements do not yet apply in Britain, less efficient models remain available there.Some manufacturers and retailers may also have taken commercial decisions not to supply certain models to the North due to temporary divergence in energy labelling regimes. The Consumer Council is aware of these issues and continues to monitor consumer experiences through its EU Exit research, including questions on household appliances in its 2026/27 programme.The British Government has indicated its intention to align ecodesign requirements with the EU, which would resolve the current disparity. Officials continue to work closely with counterparts to support this aim.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for her assessment of the condition of the seawall that partially collapsed recently at Donaghadee seafront; (ii) to detail when the integrity of the wall was last inspected by her Department; and (iii) whether any work has been carried out recently on the footpath in the area of the collapsed wall.
answer_text: Following a recent period of severe weather and tidal surges along the North Down coastline, a short section of wall under my Department’s ownership partially collapsed on Thursday 5th February. Officials closed an adjacent section of footway and nearby car parking in the interest of public safety until repairs could be assessed and undertaken. I am pleased to confirm that repair work commenced on 10 February and will take approximately one week to complete. While on site, officials will also inspect the remaining length of Department maintained sea wall and address any further areas of concern identified.My Department carries out regular routine safety inspections of the road network at this location. The most recent inspection was undertaken on 12 November 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of structures ordered to be removed after a retrospective planning application has been submitted, broken down by local council area, in the last ten years.
answer_text: My Department does not hold this information.  The issues you raise are a matter for local councils; you may wish to approach the individual councils for this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) his assessment of traffic issues on My Lady's Mile, Holywood, during drop off and pick up times at two primary schools and two secondary schools in the vicinity; and (ii) whether his officials will liaise with the schools involved, to identify any measures that could be put in place to ease traffic congestion and improve road safety.
answer_text: Responsibility for traffic management beyond the school boundary lies with the Department for Infrastructure (DfI). The Infrastructure Minister, Liz Kimmins MLA, has confirmed to me that DfI officials have identified recurring problems with vehicles parking on the existing double yellow lines. These restrictions are subject to ongoing enforcement with regular patrols undertaken as part of routine operations and in response to specific complaints. To strengthen enforcement in this area, DfI officials have arranged for increased patrols along My Lady’s Mile throughout February, with several visits scheduled each week. This targeted approach, particularly focused on peak school times, is intended to effectively address parking violations and ease congestion. DfI officials will monitor this location in the coming weeks to determine whether current measures remain appropriate and whether any further measures may be beneficial.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of traffic issues on My lady's Mile, Holywood, during drop-off and pick-up times at two primary schools and two secondary schools, in the vicinity.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. I am aware, however, that traffic congestion around schools during morning drop-off and afternoon pick-up periods is a common issue across many locations.In the case of My Lady’s Mile, officials have identified recurring problems with vehicles parking on the existing double yellow lines. These restrictions are subject to ongoing enforcement with regular patrols undertaken as part of routine operations and in response to specific complaints.To strengthen enforcement in this area, officials have arranged for increased patrols along My Lady’s Mile throughout February, with several visits scheduled each week.  This targeted approach, particularly focused on peak school times, is intended to effectively address parking violations and ease congestion.My officials will monitor this location in the coming weeks to determine whether current measures remain appropriate and whether any further measures may be beneficial.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the work carried out to date by the Regional Clinical Director for Elective Care.
answer_text: Since his appointment in July 2025 to the Regional Clinical Director for Elective Care role, Professor Mark Taylor has been instrumental in overseeing, leading and supporting the drive to reduce waiting lists.  Professor Taylor provides specialist clinical leadership and advice on the transformation of services for patients, ensuring collaboration, standardisation and dissemination of best practice. By working as one system, we are delivering more elective care, more efficiently and more consistently for patients and Professor Taylor has been central to this effort.Programme for Government targets haven’t just been met; nearly three times that number of elective activities have already been delivered.  In addition, there have significant improvements across a number of waiting lists and more efficient use of our valuable capacity.Professor Taylor, working alongside Trusts, clinical teams and managers are driving forward system-wide improvement in a challenging environment. Their efforts are to be commended and I am determined to maintain this momentum.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of the names of serving PSNI officers recently being published on the courts' website; (ii) whether her Department will conduct an investigation as to how this occurred; and (iii) whether her Department can offer advice or guidance to the individuals involved.
answer_text: (i)  Court details, other than those relating to family proceedings, are routinely listed publicly online unless an application is made to the court for anonymity. It is the responsibility of the legal representative or parties to proceedings to make this application. A decision to grant anonymity can only be made by a judge.Having been alerted by one of the parties to the cases, as a precautionary measure my Department took immediate action and made the decision to have Public Court Lists Online taken down from approximately 8.30pm on the evening of 3 February 2026 until 6am on 4 February 2026, when refreshed lists were available following the application of an interim Judicial direction, as there has not been an application for anonymity before the court.(ii) As no information was released erroneously and there was no data breach, there is no requirement for my Department to conduct an investigation or review. (iii) Any individuals appearing in court who wish to seek anonymity should discuss the matter with their legal representatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) her assessment of the regular sewage overflows from manholes on properties on the Donaghadee Road, Groomsport; (ii) the reason why overflows continue to occur; (iii) any steps her Department is taking to stop overflows happening; and (iv) whether overflows are linked to equipment failures at the nearby wastewater pumping plant.
answer_text: The management of the public water and wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:NI Water has advised that while there have been issues on the Donaghadee Road, Groomsport historically, primarily at numbers 20 and 22, there are not regular sewage overflows at this location; The most recent occasion when an overflow did occur was related to Storm Chandra, when the nearby pumping station was affected by the heavy rainfall, causing overflows from manholes;NI Water’s Wastewater Network Team will assess this site, to identify any remediation work that could help to reduce the risk of future overflows, however, this investigation needs to take place during dry weather; andThis will be determined once the investigation has been completed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for an update on the outstanding holiday payments to PSNI officers; and (2) whether her Department has established a budget figure, required to make a final settlement.
answer_text: While I cannot give a definitive timeframe at this stage, I want to reassure the Member that my officials are working closely and constructively with the PSNI to progress the Holiday Pay business case as quickly as possible.  The business case must, of course, satisfy the requisite approvals, including approval by the Department of Finance (DoF). My Department has not established a budget figure at this stage.  However, I can advise that Holiday Pay is an exceptional pressure that is wholly unaffordable within existing or future PSNI and DoJ baseline budget. Further discussions will be needed with the Executive and the DoF to consider options for funding this exceptional pressure.Furthermore, Holiday Pay is an issue that has an impact across all departments and, therefore, a solution at Block level will be required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has any responsibility to maintain the fencing that separates the open space at Alandale, Bangor, and Bangor Ring Road.
answer_text: The fence was initially provided to delineate land ownership boundaries and not for safety reasons.As land ownership has now been clearly distinguished at the rear of the footway, the fence is no longer required and when it reaches the end of its useful life or is damaged, my Department would have no plans to repair or replace it.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any strategic measures her Department is taking to mitigate against localised flooding on roadways, during periods of heavy rainfall.
answer_text: When a storm is forecast, preparations begin at the earliest opportunity with multi agency co-ordination taking place ahead of the event, in advance of the storm arriving and prior to the severe weather warnings coming into effect.Alongside our multi agency partners, officials work to respond and minimise disruption. Multi weather events which bring wind and rain can cause widespread fallen trees and flooding issues which are tackled with all available resources.In line with my Department’s policy, drainage gullies are cleaned on an annual basis as resources permit. In addition, areas which are of concern for potential flooding are checked and cleaned as resources and warning periods permit, as part of the ramping up in advance of the storm. In many cases this relates to debris which may cover the gullies and impede drainage in heavy rainfall. This year, I have been able to identify additional funding for the systems of pipeline and manholes which, along with the gullies, make up the drainage network.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the day procedure unit at Lagan Valley Hospital.
answer_text: The Lagan Valley Hospital day procedure centre is one of several Elective Care Centres (ECCs) in the region providing routine surgical procedures for patients across different Health Trust areas.  It provides a range of specialties, including endoscopy, ENT, general surgery, vascular surgery, plastic surgery and urology, with several thousand patients benefitting from procedures each year.  Established in October 2020 as the first regional day procedure centre, it has made an important contribution to improving elective care capacity and efficiency. From April to December 2025, approximately 3,400 patients received treatment at the site.By working as one system, we are delivering more care, more efficiently and more consistently for patients.  ECCs, such as the one at Lagan Valley Hospital,  have been central to this effort

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of reports her Department has received, in the last five years, of pedestrians tripping as a result of footpath damage, caused by street trees in Main Street, Groomsport.
answer_text: The roots of the trees along Main Street, Groomsport started to protrude, damaging the surface around 2023. Options are currently being considered including their removal, however it is hoped that if trees must be removed, a suitable nearby alternative location can be found to facilitate the planting of replacement trees. Alternatively, it may be possible to plant replacement trees at the same locations with species that are more appropriate for the location and less likely to cause future damage.Before any works are taken forward, my Department will engage with Ards and North Down Borough Council and other relevant stakeholders.Since 2023, officials have received a total of 6 complaints in relation to the tree roots and associated hazardous walking conditions at this location.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment on the impact that the Belfast City Hospital Mega Clinic for gallbladder surgery is having on waiting lists for this surgery.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the number of unfilled posts across the health service; (ii) whether the number of unfilled posts are reducing; and (iii) to detail the budgetary impact of filling the posts with permanent staff.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail a timeline for the current phase of work on the Queen's Parade site in Bangor.
answer_text: Construction commenced on Phase 1 of Queen’s Parade in the Summer of 2025 and is currently expected to complete in the Autumn of 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of measures being taken to reduce hospital waiting times.
answer_text: The revised Elective Care Framework (ECF), which was published on 24 May 2025, sets out a five-year roadmap to reduce waiting times, including how the £215 million earmarked funding would be used for waiting list activity. Actions which have been taken to drive down our waiting lists include the expansion of mega clinics, enhancing diagnostic capacity, increasing health and social care training places, expanding pre-operative assessment capacity and introducing a new waiting list reimbursement scheme. The continued use of waiting list initiatives, using both in-house and independent sector capacity, are also supporting the reduction in our waiting lists.As you are aware, only £50m of the earmarked funding represents new money and in line with the flexibility provided for this funding, £73.5m has subsequently been redirected to reduce the wider deficit.However, it should be noted that even within the current constrained financial environment, my Department has made significant progress since March 2025 with the number of patients waiting more than four years for an outpatient assessment reduced by 40%, the number of patients waiting for an inpatient or day case procedure reduced by 52% and the number of named procedures, as detailed in the Elective Care Framework, reduced by 70% between March – December 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the utilisation of operating theatre space across the hospital estate.
answer_text: Trusts are actively working to maximise the use of operating theatre space across the Health and Social Care hospital estate, with regional theatre utilisation up to 86% and rising toward the 90% efficiency target.However, a wide range of operational and clinical factors affect overall utilisation both at Trust and hospital site level, including case mix, workforce availability, emergency pressures and patient cancellations. The number of theatres available to use can also vary at any point in time due to planned refurbishments and / or maintenance being carried out.To support the continued improvements, Trusts are utilising improved scheduling processes, extending operating lists and strengthening pre-operative assessment pathways to reduce late cancellations. Where theatre facilities are not currently fully commissioned, my Department is working with the independent sector to explore the leasing of fallow theatres. This partnership arrangement has worked particularly well in South West Acute Hospital.As outlined within the Elective Care Framework: Implementation and Funding Plan, my Department is actively working to maximise the effective use of existing commissioned theatre capacity, whilst also seeking to commission additional capacity where appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 32796/22-27, whether her Department is considering a permanent solution to the recurring flooding that also renders the single pavement impassable for pedestrians.
answer_text: The recent flooding at Bangor Road, Groomsport has been attributed to heavy and sustained rainfall potentially overwhelming the drainage system.Road drainage systems are only designed to cope with normal average annual daily rainfall. They cannot, nor are they intended to, cope with the sudden, short duration, high intensity rain events that have become more common in recent times. Officials have carried out cleaning and jetting work at this location to ensure the road drainage system is working to full capacity, however, with increased development, more water combined with faster runoff, has been placed into the same system, resulting in overcapacity issues at times of sustained and heavy rainfall.We will, however, continue to monitor and clean the system as resources permit to ensure it is operating to its full design capacity, as well as investigate any measures which can be implemented to reduce the impact of future weather events.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will consider replacing some level of street lighting and maintain existing lighting at Coastguard Land and Orlock Lane, Groomsport.
answer_text: My Department provides and maintains street lighting only on adopted public roads and footways, in accordance with departmental lighting policy.Coastguard Lane is not an adopted road. The two streetlights previously located there were removed during NIE network upgrade works in January 2025 and will not be replaced as the road remains unadopted.Orlock Lane is also not an adopted road.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the legal costs incurred by his Department in relation to the judicial review of the decision not to grant integrated education status to two schools in Bangor.
To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the progress towards introducing a multi-year budget.
answer_text: On 6 January I published proposals for a draft multiyear Budget and formally launched a public consultation.  The consultation will run until 3 March.In parallel to this, I have been continuing to engage with my Executive colleagues.  Last week I undertook a series of Budget bilateral meetings with Ministers. I am committed to working constructively and in partnership in relation to the Draft Budget to find a way forward.The Draft Budget proposals I have brought forward have been developed against the backdrop of an exceptionally constrained financial position. The proposals therefore do not provide the level of funding I would wish to see directed at our public services; instead, they reflect what is possible within the funding available. In this context it is simply not possible to provide any department with the funding it has requested. I have been clear that adequate funding is required from Westminster for investment in public services. I continue to make this case.However, despite the challenges, the Executive must grasp this opportunity of a multi-year budget to provide certainty and aid long term planning to enable strategic investment that delivers lasting benefits for our economy, our environment and our society.It is essential that we now listen to the voices of families, workers, business and communities to ensure that the decisions we make in agreeing a Budget reflect the needs and aspirations of the people we serve.I encourage everyone to have their say during the consultation process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department is currently considering felling any mature trees on Main Street, Groomsport.
answer_text: My Department has inspected nine trees located on the footway along Main Street, Groomsport. These trees have caused significant structural damage to the footway surface, resulting in uneven conditions that present a safety risk to pedestrians. Options are currently being considered including their removal, however it is hoped that if trees must be removed, a suitable nearby alternative location can be found to facilitate the planting of replacement trees. Alternatively, it may be possible to plant replacement trees at the same locations with species that are more appropriate for the location and less likely to cause future damage.Before any works are taken forward, my Department will engage with Ards and North Down Borough Council and other relevant stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has any plans to launch a road safety advertising campaign, particularly to encourage pedestrians and cyclists to wear bright or reflective clothing, when using roadways during the hours of darkness.
answer_text: A new campaign entitled ‘Be Bold Be Seen’, was launched in autumn, aimed at encouraging pedestrians, cyclists, and other vulnerable road users to wear bold, bright or high-visibility clothing during the darker months.  The campaign includes two creative approaches under the theme: one featuring people in bright, fashionable clothing, and another using a high-visibility hedgehog character to reinforce the message in a simple and engaging way. The campaign was delivered across a range of channels, including outdoor poster sites, social media and bus rear advertising. Further information on the campaign can be seen on the Department’s road safety website, Share The Road to Zero  https://www.sharetheroadtozero.com/be-bold-be-seen. A competition giving the public the opportunity to name the hedgehog character was run online on Belfast Live to further promote the campaign and the winning name was Hi-vis Harry. An animated TV campaign to promote the need to be seen during hours of darkness and featuring Hi-vis Harry is currently in production. The campaign is sponsored by the Northern Ireland Road Safety Partnership and will be aired in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 7228/22-27, to detail the number of applications for the Community of Lifesavers Teacher Professional Learning programme, broken down by (i) post-primary school; (ii) special educational school; and (iii) EOTAS, for each academic year since 2022-2023.
answer_text: The table below sets out the breakdown of applications by school and academic year since the inception of the Community of Lifesavers Programme in 2022-23. In respect of 2025-26, the figures reflect the position up to 15 January 2026.Number of applicationsPost Primary schoolsSpecial schoolsEOTAS centres2022-20232173502023-202413329152024-20251562122025-202679140

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 35048/22-27, to detail the criteria that this junction failed to meet, to qualify for the installation of traffic light controls.
answer_text: All requests for improvements to the road network, including the provision of traffic signals, are assessed in line with the Department’s current policies and guidance and there are several factors which are considered such as: current road layout; traffic and pedestrian movements; the history and relevance of injury collisions; and environmental impact.Data in relation to injury collisions is provided by the PSNI and on review of the last three years, one incident occurred at this junction.  When assessed against the criteria Safety, Accessibility & Integration and Economy within RSPPG E032, it did not achieve a sufficiently high score compared to other higher priority locations to be recommended for further investigation and inclusion in a future works programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has any plans to resurface the (i) Beechvale Road, Killinchy; (ii) A23 Moneyreagh Road; (iii) Moss Road, Ballygowan; and (iv) A22 Killinchy Road.
answer_text: Details of the planned programme of works are included in my Department’s Annual Report to Councils.Officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with my Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether she has issued any instructions to her officials regarding interaction with companies that have a trading connection with Israel.
answer_text: No - the position is as set out in my Written Statement of 16 October.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether the Proposed Draft Budget 2026-29/30 will enable the full delivery of the Programme for Government 2024-2027.
To ask the Minister of Finance to outline any processes he will put in place to ensure that the Department of Health remains a priority over a three year multi-year budget.

To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 33972/22-27, to detail whether any school properties in North Down fall within the category of disrepair referenced in the Public Accounts Committee report.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is currently in year three of a five-year cycle of condition surveys of the entire schools’ estate. This cycle of updated surveys will bring base school condition information up to date and permit a planned targeting of investment. We have an estate of over 1,100 schools, and regrettably many of these are aged buildings and are in poor condition. The current estimated maintenance backlog for schools in the North Down constituency is estimated to be circa. £7.5 million.Years of underinvestment coupled with insufficient budget availability has meant the top priority for investment has been keeping schools open and ensuring pupils are educated in a safe learning environment.I remain committed to improving the condition of our school estate and have called for urgent, cross-party support to secure the investment needed to ensure that every child in Northern Ireland is educated in a safe, modern and inclusive environment. The current situation cannot continue.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he will answer AQW 36014/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 36014/22-27 was answered on 22 December 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will consider (i) reducing the speed limit on the A2 Groomsport Bypass to 30mph; and (ii) installing a controlled pedestrian crossing to facilitate safe pedestrian access into Groomsport, for residents on the Springwell Road, Groomsport.
answer_text: The provision of controlled pedestrian crossing facilities is governed by the department’s policy document RsPPG_E005 Controlled Crossings : Assessment Procedures.  When considering the provision of a crossing facility, taking into account the existing speed limit of 50mph, officials have advised that the location does not meet the current requirements.When considering the appropriate speed limit for a road, the Department’s policy is used to ensure speed limits are consistent and motorists are presented with the same speed limit on similar roads. This takes into account factors such as the characteristic of the road, the speed of existing traffic, the degree of frontage development, and road traffic collision data which is continuously monitored using data provided by the PSNI. The speed limit at this location has been reviewed and is considered to be appropriate.I intend to launch a public consultation in the new year on a number of speed related matters. The consultation will seek views on the wider use of permanent 20mph speed limits, the lowering of national speed limit on rural single and dual carriageways, and alignment of HGV speed limits with other regions.  When the consultation is launched, I would encourage as many people as possible to contribute to this significant and important piece of work.Concerns about driver behaviour should be directed to the PSNI on their 101 number.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has committed extra resources for gully cleaning in locations that consistently flood during periods of heavy rainfall.
answer_text: My Department places high importance on the need to adequately maintain the road network through day-to-day essential maintenance activities such as gully emptying, to maintain the structural integrity of the road network.Allocations of funding to maintain our roads have been less than required for many years and as a result, my Department has only been able to deliver a limited service across a range of functions including gully emptying. In line with my Department’s policy, drainage gullies are cleaned on an annual basis as resources permit. However, in recognition of this important area of work I have allocated further funding to deliver additional works in the form of cleaning and maintenance of drainage lines and manholes. When a storm is forecast, preparations begin at the earliest opportunity with multi agency co-ordination taking place ahead of the event, in advance of the storm arriving and prior to the severe weather warnings coming into effect. Areas which are of concern for potential flooding are checked and cleaned as resources and warning periods permit, as part of the ramping up in advance of the storm. In many cases this relates to debris which may cover the gullies and impede drainage in heavy rainfall.Alongside our multi agency partners, officials work to respond and minimise disruption. Multi weather events which bring wind and rain can cause widespread fallen trees and flooding issues which are tackled with all available resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any recent advice his Department has given to local football clubs, who were successful in advancing to the next round of the Northern Ireland Football Fund.
answer_text: Officials within my Department are currently working with colleagues in the Strategic Investment Board, Departmental Solicitors Office and DoF’s Central Procurement Directorate to examine the documents submitted by the applicants as part of the TNIFF ‘Due Diligence’ stage.  As part of this, some of the applicants have been contacted to seek additional information or clarification. 		All applicants were also contacted on 20th November 2025 regarding any ongoing development work that the applicants may be planning to undertake. This was standard business communication related to the delivery of Departmental capital grant programmes.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of any impact the Autumn Budget 2025 will have on the Programme for Government 2024-2027.
answer_text: The position remains challenging both in this financial year and across the multi-year budget period.  As an Executive, we are committed to delivering our Programme for Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any costs incurred by his Department during the various legal hearings that ended in the Supreme Court judgment in the matter of an application by JR87 and another for Judicial Review (Appellant); and (ii) what departmental budget funded this legal challenge.
answer_text: The current costs incurred by the Department as a result of the legal hearings relating to the matter of JR87 and another are estimated to be £240k. My officials anticipate that the applicants’ costs are likely to be in the region of £650k.The final total costs of the full legal proceedings are not yet known.My Department will be obliged to meet the costs relating to the legal challenge from its core departmental budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department (i) had any involvement in the legal journey that ended with the Supreme Court judgment in the matter of an application by JR87 and another for Judicial Review (Appellant); and (ii) provided any level of legal aid in relation to this case.
answer_text: The recent Supreme Court Judgement, In the matter of an application by JR87 and another for Judicial Review (Appellant) [2025] UKSC 40, makes clear that the matter was heard by the High Court and the Court of Appeal in Belfast.By virtue of Section 40 (5B) (a)(i) of the Freedom of Information Act 2000, I am not required to confirm or deny whether legal aid was granted to bring this case, as to do so would contravene the data protection principles set out in Article 5(1) of the General Data Protection.For the sake of clarity if legal aid is granted in a civil case, the legal aid certificate will indicate the steps in the proceedings which are covered and the level of representation which has been granted, whether that be for a solicitor and one or two counsel. The certificate does not specify a financial cost of the legal aid. That will be determined after the conclusion of the case. In certain circumstances, if the case was successful there may not be a cost to the legal aid fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 2606/22-27, whether her Department will consider establishing a memorandum of understanding with local councils, to undertake the cleaning of directional road signs within town centre settings.
answer_text: My Department carries out regular inspections of all public roads and footways to ensure that essential response maintenance is identified and completed as necessary. During these inspections all defects are noted, including signs requiring cleaning to improve their visibility.Defective, damaged or missing signs, as well as those needing to be cleaned, are categorised as urgent or non-urgent work with priority given to mandatory and warning signs. Cleaning of other signs is carried out as permitted by availability of resources.My Department currently has Environmental Contracts in place which includes items for sign cleaning. While current funding levels necessitates that only the highest priority works be carried out, any additional works permitted through an uplifting in funding would be delivered by my Department’s Contractors and therefore a memorandum of understanding with local councils would not be beneficial.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail when his Department will carry out pending health and safety maintenance work at Portaferry Sports Centre.
answer_text: Given competing priorities in the delivery of inescapable maintenance works across the schools’ estate, agreement has been reached that Ards and North Down Borough Council will replace the boiler serving the sports centre at St Columba’s College, Portaferry. It is anticipated that the new boiler will be installed early in 2026. In line with the management agreement between the school trustees and the Council, the costs for the boiler replacement will be apportioned between the school and the Council. Discussions are ongoing between key stakeholders in relation to the works required to the floor of the sports centre.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to outline any review that has taken place on the outcome of the recent evacuation of Parliament Buildings as a result of the building fire alarm being activated.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail whether her Department will take action to resolve recurring flooding on the A2 Belfast to Bangor Road, in the vicinity of Marino.
answer_text: I can confirm that investigations have taken place and a work instruction has been issued to remove tree roots from the drainage system which my officials believe is the cause of the problem.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 33863/22-27, to detail (i) whether legal aid was provided; and (ii) the original request to enable scrutiny of the use of public money, noting that the request did not contain any personal data of any individual.
answer_text: Since I answered AQW 33863/22-27 the judgment in these cases has been published. I can confirm that the judgment states that the two minor applicants brought their respective challenges with the benefit of legal aid. Although the applicants’ names were anonymised, the release of any further information must be considered in light of data protection principles. For that reason, and consistent with data protection obligations, no further information can be provided beyond what is already publicly available. The judgment does, however, make clear that the costs of the applicants’ cases will be taxed by a judicial officer.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the adequacy of the current legislation regarding (i) dog on dog; and (ii) dog on human, attacks.
answer_text: I consider the current legislation to be adequate with the Dogs (Northern Ireland) Order 1983 making it an offence for a dog to attack people or animals. It is also an offence for a dog to behave in a way that a person becomes fearful of being attacked. Councils have significant powers to investigate and prosecute attacks by dogs. Where prosecutions take place, the judiciary can apply penalties that range from a Level 3 fine to two years imprisonment, depending on the circumstances of the attack.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has any plans to introduce traffic light controls at the junction of the A21 Newtownards Road and Enterprise Road Business Park, Bangor.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users.Regrettably, when assessed against these competing priorities, the provision of a signalised junction did not score highly enough to be considered for inclusion in the 2025/26 works programme.All requests for improvements to the road network, including the provision of traffic signals, are assessed in line with the Department’s current policies and guidance and there are several factors which are considered such as: current road layout; traffic and pedestrian movements; the history and relevance of injury collisions; and environmental impact.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of gully cleaning units, operational at any one time, in the Southern Road Division area.
answer_text: My Department places high importance on the need to adequately maintain the road network through day-to-day essential maintenance activities such as gully emptying, to maintain the structural integrity of the road network. However, allocations of funding to maintain our roads have been less than required for many years and as a result, my Department has only been able to deliver a limited service across a range of functions including gully emptying. My Department’s current Limited-Service Policy aims to inspect and clean, where necessary, all gullies once annually. Further ad-hoc cleaning may also be undertaken to address any specific localised issues as required, within available budgets, and in advance of and in response to severe weather events. However, in recognition of the importance of this area of work, I have allocated further funding to deliver additional works in the form of cleaning and maintenance of drainage lines and manholes. My department has a total of 18 vehicles available in Southern Division for the purposes of clearing road gullies.  These include vehicles owned by my department or those available as part of contracted in gully cleaning services.  It should be noted that due to required servicing and maintenance of the vehicles, not all units may be available at any given time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the area of Belfast Lough that is recognised as having unacceptable levels of pollution has moved closer to the open sea during the last two years.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of the recent Public Accounts Committee report which states that many schools are in a state of disrepair and may pose a risk to the health and safety of pupils and staff, to detail the number of schools in North Down which fall in to this category.
answer_text: I welcome the Public Accounts Committee’s report on the schools’ estate, as it brings into sharp focus the critical and long-standing issues of school infrastructure and funding. Severe and unsustainable financial pressures have left my department unable to carry out routine maintenance or invest in long-term improvements across the schools’ estate. The Education Authority (EA) is currently in year three of a five-year cycle of condition surveys of the entire schools’ estate. This cycle of updated surveys will bring base school condition information up to date and permit a planned targeting of investment.  Health and safety works are prioritised within the current budget constraints so if any school identifies an urgent issue, they should either report to the EA’s Maintenance Helpdesk or submit an Unavoidable Minor Works Application to the EA or my department. I remain committed to improving the condition of our school estate and have called for urgent, cross-party support to secure the investment needed to modernise and protect Northern Ireland’s schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has any plans to resurface Church Avenue, Bangor.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of the planned works programmes for the 2025/26 financial year are provided in my Department’s Annual Report to Ards and North Down Borough Council 2025/26 Officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with my Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has any plans to resurface (i) Sunningdale Park, Bangor; and (ii) the adjoining Knockdene thoroughfare.
answer_text: My Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of the planned works programmes for the 2025/26 financial year are provided in my Department’s Annual Report to Ards and North Down Borough Council 2025/26.Officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with my Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the costs incurred by his Department, in relation to the recent High Court hearing for a judicial review of the decision not to grant integrated status to two Bangor schools.
answer_text: I am unable to disclose the precise costs incurred in relation to the recent High Court hearing referred to above. Not all costs incurred in relation to the matter to date have been invoiced yet, and the matter is now subject to appeal.  Costs to date are therefore incomplete and further costs are being incurred on an ongoing basis. While I am unable to quantify at this time in exact monetary terms how much these cases have cost my Department, I can advise that the costs incurred are likely to be significant.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the level of any legal aid granted to those bringing a call for a judicial review on the Minister for Education's decision to reject integrated status to two Bangor schools.
answer_text: I cannot provide the information sought as to do so would be contrary to section 40(2) of the Freedom of Information Act as it is personal data.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the resources her Department allocates to cleaning road traffic signage in town centres.
answer_text: My Department carries out regular inspections of all public roads and footways to ensure that essential response maintenance is identified and completed as necessary. During these inspections all defects are noted, including for example defective signs and signs requiring cleaning to improve their visibility.There is no resource specifically allocated to the cleaning of road traffic signage within my Department’s budgeting process. The budget is allocated as part of a figure for the maintenance of signs including the replacement of damaged and end-of-life signs.Budgets are allocated by Division and then by Section Office based on a range of criteria designed to reflect need and present a fair settlement across all areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on major maintenance and rebuild projects across the education estate in North Down.
answer_text: Major Works Programme In North Down there are currently three schools on the Major Capital Works Programme. Building contractor procurement is underway for a new school for Bangor Central Integrated Primary School –. Construction is underway for a new school for Crawfordsburn Primary School. At Priory College, the new build school project is in the Technical Design and Pre-Tender stage. Special Educational Needs Capital Programme A project to provide additional classroom and ancillary accommodation is in the design stage at Clifton School.  The provision of a dual campus for the school is also under consideration. The former St Annes Primary School, Donaghadee is also being assessed for a duel campus for Killard School. School Enhancement Programme (SEP)At St. Columbanus College a project to provide a new teaching block for science, PE, careers and sixth form is in the design stage. At Sullivan Upper a project to provide additional classrooms and staffroom is also in the design stage. At Glencraig Integrated Primary School a Business Case is in development for this project, to extend and refurb existing classrooms and provide additional resource spaces.A Business Case is in development for Kilcooley Primary School to extend and/or refurbish existing accommodation. Due to the current funding position, Major Works and SEP projects that are not in construction contract are being progressed to the pre-construction phase. They will progress to construction when funding becomes available. Minor Works Programme Minor works projects at schools in North Down to address inescapable statutory requirements and ensure schools remain open and safe are ongoing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department will consider the establishment of a Northern Ireland sporting museum to highlight the achievements of our local sports people, who continue to deliver personal and team sporting success across many disciplines.
answer_text: Responsibility for the establishment of Museums does not rest with my Department. Department of Communities is responsible for the establishment of Museums through its Partner Organisation National Museums NI, as well as Sport.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will consider the establishment of a Northern Ireland sporting museum to highlight the achievements of our local sports people, who continue to deliver personal and team sporting success across many disciplines.
answer_text: I currently have no plans to develop a sports museum, however National Museums NI have a number of sporting exhibits throughout their museums.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline any plans her Department has to prevent the recurring flooding on part of the B511, Bangor Road, Groomsport, during periods of heavy rainfall.
answer_text: The recent flooding at Bangor Road, Groomsport has been attributed to heavy and sustained rainfall potentially overwhelming the drainage system.Road drainage systems are only designed to cope with normal average annual daily rainfall. They cannot, nor are they intended to, cope with the sudden, short duration, high intensity rain events that have become more common in recent times. My officials have carried out cleaning and jetting work to ensure the road drainage system is working to full capacity, however, with increased development, more water has been placed into the same system, resulting in overcapacity issues at times of sustained and heavy rainfall.We will, however, continue to monitor and clean the system to ensure it is operating to its full design capacity, as well as investigate any measures which can be implemented to reduce the impact of future weather events.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the additional gutter clearance her Department has carried out recently, as part of it's winter preparations, to cope with adverse weather conditions, in particular surface water flooding on public roadways.
answer_text: When a storm is forecast, preparations begin at the earliest opportunity with multi agency co-ordination taking place ahead of the event, in advance of the storm arriving and prior to the severe weather warnings coming into effect.Alongside our multi agency partners, officials work to respond and minimise disruption. Multi weather events which bring wind and rain can cause widespread fallen trees and flooding issues which are tackled with all available resources.In line with my Department’s policy, drainage gullies are cleaned on an annual basis as resources permit. In addition, areas which are of concern for potential flooding are checked and cleaned as resources and warning periods permit, as part of the ramping up in advance of the storm. In many cases this relates to debris which may cover the gullies and impede drainage in heavy rainfall.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are technical solutions available to capture the registration numbers of vehicles driving through a red traffic signal.
answer_text: My Department is not in possession of, nor operates, any equipment that can capture the registration numbers of vehicles driving through red traffic signals.There are currently 6 ‘red light running’ cameras across the North which are managed by the Northern Ireland Road Safety Partnership.  Any queries regarding their management and enforcement should be forwarded to the following address:Northern Ireland Road Safety Partnership42 Montgomery RoadBelfastBT6 9LD

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline his strategy to prevent pollution in Belfast Lough reaching the levels being experienced in Lough Neagh.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether his Department has ever considered establishing a central register of wills, lodged with legal, financial and other institutions, to help streamline searches for the existence of a will after a bereavement.
answer_text: My Department has policy responsibility for the substantive law of wills, with operational responsibility falling to other bodies such as the Law Society and the Probate Office of the High Court when probate is applied for. The Law Society has systems in place to record where wills are held. After death, and when probate is granted, the will becomes a public record and is handled by the Probate Office. Searches of wills granted probate can be made through the Public Record Office (PRONI).There are no plans to compel individuals to register their will centrally.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will consider a public inquiry following the publication of the McCullough Review report, as recently called for by an Alliance Party representative on the Northern Ireland Policing Board
answer_text: While the McCullough Review found no evidence of systemic surveillance of journalists, which is to be welcomed, it did not amount to a clean bill of health, with several findings of poor or unlawful practice.  This is not solely a matter of operational policing, for which the Chief Constable is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board, but also impacts on wider issues of confidence. The Policing Board has stated that it views the Report as part of fulfilling its duties under Section 59 of the Police (Northern Ireland) Act 2000.  The Board has made clear it wishes to carefully review the findings of the McCullough Review, and to receive a formal response from the Chief Constable on remedial action and plans for implementing the recommendations, before deciding on next steps.  I believe it is important that the Board is given the time and space to fully consider the report and its preferred way forward. As I have said previously, I share the serious concerns which have been expressed by the Board in relation to this issue and stand ready to support the Board in whatever actions it considers necessary.  In addition, the Investigatory Powers Tribunal has yet to report, after which a further update to McCullough will be made. Ahead of the Tribunal findings and the Board’s considerations, it would be premature to comment on what actions may be required on my part.It is important to note that the only call for a public inquiry to date of which I am aware is specifically in relation to powers which fall outside the devolved space, including oversight mechanisms for use of investigatory powers and the involvement of security services, both of which would necessarily fall to the Secretary of State to address.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will consider introducing new legislation to tackle drivers who deliberately drive at police officers or ram police vehicles, in order to evade police attention.
answer_text: All attacks against PSNI officers are unacceptable.   Any assault on a police officer is an attack on our whole community, putting additional pressure on already stretched officer numbers and resources.  There are a number of offences that can be applied in the case of attacks on police.  However, for offences involving vehicles, there is, since 2004, the offence of aggravated vehicle taking. Where the vehicle unlawfully taken is driven in such a way so that it causes death or serious bodily injury, that offence can only be tried in the Crown Court with a maximum sentence of 14 years’ imprisonment and/or an unlimited fine. Where the unlawfully taken vehicle is driven in a way which causes injury or damage to persons or property the offence can be tried in a Magistrates or Crown Court, and carries a maximum penalty of five years if tried in the Crown Court. Similarly, should the driving be of such a nature that the evidential test is met for a charge of causing death or serious injury by dangerous driving or causing death or serious injury by careless driving while under the influence of alcohol or drugs, these offences also carry a maximum 14 year sentence.  Under my Sentencing Bill, I intend to increase that maximum to 20 years.  If there is evidence to prove that a vehicle was used as a weapon with the intent to kill, or death is caused by unlawful driving and that driving objectively placed the victim at risk of physical harm, the charge could move from one of dangerous driving causing death, to murder or unlawful act manslaughter.  Historically the difficulties in establishing all elements of those offences led to the creation of the alternative statutory road traffic offences (for example, causing death by dangerous driving). Further to this, my proposed Sentencing Bill will legislate for a new offence of assaulting a person who is providing services to the public, performing a public duty or providing a public service.   This would include attacks on police officers and emergency service workers.This new offence will carry a higher maximum penalty on conviction than the sentences currently available for similar less serious assault offences.   Additionally, if a more serious assault offence is charged, I will make the fact that the victim is such a worker a statutory aggravating factor, meaning the courts will be required to reflect this specifically in the sentences imposed.I intend to introduce this Sentencing Bill before the end of 2025, subject to Executive approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the roads maintenance budget for 2025-26.
answer_text: There has been historic underfunding for the maintenance of the road network for many years, and I am keen to improve our infrastructure with the limited funding available to me as it delivers real benefits for our citizens, our communities and the economy. I recognise the need for greater investment in the road network, however my Department faces a wide range of challenges and funding pressures across the many areas and the well documented funding pressures restrict the ability to move to a more sustainable position.The ‘Review of the Structural Maintenance Funding Requirements for the Department for Infrastructure (Roads)’ made five recommendations, the main one relating to the level and timing of funding made available for structural maintenance of the road network. Greater certainty of budgets, that meet the level of funding recommended in the report, would allow my Department to bring forward much more effective and properly resourced maintenance programmes. The report suggests it should be entirely possible to achieve significant efficiencies if adequate long-term budgets were in place. These savings might allow the structural maintenance budget to be reduced longer term or, better still, allow my Department to make a much needed impact on reducing the maintenance backlog, which would, in turn, yield even greater efficiencies.My Department continues to use this evidence to argue for increased funding in line with the recommended level of £143m (at 2018 prices). My Department submits bids in line with the recommendation and has included a pragmatic ramping up approach which reflects the capacity of internal and external resources. My officials continue to work closely with the Department of Finance to secure long-term funding and, in particular, to highlight the need for multi-year budgets. There are many competing financial demands across my Department and it will be challenging to address years of underfunding; however, I am committed to improving our roads here and I continue to pursue additional finding for investment in the road network.I will continue to make the case for investment in infrastructure here, which includes improving the quality of our road network.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the options available to her to find the budget to fund the compensation payments due to officers and staff affected by the PSNI data breach.
answer_text: My Department is currently facing exceptional pressures of £228 million arising from legal claims, including the PSNI data breach. These costs are best estimates at this stage as there will be a range of potential costs depending on the eventual settlement of these legal claims. The estimated cost of £228 million represents approximately 16% of my Department’s current non-ringfenced Resource DEL budget of £1.4 billion for 2025-26. In addition, my Department reported stabilisation pressures of £32 million for 2025-26 in the recent June Monitoring Round.  The PSNI received a 64% share of my Department’s 2025-26 Opening Budget allocation. The remaining budget was allocated in full to fund all the other elements of the justice system, including Prisons, Courts, Legal Aid and Probation. Given the demand-led nature of these front-line services, there is minimal scope, if any, to reduce or divert resources from one critical area to another given that the services delivered are all deemed to be Departmental priorities and interdependent.Given the quantum involved, the costs of settling the PSNI data breach claims are not affordable from within the Department of Justice (DoJ) budget under any circumstance without additional funding from the Executive or HM Treasury. I will continue therefore to engage with the Minister of Finance and Executive colleagues to secure this vital funding in order to resolve this matter.My officials also continue to engage with their Department of Finance (DoF) counterparts and I understand that DoF officials remain in discussions with HM Treasury officials.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the reinstatement of utility trench works on the Ballysallagh Road, Craigantlet, has recently been inspected.
answer_text: I am advised that an inspection of Ballysallagh Road, Bangor was carried during the week commencing 11th August 2025.  The inspection found no actionable defects within the utility track. My officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when the vegetation growth on footpaths and roadsides in the Bangor West area will be addressed.
answer_text: My Department undertakes routine weed control on the adopted road network, including footways, throughout the North.  Weed control is undertaken annually and normally results in weeds initially being treated once per year, with further treatments carried out as required, following an assessment of the effectiveness of the initial action.  I have been advised that, within the Ards and North Down area, the initial weed treatment commenced in early May and was completed in early August. Secondary treatments including remedial action is on-going throughout the area.I would encourage your constituents to report any roadside issues, such as weeds, trees, hedges sightlines etc using the online reporting tool, following the link below:https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/services/roadside-grass-cutting-weeds-and-overgrown-hedges-or-trees

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the installation of a controlled crossing on the East Circular Road, Bangor, in proximity to St Columbanus' College; and (ii) whether a preferred site for the crossing has been finalised by her Department.
answer_text: Road safety continues to be a priority for my Department to ensure that all road users can travel safely and with confidence. I am acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, especially near our schools.		 I can confirm a new controlled pedestrian crossing on the A2 East Circular Road, Bangor, has been programmed for this financial year and will be completed as soon as resources are available.	The crossing will be constructed between the junction of Ballymaconnell Road and Ballymaconnell Road South and will facilitate pedestrians crossing the dual carriageway close to St Columbanus’ College.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail what mitigation steps his Department has taken to date to avoid Belfast Lough having blue-green algae pollution similar to that of Lough Neagh.
answer_text: My Department is obligated under the Urban Waste Water Treatment Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2007 to conduct reviews of sensitive areas to mitigate pollution. The Inner Belfast Lough Shellfish Waters Protected Area has been identified as a sensitive area in both the draft 2019 and 2023 Reviews. Subject to Executive approval, this designation would require improved wastewater treatment to meet regulatory standards.I am acutely aware of the need to progress this important area of work as a matter of urgency and I will provide an update on this matter soon. My Department continues to monitor and confirm reports of blue green algae and there are no confirmed reports in Belfast Lough this year to date.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline the value to the local economy provided by cruise liners visiting Belfast in each of the last three years.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the delivery of pay awards in 2025-26.
answer_text: On the 28 May, in a written statement to the Assembly, I confirmed my decision to issue a Ministerial direction to implement the pay recommendations for 2025/26. I informed my Department’s Permanent Secretary that I wanted the current recommended increases to be approved without delay.
