For too many years we have been expecting staff to wait to receive the pay awards they are entitled to.  At the heart of this decision is my recognition of the immense dedication and professionalism of our HSC workforce.  Staff deserve to be valued and fairly remunerated for their vital work.  Timely pay awards are an important part of that. And I also believe delivering upon the awards are central to the wider Executive promise of pay parity.You will also be aware that my decision to issue a Ministerial Direction to implement the 25/26 Pay Award has been referred to the Executive for consideration and decision.  I have on, 2 occasions, raised the issue at Executive meetings without any progress or decision being taken.  The HSC Pay Award was again discussed at the meeting on 04 September and, while there was another robust discussion, regrettably the Executive is still not in a position that would allow it to take a decision on the issue, and which would then allow the Department to proceed with implementing a 25/26 Pay Award for HSC staff. I will continue to engage with the Executive to try to find a resolution that will allow us to move forward in delivering a pay award that fully recognises the valuable services provided by our HSC workforce.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of why the recommendations of full-time and reserve police officer numbers, contained in the Patten Report, have not been achieved.
answer_text: As a political member of the Policing Board, you will be aware that the recruitment of both full-time and reserve police officers is an operational matter for the Chief Constable. You may therefore wish to contact the Chief Constable directly for further detail.However, it is important to note that in the previous mandate when I was in office, police numbers were increased to 7,100, and it is deeply regrettable that the subsequent suspension of the political institutions contributed to a situation where that fell so significantly. I remain firmly committed to addressing the situation and supporting the PSNI’s Workforce Recovery business case. With the recruitment and training process already underway, it is reassuring that my Department will be given first call to access up to the £7m required in future monitoring rounds in the current financial year to support the first year of the business case. Furthermore, the Finance Minister has given a commitment to pursue full funding for the business case through the multi-year Budget process, commencing after 2025-26.  I have been clear in my discussions with the Finance Minister and Executive colleagues that the stabilisation plan requires the full £200m to be made available, without which the value of year one commitments is extremely limited. It is therefore vital that subsequent years of the business case are supported in the 3-year budget process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) whether the Chief Constable will commence the Recruitment Recovery Plan at risk until the Minister of Finance can confirm an amount of additional budget, up to £7 million, in the October Monitoring Round; and (ii) whether her Department made a formal bid for this additional budget in the June Monitoring Round.
answer_text: As a political member of the Policing Board, you will be aware that recruitment is an operational matter for the Chief Constable. You may wish to seek further information directly from the Chief Constable on this matter.  However, in response to your questions, I can confirm the following:(i) With the recruitment and training process already underway, it is reassuring that my Department will be given first call to access up to the £7m required in future monitoring rounds in the current financial year to support the first year of the Workforce Recovery business case. Furthermore, the Finance Minister has given a commitment to pursue full funding for the business case through the multi-year Budget process, commencing after 2025-26.  	(ii) My Department did make a formal bid for this additional budget in the June Monitoring Round.  The £7 million formal bid was submitted to the Department of Finance to support the Workforce Recovery business case and the growth in police officer and staff numbers. The bid was ranked as my Department’s number one priority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the underpayment of rates for 9-11 Church Street, Ballymena, since 2007; and (ii) his assessment of this situation.
answer_text: My response to AQW 29918/22-27 sets out the Department’s position on rate collection and the ongoing actions taken by Land & Property Services (LPS) to secure payment of rates.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the steps his Department has taken since 2007 to recoup unpaid rates bills for 9-11 Church Street, Ballymena.
answer_text: The Department, through Land & Property Services (LPS), takes substantive recovery action to recover unpaid bills.  These actions include ongoing communications and contact with ratepayers to ensure rates are paid, giving every opportunity for ratepayers to constructively engage on addressing the rates they owe. LPS will progress legal actions for recovery of unpaid rates for persistent non-payment.Everyone with a responsibility to pay rates must do so to help fund the vital public services citizens and businesses rely on.LPS now collects over £1.6billion in rates revenue each year to fund local and regional services here. It remains committed to collecting unpaid rates and continues to take proactive steps to do so. While LPS does aim to work with those struggling to make payment, it will also ensure to progress legal actions for recovery of unpaid rates for persistent non-payment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he received any bids from the Department of Justice in the June Monitoring Round for (i) the £5 million package to address the costs to the PSNI of the recent unrest; and (ii) the £7 million to allow the PSNI Recovery Plan to commence.
answer_text: In the 2025-26 June monitoring round, the Department of Justice bid for £36.7 million of non-Ringfenced Resource. These bids included, £5 million for “PSNI Policing Pressures - Disorder Costs” and £7 million for “PSNI - Work Force Recovery”. The details of all bids submitted were published on the Department’s website on 30 June 2025. Link provided below; https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/publications/minister-finance-oral-statement-2024-25-provisional-outturn-and-2025-26-june-monitoring-round

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of the annual volume of untreated sewage discharging into Belfast Lough from NI Water wastewater treatment works.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for her assessment of branded vehicles being parked for lengthy periods on public highways as a form of advertising; and (ii) whether her Department has considered introducing legislation to stop such practice.
answer_text: Whilst my Department has no plans to introduce legislation in relation to branded vehicles being parked on public highways, my Department continues to take enforcement action against any vehicle parked in contravention of specified decriminalised parking restrictions or prohibitions. Certain offences, such as dangerous parking or parking causing an obstruction, have not been decriminalised and responsibility for the enforcement of these remains with the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for a progress report on a new build for Bangor Central Integrated Primary School.

To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of assaults when assailants kick and stamp on the head of their victims; and (ii) whether she will consider introducing legislation to cover this specific offence.
answer_text: Kicking and stamping on victims’ heads are particularly vicious elements to any assault.  The Northern Ireland Court of Appeal has identified this behaviour as requiring condign punishment and has provided guidance to the effect that the element of deterrence in sentencing for such cases must be large and must override the factors which otherwise tend to keep such sentences down.  In addition, sentencing guidelines for the magistrates’ courts identify this as a factor placing the offence in the category requiring a higher starting point for the sentence determination. The guidelines apply to the offences of common assault; assault on police; assault occasioning actual bodily harm; and wounding or inflicting grievous bodily harm and can be seen on the judiciary-ni website at Sentencing Guidelines - Magistrates' Court - Assault Offences | Judiciary NI.    In light of the wide range of existing assault offences designed to deal with all levels of seriousness, and the courts’ established guidance on dealing with cases where kicking and stamping are features, I have no plans to introduce a further specific offence.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of recent statements by Ards and North Down Borough Council and Bangor Marine in relation to the Queen's Parade redevelopment scheme, for his assessment of when work will commence on the site.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment on whether the methodology deployed by her Department to repair potholes, represents value for money by providing a permanent repair.
answer_text: Article 8 of the Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993 places a duty on my department to maintain all public roads and footways in reasonable condition.  To comply with this obligation, the department has a set of maintenance standards in place, which establish the frequencies for road inspections dependent on traffic volumes, and specify response times for the repair of defects.Where resources and weather conditions permit, it is my Department’s assessment that a single visit to each location and the delivery of a permanent repair will provide the best value for money. An interim repair will only be provided where it is not possible to deliver a permanent repair within the relevant timescale.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail: (i) his Department's current contractually committed role in the Queens Parade, Bangor, Redevelopment Scheme; and (ii) who has legal title to the land involved.
answer_text: My Department, Bangor Marine, and the Ards and North Down Borough Council signed a Development Agreement for the Queen’s Parade Development on 13th May 2019. This agreement forms the contractual basis for the development of the site. My Department, the Ards and North Down Borough Council and the Crown Estate have legal title to the relevant portions of land within the development scheme boundary.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) when the road markings on the A48 Gransha Road South, between the Kerrs Road junction and the approach to Six Road Ends, was last inspected; and  (ii) whether any refurbishment of the road markings is scheduled.
answer_text: The last maintenance inspection on A48 Gransha Road South, between the Kerrs Road junction and the approach to Six Road Ends was November 2024.As a result of the inspection my officials issued a work order to have all road markings along the A48 Gransha Road South, between the Kerrs Road junction and the approach to Six Road Ends refreshed during the summer months when weather conditions are more favourable.As this work is weather dependent I am unable to give an exact date the work will be completed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his officials have found evidence of the presence of noxious weeds, as defined by the Noxious Weeds (Northern Ireland) Order 1977, growing on roadside verges that are under the ownership of the Department for Infrastructure and that are close to agricultural land.
answer_text: The Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA’s) legal obligation in relation to noxious weeds is to manage risks to the productivity of agricultural land. The Department investigates all complaints of weeds, listed under the Noxious Weeds (Northern Ireland) Order 1977, and issues compliance notices as appropriate, where the weeds are deemed to impact agricultural land productivity. The Department will enforce breaches to the conditions of any notice issued under the Order.So far in the 2025 season, DAERA has not received any complaints pertaining to weeds on roadside verges under the ownership of the Department for Infrastructure.Concerns about Noxious Weeds can be reported to the Department via planthealth@daera-ni.gov.uk, and these will be investigated by DAERA Plant Health Inspectors.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) which part of the Programme for Government addresses the ongoing pollution of Belfast Lough; and (ii) what action the Programme for Government proposes in the short term to address the issue of the pollution of Belfast Lough.
answer_text: The Programme for Government (PfG) 2024-2027 provides a clear framework for tackling environmental issues. While Belfast Lough is not specifically named in the PfG, as I advised in my response to AQW 23285/22-27, the water quality challenges it faces must be viewed within a wider context.  The PfG also recognises the importance of investment in public infrastructure, including wastewater infrastructure, which is of particular relevance to Belfast Lough given that wastewater is a significant pressure on water quality within the Belfast Lough catchment.    My Department is committed to addressing water quality issues in the Lough. For example, DAERA has a statutory obligation to carry out reviews of sensitive areas under the Urban Waste Water Treatment Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2007 and Belfast Lough is included in these reviews. As outlined in AQW 23285/22-27 I am progressing plans to identify the Inner Belfast Lough Shellfish Water Protected Area as a sensitive area, requiring additional wastewater treatment. I am acutely aware of the need to progress this important area of work as a matter of urgency and hope to be able to provide an update on this matter in the near future.  I also recently designated Brompton Bay (Bangor) and Donaghadee as identified Bathing Water sites.I am committed to taking relevant appropriate actions within the remit of my Department in terms of monitoring, regulation and enforcement. Wastewater infrastructure and associated investment falls under the remit of the Department for Infrastructure.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail what powers his Department has to prosecute any individual or public body that is seen to be contributing to the pollution of Belfast Lough.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) is committed to tackling water pollution by pursuing offenders in line with the DAERA Enforcement Policy under legislation including the Water (Northern Ireland) Order 1999. 		NIEA regulates discharges to the water environment through Discharge Consents issued under the Water Order (Northern Ireland) Order 1999. Where an operator fails to comply with the standards set out in the Discharge Consent or has caused a pollution incident, then DAERA’s Enforcement Policy is applied.		In addition to formal prosecution, other enforcement actions available to the Department include the issuing of Warning Letters, Notices and Breaches of Cross-Compliance for agricultural incidents. NIEA actively encourages the public to report water pollution issues to it using the NIEA Incident Hotline on 0800 807060.	Under the Marine and Coastal Access Act 2009, my Department is the authority for marine licensing activities and development in the Northern Ireland inshore region. It also has enforcement powers in relation to marine licensing offences including the authority to investigate and prosecute unlicensed activities that may cause harm to the marine environment.	My Department’s Enforcement Liaison Group has engaged with the Fisheries Bill team who are reviewing enforcement across a range of policy proposals to inform the draft Fisheries Bill. This will include the introduction of fixed and variable monetary penalties for non-compliance with certain offences and an increase in some maximum penalties including for water pollution. My Department is engaged with officials from the Department of Justice on this issue and it is hoped that the policy consultation will take place in late June.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the role of their Department in the delivery of the Programme for Government 2024-2027.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail when his Department became aware that two of the contractors involved in the Queens Parade Bangor redevelopment project had announced that they were withdrawing from further involvement in the scheme.
answer_text: I am aware of recent media coverage regarding the Queen’s Parade scheme but am unable to comment on commercially sensitive matters. However, any proposed amendments to the Bangor Marine partnership structure are considered in line with the Queen’s Parade Development Agreement and are assessed appropriately. The few remaining legal matters are being worked through as quickly as possible.  These issues have proven more complex than anticipated. I hope to provide an update on timelines soon.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on his Department's financial position.

To ask the Minister for Communities whether local concerns in relation to changes to the management structure of the delivery company involved in the Queen's Parade project, Bangor, will have any impact on the current commencement date.
answer_text: The Queen’s Parade project has made significant progress recently with some key legal documents having secured Council approval. It would not be appropriate, at this stage, to comment on commercially sensitive details.  However, my officials continue to work closely with the Developer and Ards and North Down Borough Council and are still hopeful of a start date in late April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the threshold to be met before her Department repairs damaged road signage.
answer_text: Road safety is a key priority for my Department, and I am fully committed to reducing the number of accidents on our roads. Road signs serve an important function in conveying information and requirements about the road and there is no doubt that poorly maintained road signs can have a negative impact on road safety. With this in mind, my officials undertake regular maintenance inspections of all roads during which all defects, including defective signs and road markings, are noted. Road sign repairs are prioritised to address those posing the most serious risk to road users such as missing Stop and Give Way signs. Repairs on other damaged regulatory signage necessary for continued road safety are also carried out subject to the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 22229/22-27, to detail (i) when road markings on the B511 Bangor Road, Groomsport, were last inspected; (ii) the findings of the inspection; (iii) whether any action was recommended.
answer_text: My officials carry out routine inspections of the road network during which defects meeting the current limited service intervention levels are recorded for repair.  As part of these inspections, officials also identify safety hazards, such as missing or worn lines, and damaged signage.The last routine cyclical maintenance inspection on B511 Bangor Road, Groomsport was 13 September 2024.As a result of the inspection my officials are planning to have all road markings along the B511 Bangor Road, Groomsport refreshed during the summer months when weather conditions are more favourable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department provides a core grant scheme for the community and voluntary sector.
answer_text: My Department provides core funding via a range of Voluntary and Community Sector grant funding programmes.  These programmes provide a contribution towards eligible costs, which may include salaries and running costs incurred in the delivery of a range of agreed policy outcomes. Core grant funding is provided through:“The People and Place Strategy – A programme for Neighbourhood Renewal”, comprised of three grant funding programmes with a total revenue budget in 2024/2025 of circa £19m.Nine Voluntary and Community infrastructure support programmes with a total budget in 2024/25 of circa £8.6m.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department provides a core grant scheme for the community and voluntary sector.
answer_text: My Department provides grants to a range of community and voluntary organisations to support the aims and objectives of the Transport Programme for People with Disabilities, Rural Transport Fund and Community Places Grant.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department provides a core grant scheme for the community and voluntary sector.
answer_text: The Department of Education does not provide a core grant scheme for the community and voluntary sector.  However, the Department does provide grant funding through various programmes, directly and indirectly to community and voluntary sector organisations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department provides a core grant scheme for the community and voluntary sector.
answer_text: My Department does not provide a specific core grant scheme for the community and voluntary sector, but instead mainly commissions services from the sector. However, my Department did provide core grant funding to the community and voluntary sector in the 2024-25 financial year in a number of areas, including the provision of support services to victims and witnesses of crime, and services for the NI Prison Service such as supporting people affected by crime and imprisonment and supporting employment of care experienced youth mentors.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether their Department provides a core grant scheme for the community and voluntary sector.
answer_text: The Minority Ethnic Development Fund provides core funding support for voluntary and community organisations working with minority ethnic people and groups.The Executive Office also provides core grant schemes for the community and voluntary sector through the Community Relations Council (CRC) and the Victims and Survivors Service (VSS).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he has led any discussions within the Executive regarding the introduction of new revenue raising streams.
To ask the Minister of Education to provide an update on the project to rebuild Bangor Central Integrated Primary School.
answer_text: The Integrated Consultant Team have completed the revised technical design (RIBA Stage 4) to take account of the inclusion of Enhanced Learning Support provision at the school and a revised planning application was submitted on 21 February 2025.It is anticipated that construction will commence in the last quarter of 2025, however this will be dependent on the relevant approvals being obtained.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, due to the increase in trade as reported by the Belfast Harbour authorities, whether her Department has any plans to minimise the logistical challenges that this growth brings.
answer_text: My Department regularly engages with Belfast Harbour and has consulted with them on the proposed York Street Interchange scheme, which will provide good connectivity to the strategic highway network and minimise any logistical challenges.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department provides resources for the urgent removal of immobile vehicles from the M1 and Westlink during peak hours, as a result of a breakdown or road traffic collision, that may be interfering with traffic flow.
answer_text: My Department is not responsible for the removal of immobile vehicles from the motorway network. Broken down or immobile vehicles are removed under arrangements between the PSNI and the vehicle owners. However, my Department employs contractors on the M1 and Westlink who attend incidents and provide temporary traffic management to assist where requested by PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether he has any plans to request that the Executive includes mitigating measures in the draft Programme for Government 2024-2027 to address the crisis in the water quality of Belfast Lough.
answer_text: I share concerns about Belfast Lough’s water quality; however, this issue must be viewed within the wider context of water quality across Northern Ireland.The Programme for Government 2024-2027 includes measures to address these challenges. The Environmental Improvement Plan and Lough Neagh Action Plan, both approved by the Executive, contain commitments to improve waterways.A significant pressure within the Belfast Lough catchment is from wastewater and although wastewater infrastructure falls under the remit of the Department for Infrastructure, my Department is engaging with Northern Ireland Water to enhance regulation, monitoring, and treatment of wastewater. I am also progressing plans to identify the Inner Belfast Lough Shellfish Water Protected Area as a sensitive area, requiring additional wastewater treatment.Additionally, I will soon bring the Third River Basin Management Plan to the Executive. This includes measures to improve water quality, which will include that in Belfast Lough. Addressing this issue depends on proper investment, and I will continue supporting funding bids for necessary wastewater infrastructure improvements.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the total cost to her Department for the removal of fallen trees from public roads and footways in the aftermath of Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: Following high level initial estimates of the costs associated with the impact of Storm Éowyn my Department has allocated an additional £2.8m to local Roads Divisions to cover storm related pressures including the removal of fallen trees from public roads and footways.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the cost of the operation to clear fallen trees on the Springwell Road, Groomsport, following Storm Éowyn; and (ii) whether her Department plans to recover its costs from the owners of the trees.
answer_text: Following a storm event such as Storm Eowyn, my Department’s primary focus is on removing any fallen trees from the public road network as quickly and efficiently as possible to allow the road to be reopened. Outside of a storm event, where my officials identify overgrown, damaged or diseased trees located on land adjoining the road that endanger or obstruct road users, as a first step owners / occupiers are requested to co-operate in the removal or cutting of the trees to address any concerns in relation to public safety.The cost of the operation associated with the clearance of fallen trees on Springwell Road in Groomsport is estimated to be approximately £3k. Although my Department has discretionary powers to recover costs from the owner or occupier of land from which damage is caused, such as a fallen tree, this would require a significant undertaking by my officials and divert limited staff resources from other safety related work.  My Department obtained a funding allocation of £2.8m to undertake recovery and repair work across the North as a result of Storm Éowyn to ensure public roads are open and safe for public use.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department is giving any consideration to introducing the examination of LED vehicle headlights, and the bulbs being used, during the MOT testing process.
answer_text: I can advise that the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) checks the operation and alignment of a vehicle's headlamps, including LED headlamps, during MOT testing, in compliance with the Motor Vehicle Testing Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2003 and the Road Vehicle Lighting Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2000.  These Regulations do not currently provide for a check of the headlamp's intensity (lumens), and there are currently no plans to add this as a testable item.I am though conscious of the current public concern in relation to the effect of glare from the headlights of oncoming vehicles. In October 2024, the Department for Transport (DfT) awarded Transport Research Laboratory (TRL) a research contract to investigate the factors that cause glare from the headlights of oncoming vehicles, and how to address this. TRL are due to report their findings in Spring of 2025. My officials will monitor the progress of this research and will advise of any implications or advice for road users here once the findings have been published.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on recent discussions with the Crown Estate in relation to the Queen's Parade project in Bangor.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 21704/22-27, whether her Department only placed road closed signage at the Springwell Road and A2 junction, Groomsport, on 5 February 2025.
answer_text: I am advised that my officials in my department attended this site on 25th January 2025, placing road closed signs at both junctions.Unfortunately two separate reports on the removal of these signs were received by my department, on the 28th and 31st January 2025 respectively.  On both occasions my officials attended the site the same day and replaced the signage.  No further reports of signage removal were received.A specialist tree surgeon attended the site on 3 February 2025 to begin to the removal of the trees.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department recovers the costs associated with removing fallen trees that are blocking public roadways from the owners of private property from where the trees were growing.
answer_text: Following a storm event such as Storm Eowyn, my Department’s primary focus is on removing any fallen trees from the public road network as quickly and efficiently as possible to allow the road to be reopened. Outside of a storm event, where my officials identify overgrown, damaged or diseased trees located on land adjoining the road that endanger or obstruct road users, as a first step owners / occupiers are requested to co-operate in the removal or cutting of the trees to address any concerns in relation to public safety.If that fails, the Department has powers under Article 50 of the Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993 to serve a notice on the owner or occupier requiring them to remove or cut back the tree or hedge to remove the danger or obstruction.  Where an owner or occupier does not comply with the requirements of the notice, my Department may carry out the necessary works and seek to recover the costs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the fees paid to energy providers to provide power for (a) street lighting; and (b) signage lighting in each of the last five years; and (ii) how the final annual fee is calculated.
answer_text: The cost of energy to supply power for Street Lighting and Signs for the past five financial years from 2019-20 to 2023-24 is set out in the table below:Energy costsCategory2019-202020-212021-222022-232023-24£mStreet Lighting11.368.5517.4519.1711.66Signs0.160.110.150.160.09My Department’s electricity for street lighting and traffic signs is procured through a competitive contract, currently awarded for a two-year period. Final costs are calculated based on actual load and burning hours and are paid on a monthly basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, given the recent decision of NI Water to stop remedial work on a number of wastewater treatment works that use Belfast Lough for waste disposal, for his assessment of the impact on the water quality of the lough.
answer_text: Wastewater is a significant pressure on water quality within the Belfast Lough catchment.  I am concerned that any reduction in development or upgrade of wastewater infrastructure will have detrimental impacts on water quality.		Belfast Lough is already failing to meet ‘Good’ status and a Shellfish Water Protected Area in the inner Lough is failing to meet water quality objectives with evidence of a deteriorating situation at some shellfish sites.  		There are four identified bathing waters in this water body Helen’s Bay, Crawfordsburn, Ballyholme and Groomsport, with an additional candidate site at Brompton.  Although all of these sites are currently passing the standards, there is no room for complacency. 		The programme of measures within the River Basin Management Plan to improve water quality in Belfast Lough is reliant on appropriate investment in wastewater infrastructure.  I will shortly be bringing the Third River Basin Management Plan to the Executive with my proposals to improve water quality.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when the public car park at Cairn Wood, Craigantlet, will reopen.
answer_text: My Department’s Forest Service has a partnership arrangement in place with Ards and North Down Brough Council, whereby Councils has responsibility for the management and provision of recreational facilities, including the public car park, in Cairn Wood, Ballysallagh forest.Storm Éowyn caused significant damage to the recreational facilities within this forest and to date, there has been no public notification in relation to the re-opening of the carpark and associated recreational facilities by the council.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the criteria used to decide when central road markings on rural roads are replaced; and (ii) how often such road markings are inspected.
answer_text: Road safety is a key priority for my Department and we are fully committed to reducing the number of accidents on our roads. Road markings serve a very important function in conveying information and requirements about the road and there is no doubt that poorly maintained road markings can have a negative impact on road safety. With this in mind, my officials undertake regular routine maintenance inspections of all roads during which all defects, including defective signs and road markings, are noted. The frequency of these inspections depends on the type of road and the volume of vehicular and pedestrian traffic. Town centres and major traffic routes are generally inspected monthly, while all other roads and footways are inspected at either three or six-monthly intervals.Road markings are not replaced within a routine/specific time frame, rather when approximately 30-40% of the marked area is ineffective or worn away. Where the condition of road markings falls below the required standards, they are renewed as soon as possible within the available funding limits. Repairs are prioritised to concentrate on regulatory road markings and those longitudinal lines necessary for continued road safety. Additional road markings are considered for renewal subject to the availability of funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline any plans he has to introduce a two-year budget cycle in 2026.
To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on (i) the commencement of work on the Regeneration of Queen's Parade, Bangor; and (ii) the current position of discussions with the Crown Estate.
answer_text: My officials, along with Ards and North Down Borough Council, are continuing to work closely with the developer, Bangor Marine, in an effort to progress this scheme. The Developer, Council and the Department are currently working to get the final remaining legal agreements and approvals in place to enable a start on site. These issues have proven more complex than anticipated and discussions between the Developer, the Council and Crown Estates are ongoing. I will provide a further update in the next few weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the schedule of roadside gully drainage cleaning carried out on the A2 Road between Knocknagoney and Bangor during the last 12 months.
answer_text: My Department’s current Limited Service Policy for road maintenance requires one gully clean to be undertaken each year and the local Ards and North Down road maintenance team is currently on target to meet this target.  Further ad-hoc cleaning may also be undertaken to address any specific localised issues as required, within available budgets.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) why the fallen tree blocking the Springwell Road, Groomsport, has not been removed; (ii) why no advance warning signage has been put in place since the tree came down; and (iii) whether his Department has responsibility to remove the tree.
answer_text: My Department continues to address the impact of Storm Éowyn across the road network in a significant response and recovery effort over a wide geographical area involving multiple agencies.Several privately owned trees on the Springwell Road fell onto electricity wires during Storm Éowyn resulting in the need to erect temporary road closure signage on 28 January 2025. The trees were entangled with live electrical wires which needed to be isolated by NIE and it was confirmed to my officials that the trees could be safely removed on 30th January 2025.  A specialist tree surgeon contractor was required to remove these trees, and this commenced on 3 February.  I can also advise that the trees have been cleared and the road is now open to traffic.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any changes made to the eligibility criteria for funding from the Northern Ireland Football Fund compared to the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme.
answer_text: In May 2024 I approved a Strategic Outline Case for The Northern Ireland Football Fund (formerly titled the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme). I approved the Business Case for the Performance Programme in December 2024 and have just recently opened a call for applications to the Fund from NIFL Performance clubs. Eligibility criteria for the Performance Programme can be found in the application form. This is the first time the Programme has opened for applications. Therefore, there were no previous application criteria. I am moving at pace with proposals to invest at grassroots level and in creation of a National Football Centre.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact of ceasing agency recruitment for social workers.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the final cost of the Groomsport roundabout resurfacing project.
answer_text: The final measurement for the works at Groomsport Road Roundabout was £414,000.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has any plans to review the positioning of a central island on Bloomfield Road, Bangor.
answer_text: I can advise that Bloomfield Road in Bangor currently benefits from several uncontrolled crossing points at the following locations along the road:i.	Gransha Road/ Castle Street junctionii.	Near Bloomfield Placeiii.	Adjacent to the access to playing fields near Sunningdale Parkiv.	At Bloomfield Road RoundaboutMy Department has also recently installed a controlled crossing on the Bloomfield Road close to Jordan Avenue which replaced an existing uncontrolled crossing point.  As such, my Department has no further plans to review the positioning of these uncontrolled crossings on Bloomfield Road, however I have asked officials to continue to monitor for any safety issues that may warrant consideration of engineering improvements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the recent pollution incident at Ballyholme Beach, Bangor.
answer_text: On Thursday 9 January 2025, NIEA received and investigated a report of pollution in the Cotton (Ballyholme) River, Bangor. Investigation identified an agricultural source and evidence was gathered on site by NIEA. Immediate action was required of the site operator to stop further discharge. Further investigation on the morning of 10 January 2025 confirmed that the discharge had stopped. A major fishkill was confirmed. However, as this is a live investigation, no further details can be provided at this time. The pollution incident was confirmed as High Severity due to the significant environmental impact on the waterway and on Ballyholme Bay. The Local Council was notified and an “advice against bathing” notice issued. DAERA’s bathing water team collected water quality samples at a number of locations on Friday 10 January 2025 to monitor any impact.  The results showed very low bacterial counts indicating that the pollution in Ballyholme Bay had dispersed by the morning of 10 January.  The counts in the Cotton (Ballyholme) River remained elevated at the time of sampling.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of applications for XL Bully Exemption Certificates have been received (i) in total; and (ii) in North Down.
answer_text: As of 17 December 2024, information provided by local councils indicates that there have been 255 applications for XL Bully Exemption Certificates.Ards and North Down Borough Council has advised me that 19 applications have been made in their area.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of applicants, after interview, that met the criteria for consideration of Ministerial appointment as chairperson of the Education Authority.
answer_text: The assessment panel recommended four candidates as suitable for appointment for the position of chairperson of the Education Authority.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has any plans to consider late applications for XL Bully Exemption Certificates.
answer_text: The deadline for Exemption Certificate applications was set in legislation and has now passed. The owners of XL Bully dogs in Northern Ireland have had the longest period available, of any UK jurisdiction, to obtain an Exemption Certificate. It would not be in the interests of public safety to extend this deadline nor is it possible for councils to accept late applications.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of any negative impact on road safety caused by the glare of new generation headlights on drivers of oncoming vehicles.
answer_text: The responsibility for certifying a vehicle for use on our roads lies with the Vehicle Certification Agency (VCA), which is an Executive Agency of the Department for Transport (DfT). VCA work to the regulations that are ultimately agreed to by DfT. You may wish to note that as part of the annual roadworthiness test, the Driver Vehicle Agency checks the headlight alignment on vehicles, however, light intensity or glare are not currently testable items. DfT officials have advised that all vehicle headlamps are designed and tested to comply with internationally recognised standards. This is to ensure that they balance the need to be sufficiently bright to illuminate the road ahead against the risk of affecting the vision of other road users. The standards define the beam pattern and include maximum and minimum light intensities. In light of concerns raised by the public, DfT officials raised the issue at the United Nations international expert group on vehicle lighting. Proposals to amend headlamp aiming criteria were subsequently agreed in April 2023, together with requirements for mandatory automatic headlamp levelling. The implementation of the changes agreed will take some time as vehicle manufacturers have been allowed the time needed to redesign their products and adapt the manufacturing process. DfT expect that the tighter tolerances will come into effect in September 2027. Once implemented, these tougher requirements for new vehicle types will in due course help alleviate the number of cases where road users are dazzled.In addition, DfT have recently awarded Transport Research Laboratory (TRL) a research contract to investigate the factors that cause glare from the headlights of oncoming vehicles, and how to address this. TRL are due to report their findings in Spring of 2025. My officials will monitor the progress of this research and will advise of any implications or advice for road users here once the findings have been published.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) to detail the reasons why her Department has rejected the application by the K9 Search and Rescue charity to be included on the NI Search and Rescue Register; and (ii) for her assessment in relation to the capacity of canine search and rescue currently on the register.
answer_text: The decision to decline the application from K9 Search and Rescue Northern Ireland for membership in the Northern Ireland Search and Rescue Practitioners’ Group was based on an assessment against the agreed criteria set out in the application process.  Based on this process and an assessment of current service provisions, it was determined that there are already established canine search and rescue services in place, and no service gap in this area was identified that would necessitate additional resources.  I am satisfied that the assessment was made following the correct and established process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of how (i) the current planning permission backlog; and (ii) wastewater and sewerage restrictions on new builds will impact on plans for the future provision of social housing.
answer_text: The Housing Executive monitors the progress of all social housing planning applications which have yet to be approved by council planning offices and regularly engages with both housing associations and council planning representatives.		Whilst the current wastewater and sewerage restrictions do not necessarily preclude new social housing developments from being approved and developed, it will mean that the majority of new developments require a site-specific NI Water engineering solution in order to progress. 	This process inevitably adds significant additional time (and cost) to the Planning Approvals process for new social housing developments, which creates an on-going challenge to the delivery of all new social housing annual targets, placing additional pressure on the housing system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any meetings he has held with representatives of small businesses to discuss supply issues due to the Windsor Framework, including any assurances he was able to offer them.
answer_text: My officials and I engage regularly with representatives from small businesses, and representatives from the Federation of Small Businesses in the course of the Department’s work.  This includes engagement via the Dual Market Access Stakeholder Forum, that is co-chaired by InvestNI.  Members of the forum can request guidance and support on Windsor Framework related issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether a flat valve outlet in the Donaghadee area that flows directly into the sea during periods of heavy rainfall has been flowing continuously into the sea due to a valve failure.
answer_text: It has not been possible to identify the exact location for the flap value which is referred to in your correspondence. However, flap valves normally associated with my Departments Rivers Directorate infrastructure would tend to run continuously as they are generally located at the end of watercourses which have been culverted and, by their nature, run to varying extents all the time. Flap valves associated with road drainage run intermittently depending on the prevailing weather conditions. NI Water is aware of reports of an “open” flap valve on the slipway in Donaghadee foreshore. Inspections confirm this is not an NI Water asset. The suspected outfall has been identified as surface water, with no sign of sewage coming from this outfall or in the surrounding area. Inspections were also made of the NI Water sewer network in vicinity of Millisle Public toilets, with no issues of concern detected.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of whether the outworking of the Pet Travel Scheme has been adequately advised to travellers intending to travel to Northern Ireland accompanied by their pets.
answer_text: Following the coming into force on 12 April 2024 of the Windsor Framework (Implementation) Regulations 2024, the movement of pets between Great Britain and Northern Ireland, including the outworkings of the Pet Travel Scheme, is now subject to the direction and control of the Defra Secretary of State.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 17121/22-27, (i) whether his Department corresponded with the Northern Ireland Road Safety Partnership in relation to the information requested in the original question; and (ii) whether his Department is in receipt of the information contained in a reply from the partnership.
answer_text: I can advise that the cameras referred to in AQW 17121/22-27 are not in the ownership or operation of my Department. Rather the policy, management of and statistics associated with these cameras are the responsibility of the Northern Ireland Road Safety Partnership (NIRSP).My officials have liaised with NIRSP and requested that Mr. Chambers be furnished with the information requested in AQW 17121/22-27.  NIRSP have confirmed that this has been carried out.Should you have any further queries of this nature, please refer them directly to NIRSP for response.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 17927/22-27, whether his Department has the means to establish whether trade between Northern Ireland and the United Kingdom has been reduced under the auspices of the Windsor Framework.
answer_text: No. Monitoring of trade in goods under the Windsor Framework is the responsibility of the British government.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether the unadopted status of The Point, Groomsport, is taken into account when establishing the rateable value of residential properties along this roadway.
answer_text: The Capital Value of residential properties along this roadway has been assessed by Land & Property Services in accordance with the statutory provisions of the Rates (NI) Order 1977. The Capital Value is an estimate of the price a property would have sold for at the valuation date 1 January 2005 based on the statutory assumptions reflecting its locality and compared to properties which are broadly similar in terms of location, age, size and construction.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to confirm whether the Sydenham bypass resurfacing project is currently on schedule.
answer_text: I can confirm that the project is progressing well, and that it is on track for completion as planned. Weather conditions have been favourable, and no significant delays have been experienced to date. Officials continue, however, to monitor the situation closely to ensure that any unforeseen issues are addressed promptly.My Department understands the importance of minimising disruption to road users and local communities and is committed to completing these works as quickly and efficiently as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 17230/22-27, whether his Department has been able to identify whether trade between Northern Ireland and the rest of the United Kingdom has been reduced due to the Windsor Framework.
answer_text: No.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans he has to reinstate the reflective central road markings on the Donaghadee Road and Gransha Road South between Newtownards and Six Road Ends.
answer_text: Following routine road inspections by my Department, a number of areas in the Ards and North Down area including Donaghadee Road have been identified for renewal of road markings. Subject to favourable weather conditions and available resources, works are planned to be carried before the end of the financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department has any plans to engage with colleagues in the UK and the Republic of Ireland in relation to the change in the United States of America administration having an impact on the UK and Republic of Ireland economies.
answer_text: I have regular conversations with my counterparts in Ireland and Britain where we discuss all matters related to supporting our economies to grow.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 16729/22-27 and AQW 16728/22-27, to detail what role she and her Department have in providing welfare support for PSNI officers and civilian staff.
answer_text: I refer the member to my response to AQW 16730/22-27 on the 23 October 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of staff employed to conduct the business of the Bangor Housing Executive Office in each year since 2017.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The table below sets out the number of staff (headcount), and full-time equivalent number of staff, located in the Bangor Housing Executive Office on 31 March of each year for the period 2017 – 2024:YearStaffing ComplementHeadcount (March)Staffing ComplementFull Time Equivalent (March)20172319.920182421.120192319.720202420.720211916.420222017.420231916.220241815.0The main reason for head count having reduced from 2021 onwards is due to the Housing Solutions Team moving from Bangor to Strangford House, Newtownards at that time.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any discussions he has had with the Minister for Infrastructure in relation to pollution in Belfast Lough; and (ii) any actions that have been agreed to address the issue.
answer_text: I met Minister O’Dowd on 9 July 2024 to discuss the impact of wastewater infrastructure across Northern Ireland and the impact of sewage pollution upon our rivers, lakes and loughs.  I have requested officials review the Statement of Regulatory Principles and Intent (SORPI). In addition to this I continue to support Minister O’Dowd in bids for funding for wastewater infrastructure and we will be meeting in the near future to continue our discussions on wastewater.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of any barriers that international investors face when seeking to invest in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Despite intense competition for investment from nations, regions and cities across Europe, the North has an enviable reputation in international markets.  I have witnessed this first-hand during investment meetings with existing and potential international businesses both locally and during my recent overseas engagements, alongside Invest NI, in Chicago, Toronto and Berlin. Dual Market Access for trade and investment, and the infrastructure investments through City & Growth Deals also provide an opportunity to further enhance our reputation as an investment location.I am building on the North’s reputation through the recent publication of my Regional Strategy; to increase good, productive, low carbon investment across all regions of the North. With each region developing bespoke investment propositions to increase our international attractiveness whilst capitalising on regional strengths and capabilities.This will allow us to focus on competing for investments in our sectors of strength.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of how the current size of the workforce of the Housing Executive meets its needs.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive currently employs around 3500 people. Our workforce planning process helps ensure that we maintain the required workforce to meet business need. Our workforce plan is normally reviewed twice a year and sets out planned resourcing activity both annually and over a 5-year period.We actively seek to fill vacancies on a permanent basis where possible and over the last 3 financial years we have appointed 726 employees. In addition to the workforce planning process, we also regularly review Directorate structures to ensure that they are appropriately resourced to meet the needs of our customers. Whilst in general terms, our assessment is that we are currently resourced to meet business need we do, like all organisations, experience resourcing pressures from time to time and we take appropriate action to address these, including putting temporary arrangements in place where necessary.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the reasons why the criteria has not been met to enable his Department to impose a 30mph speed limit on Orlock Road, Groomsport.
answer_text: My answer to AQW 6529/22-27 advised that, when considering the appropriate speed limit for a road, my Department’s policy “Setting Local Speed Limits in Northern Ireland” is used to ensure speed limits are consistent and motorists are presented with the same speed limit on similar roads, taking into account factors such as: the characteristic of the road; the speed of existing traffic; the degree of frontage development; and road traffic collision data, which is continuously monitored using data provided by the PSNI.Orlock Road is an unclassified single carriageway road, approximately 730m in length, rural in nature, with limited frontage development and no current plans for further development.  It has low traffic flows and a good road safety record with no history of personal injury collisions recorded within the most recent 3-year period (March 2021 – March 2024).  Therefore, the current National Speed Limit (60mph) is consistent with my department’s policy and remains appropriate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of any impact the Windsor Framework has had on trade between Northern Ireland and the rest of the United Kingdom.
answer_text: It will take time to fully understand the impact of the Windsor Framework on trade between the North of Ireland and Britain.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has any further plans for tree felling to combat ash dieback in North Down.
answer_text: Ash dieback is now established in the wider environment in Northern Ireland as in other European countries. This led to an EU legislative amendment to the Plant Health Regulations in 2019, which no longer considered ash dieback as a quarantine pest. On that basis, affected trees are not required to be removed as a disease control measure. However, it remains important for landowners to exercise their public safety responsibilities regarding the management of affected trees and woodlands. Tree safety inspections are planned to take place in Forest Service forests across the north Down area over the forthcoming weeks. The forests due for inspection include Ballysallagh, Clandeboye, Portavoe and Mount Stewart, with inspections due to be completed before the end of 2024.  The inspections will inform the nature and quantity of interventions required to manage the risks of damage to the public or property, including any necessary tree felling.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether his Department, when planning major roadworks that require lane closures, adjust the timing of traffic lights to assist a more controlled flow of traffic; and (ii) whether more importance is placed on enforcing yellow box regulations to ensure traffic flow during such works.
answer_text: I understand the frustration that essential infrastructure projects can cause, particularly in terms of traffic disruption and every effort is made to coordinate and plan road works to minimise, as far as is possible, their impact on road users. Improvements to our road network are critical to maintaining its safety and supporting future growth. The long-term benefits of these projects will include improved traffic flow, reduced road maintenance, and safer journeys for all road users. Traffic signal timings are reviewed and amended where necessary to cater for changing traffic patterns as efficiently as possible, within the constraints of a busy urban setting.The enforcement of yellow box junctions rests with the PSNI and they should be contacted directly using the 101 number to report any infractions of this nature.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) how contractors are compensated for having to cease work on projects during the pre-Christmas pause period; and (ii) the impact the pausing of work will have on the overall cost of the project.
answer_text: I can advise that no compensation is paid to contractors during the Christmas Embargo period. The Department has no information on any likely effect on the cost of projects, however the embargo only effects planned works and my officials liaise extensively with contractors and utilities to limit the effect of the embargo.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has any plans to increase the number and location of cameras to detect drivers going through red traffic lights; (ii) to detail the number of these cameras that are currently operating; and (iii) to detail the number of detections (a) made; and (b) prosecuted, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The policy regarding the management of these cameras and the statistics associated with the number of detections is the responsibility of the Northern Ireland Road Safety Partnership, and I suggest that you contact the Partnership directly.Their contact details are:Northern Ireland Road Safety Partnership42 Montgomery RoadBelfastBT6 9LDPhone: 028 9092 2751Email: zNIRSP@psni.police.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his officials consulted businesses and retail leaders when deciding on the date for the pre-Christmas pause on all planned roadworks; and (ii) whether he is giving any consideration to moving the pause date forward.
answer_text: As in previous years there will be a voluntary embargo on all non-essential work on key routes into Belfast between 25 November 2024 and 2 January 2025.As the embargo is a well-established approach, having been in operating for over 20 years, and is in place to benefit businesses and retail, there is no need to consult on it.There are no plans to extend this period as an unplanned change would cause contractual issues which could result in very significant additional costs.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister whether the Irish Football Association has made any approach requesting them to make representations to the UK Government on the matter of visas being withheld from the Belarus National Football Team, to allow them to fulfill an upcoming game against Northern Ireland in Belfast.
answer_text: We understand the UK Government has agreed to issue visas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the current position in relation to his Department's purchase of the site of the former leisure centre in Newtownards.
answer_text: My Department is awaiting confirmation of Ards and North Down Borough Council’s former title to the subject vested land to enable the final compensation settlement to be assessed and paid. It should also be noted that the compensation package for this land will also take into account the reconveyance of a significant part of the site back to the Council to facilitate their plans for a new play park.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department has any plans to address the sale of counterfeit car parts.
answer_text: My Department’s Trading Standards Service (TSS) has responsibility for enforcing the criminal provisions of the Trade Marks Act 1994 (TMA 94) and the Copyright, Designs and Patents Act 1988 (CD&PA 88). The TSS is aware of the Intellectual Property Office’s recently launched awareness campaign warning of the dangers of buying counterfeit vehicle parts. The TSS has seized counterfeit brakes, tyres, alloy wheels and other car parts from local retailers and will continue to act when it identifies fake goods being sold on the high street or online. The Trading Standards Service will investigate any alleged breaches of the TMA94 and CD&PA88 that come to its attention and take the appropriate enforcement action, where necessary. If consumers or businesses have a complaint they should contact Consumerline,   the Trading Standards Service’s consumer advice helpline, on 0300 123 6262 or log on to www.consumerline.org.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has made any representations to the UK Government to facilitate the issue of visas to the players of the Belarus National Football Team and officials to allow them to fulfill their upcoming fixture against Northern Ireland at Windsor Park.
answer_text: Officials from my Department have been engaging on this matter with the Irish Football Association and colleagues in The Executive Office; Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office; Department for Digital, Culture, Media & Sport; Home Office and Northern Ireland Office.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the impact that existing levels of pollution in Belfast Lough is having on marine life.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any assistance her Department has provided to the PSNI to help with the welfare of (i) officers; and (ii) civilian staff.
answer_text: The Department of Justice provides funding to the Police Rehabilitation and Retraining Trust (PRRT) and the Northern Ireland Police Fund (NIPF). These organisations provide financial, psychological and physical support to serving and retired police officers. The services provided do not extend to civilian staff.The budget for PRRT in the 2024/25 financial year is £1,784k.  The budget for NIPF in the 2024/25 financial year is £1,499k.The Department has also provided grant payments to Police Treatment Centres (PTC), a charitable organisation, which provides treatment and rehabilitation for serving and retired police officers. Matters directly relating to the welfare of PSNI officers are the responsibility of the Chief Constable. PSNI civilian staff are employees of the Northern Ireland Policing Board with HR responsibilities deferred to the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether a waiting list exists for welfare support for PSNI (i) officers; and (ii) civilian staff.
answer_text: Matters relating to the welfare support of PSNI officers are the responsibility of the Chief Constable.  PSNI civilian staff are employees of the Northern Ireland Policing Board with HR responsibilities including welfare support are conferred on the Chief Constable. It is therefore suggested that you may wish to direct you question to the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the welfare support available to PSNI (i) officers; and (ii) civilian staff.
answer_text: Matters relating to the welfare support of PSNI officers are the responsibility of the Chief Constable.  PSNI civilian staff are employees of the Northern Ireland Policing Board with HR responsibilities including welfare support are conferred on the Chief Constable. It is therefore suggested that you may wish to direct your question to the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any plans her Department has for the site of Rathgael House, Bangor, after its demolition.
answer_text: As part of our ambitious Office Estate Strategy, my Department has progressed plans to demolish Rathgael Tower. We have also invested in improvements to the other accommodation on the site to deliver our needs for the area. Following the demolition, works will progress on a new entranceway to the site.The Department is also considering longer term accommodation options for the area and is in discussion with relevant stakeholders to help inform any future decisions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the estimated cost for the demolition of Rathgael House, Bangor.
answer_text: The estimated cost for the demolition of the Rathgael House Tower and Balloo Annex buildings is £750,000.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans his Department has for the former premises of Cotton Primary School.
answer_text: The former Cotton Primary School is being retained by the Education Authority for potential alternative educational use for Special Educational Needs provision.  The review on its potential usage is currently ongoing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) whether he had prior notice of Ulster University's intention to create a new Strategic Policy Unit; and (ii) for his assessment of appointments being made to this unit that has not been open to a fully transparent public employment process.
answer_text: As an autonomous institution, Ulster University is responsible for its own policies and procedures, including recruitment and retention.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether his Department has responsibility for the trees growing on land between Carolhill Drive and East Circular Road, Bangor; and (ii) when these trees were last inspected for any required remedial work.
answer_text: My Department carries out regular routine maintenance inspections on all roads. As part of these inspections, officials will note trees and hedges within or immediately adjacent to the road boundaries, which are causing an obstruction, obscuring road signs, or have the potential to create danger for the road user. My Department has responsibility for the trees growing on land between Carolhill Drive and East Circular Road, Bangor. The last inspection of Carolhill Drive was completed on 23rd August 2024 and the last inspection of East Circular Rd was completed on 18th September 2024. During these routine inspections a visual inspection of the trees, for any obvious safety issues, took place and did not identify any issues. There has been no specialist survey of these trees.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline what consideration she is giving to revenue raising measures.
answer_text: The Budget Sustainability Plan outlines the additional income that will be generated in 2024-25 and sets out possible income generation for 2025-26. Final decisions on income generation for 2025-26 are best taken in the context of the budget setting process, when the overall budget envelope available to the Executive is known.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice who compiled the (i) scope; and (ii) wording of the Terms of Reference for the current review into the Northern Ireland Policing Board.
answer_text: The scope and wording of the Terms of Reference for the current review into the Northern Ireland Policing Board were initially developed by the Policing Board. They were reviewed and revised by senior Departmental officials following engagement with the Review Panel, prior to my approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the assistance his Department has provided the agri-sector in relation to the challenges of internal regulatory divergence.
answer_text: My Department has not, to date, received requests for assistance from the agri-sector in relation to regulatory divergence, for the areas under my direction and control.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the rebuild of Bangor Central Integrated Primary School.
answer_text: I announced Bangor Central Integrated Primary School to proceed to construction on 28 May 2024. However following a review of the proposed project a reduction in the total enrolment numbers at the school in recent years has been identified.My officials are currently engaging with school representatives and the school’s Managing Authority regarding the implications of this reduction in enrolment and to determine the size of the new school building that will ultimately be delivered.  My Department remains committed to the delivery of a new school building for Bangor Central Integrated Primary School.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of how the NIEA processes anonymous complaints of pollution to identify complaints that are vexatious or malicious.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) relies on the receipt of information from members of the public who can report incidents to the NIEA Incident Hotline on 0800 807060 and select a range of options to help direct their call to the correct team. Reports can also be sent  by email.  NIEA also receives information from other Units within DAERA or through other law enforcement and government agencies.  People making reports may not be willing to disclose their contact details for various reasons, however where an anonymous information about an environmental impact is reported, NIEA will investigate.   Whilst there is no specific policy or test in place to determine the motive of the those submitting reports to the NIEA incident hotline, all information received is assessed by the relevant Unit before it is actioned.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has any plans to resurface Silverbirch Road, Bangor.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition. I am advised by my officials that Silverbirch Road, Bangor is considered to be in reasonable condition and the road is not included in any resurfacing works programme. While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need of intervention. Details of the planned list of resurfacing schemes for the 2024/25 financial year in the Ards and North Down council area will be included in the Department’s Annual Report to Council on 8 October 2024. Following this presentation, the Report will be published on my Department’s website. It will also be issued to MLAs and MPs in each respective Council area and placed in the Assembly library.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of his recent trade mission to the United States of America.
answer_text: I travelled to the United States and Canada with twelve of the North’s most innovative companies from right across the province. These companies offer solutions for different sectors including financial services, insurance, education, green energy, and manufacturing.The US and Canada are two of the North’s most important markets. In the 12 months leading up to March 2024, exports to these markets reached £2.3 billion, highlighting the wealth of opportunities available for local companies.Over the course of the week, company representatives participated in numerous business networking engagements and one-to-one meetings with potential clients, business partners, stakeholders and distributors.  Feedback from the trade delegates was universally positive, with many of the companies reporting solid new business opportunities, including invitations to submit proposals, secure potential distribution partners and win new export sales.  The delegates represented the best of the North’s innovation and formed strong connections, working together to benefit each other and the North of Ireland economy.  Whilst some of the outcomes from this trade mission will take time to come to fruition, Invest Northern Ireland is continuing to work closely with the participating companies. They will monitor progress and outcomes in the comping months and continue to support them in these key export markets.  		During the trade mission I also met with key business leaders and politicians to strengthen our transatlantic relationships, and I took the opportunity to highlight dual market access, which, combined with our strong business environment, makes the North an ideal destination for foreign direct investment.		Finally, whilst in Toronto I also launched a new Canadian Advisory Council, which will work closely with Invest Northern Ireland and North of Ireland Connections, focusing on sectors such as Agri-Tech, Advanced Manufacturing & Engineering, and Fintech & financial services.  The Council will support the Northern’s trade and FDI strategy, helping local businesses expand their presence in the Canadian market.  They will also importantly seek to open a channel for Canadian companies interested in investing in the North of Ireland
