question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether his Department is now advising that AQWs regarding Housing Executive statistics are sent directly to the Housing Executive for reply; (ii) when this change of policy was introduced; and (iii) to detail the rationale behind the decision.
answer_text: This approach was adopted in February 2024 to ensure clarity between the Department’s policy responsibilities and Northern Ireland Housing Executive’s (NIHE) operational remit, as well as to ensure that MLAs receive accurate, timely and full responses.Following the Speaker’s comments in Assembly plenary on 12 March 2024, I will seek operational information from the NIHE Chief Executive and provide it in response to Assembly Questions.Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether he is aware of the work done by the Ballycopeland Windmill Ability Cafe to empower disabled workers; (ii) whether he is aware that this cafe is facing closure; and (ii) whether his Department has any plans to assist in avoiding this closure.
answer_text: I am very aware of the importance of my Department’s state care monuments to local communities and their role in attracting visitors. I am equally aware of the importance of supporting people with disabilities to move closer, into and sustain employment through a range or pre-employment and in-work support provision.Both of these elements of my Department’s responsibilities come together with regard to Ballycopeland Windmill, a state care monument where Ulster Supported Employment Limited (USEL) manage the facilities including the Ability Café.The challenge of finding a viable operating model for the site, within a difficult budgetary environment, is one that I have instructed my officials to examine further. They are assembling a business case to assess a number of options.Decisions following the review may at that stage remain subject to confirmation of budget for 24/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many residents from North Down have had renovation grants not approved due to a lack of funding.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the NI Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive administers the discretionary grants – one of which is Renovation – on behalf of the Department for Communities.  Discretionary grants are subject to an enquirer’s eligibility being met, defined criteria and budget availability.  The Housing Executive has confirmed that in the last two financial years the following number of grant enquiries from North Down, defined as renovation, were cancelled due to budget restrictions:Year 2022/232023/24Renovation enquiry5 0It should be noted the classification ‘Cancellation - Due to Budget Restrictions’ was added during the financial year 2022-23. Data prior to this is not available.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the NI Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 7371/22-27, on what date the road surface of the Groomsport roundabout was found to display early signs of structural failure.
answer_text: Roads will generally deteriorate and become progressively worse over a period of time. Early symptoms include cracking and or deformation before potholes start to form. It can take many years from the first symptoms until defects, such as potholes, start to appear. It is therefore not possible to provide a specific date of when a road starts to show signs of deterioration as my Department does not routinely record signs of wear or defects that do not require action. In the case of Groomsport Roundabout, the resurfacing is being carried out to address the current structural integrity issues and prevent the need for much more costly intervention and disruption later.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the structural integrity of the road surface on Groomsport Road, Bangor, from the Groomsport roundabout to Windmill Road junction.
answer_text: My Department has reviewed the condition of the Groomsport Road from the Groomsport Road Roundabout to the junction at Windmill Road and confirmed that, although the surface shows signs of deterioration as a result of historical road openings, it remains structurally sound.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the danger that the presence of an XL Bully dog kept as a pet in a domestic setting poses to family members and visitors to the home.
answer_text: I have taken the decision to introduce safeguarding measures for XL Bully type dogs as I believe it is the most appropriate way to protect public safety.	Legislation will require owners to appropriately record and account for their dog, to have third-party insurance regarding that dog, to meet certain conditions in public, and to keep their dog in a secure place when not in public.The Dogs (Northern Ireland) Order 1983 currently enables councils to impose control conditions on dogs where an officer has reasonable cause to believe that an offence has been committed.  For example, one such condition would require that the dog is kept securely confined in a building, yard or other enclosure. It remains the case that owners are responsible for controlling their dog, both at home and in public.  My Department have published advice and guidance on ‘responsible dog ownership’ and ‘sharing your house with a dog’ and it is intended that this information will also be included in upcoming messaging on XL Bully type dogs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how his Department plans to develop late night travel options from Belfast city centre.
answer_text: The provision and operation of services is an operational matter for Translink. You should, therefore, direct your question to Translink at aqw.request@translink.co.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many families are on the housing waiting list in (i) Holywood; (ii) Donaghadee; and (iii) Millisle.
answer_text: The number of applicants on the social housing waiting list according to the requested criteria is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.You should, therefore, direct your question to the Housing Executive at its dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what support is available to (i) prison officers; and (ii) police officers who are injured whilst on duty.
answer_text: I have the utmost respect and appreciation for prison officers and police officers for the vital role they play in building a safer community in Northern Ireland.  The Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) has a partnership arrangement with the Police Rehabilitation and Retraining Trust (PRRT) that provides officers who have sustained a physical injury or a psychological impact in the course of their duty with access to physiotherapy and counselling services.   In addition, Prison Officers can access the full range of health and wellbeing services available to all NICS staff through NICS HR and may make an application to the Civil Service Injury Benefits Scheme.The level and nature of support available to police officers is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board (NIPB).  I am committed to respecting both the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the NIPB.  You may wish to direct your question to the PSNI directly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how many applicants have applied for the Shared Skills Academy in each year since its introduction.
answer_text: My department holds records for the previous six financial years. The number of applications to Assured Skills academies in each year are listed in the table below: 	Financial Year	No. Applications to Assured Skills Academies	2017/18	1,357	2018/19	2,533	2019/20	2,063	2020/21	4,236	2021/22	3,074	2022/23	1,678

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the impact of the Shared Skills Academy on the economy.
answer_text: Assured Skills academies have had a very positive impact on the local economy,  providing industry focused training to individuals, equipping them with the  skills required to compete for guaranteed job opportunities in growth sectors. Since 2011 over 2,900 individuals have benefited from the programme, with over 2,500 individuals gaining full-time employment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 6393/22-27, to detail (i) how many attempts his Department has made to recover the cost of damage to road signage; (ii) how many of these attempts were successful; and (iii) the value of such recoveries, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: My Department does not maintain detailed information on the cost recovery associated with damage to road signage. As advised in my answer to AQW 6393/22-27, where my officials are able to identify the vehicle owners responsible for damage to road signs, and where it is cost effective to do so, my Department may seek to recover the relevant repair costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer funding.
answer_text: The Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for football is an important flagship Executive commitment and I recognise the valuable contribution it can deliver for football and wider society. I recognise and share the deep frustration of many that this commitment has not yet been delivered. I have asked my officials to present me with proposals and options for consideration as a matter of priority.Once final proposals are confirmed and approvals are secured, I will be able to provide a timeframe for the process to apply for funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many parking spaces are included in the plans for the Casement Park project to facilitate (i) coaches; and (ii) cars.
answer_text: The current planning permission for the redeveloped Casement Park Stadium, includes circa 200 car and motorcycle parking spaces.While there are no specifically demarcated coach spaces, it is proposed that car park configuration will be adjusted on match days to accommodate up to six coaches.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to confirm whether discussions involving the Casement Park financial arrangements will not be linked to discussions on the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer funding.
answer_text: There will be separate discussions around funding for the completion of the Regional Stadia Programme and funding for other Executive flagship projects including the the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Football.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, pursuant to AQW 5992/22-27, whether the figure of tree felling quoted in his response includes tree felling that has continued during the winter and to date.
answer_text: My earlier response referred to 20 trees that had been felled near the entrance to Cairn Wood for public safety reasons in the autumn of 2023.  As part of overall tree safety management of the Cairn Wood area, an additional 40 - 45 mature trees have been subsequently felled along the Craigantlet road adjacent to the forest for road safety reasons, due to evidence of ash dieback disease and other defects which included evidence of Ganoderma fungus, rot in the trunk of trees, broken tops or snapped branches, included bark unions, extended limbs and dead trees.While this completes the currently scheduled work programme, due to the ongoing prevalence of ash dieback disease future tree inspections may identify the need for further trees to be felled.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she plans to review the operation of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.
answer_text: I am considering options for the commissioning of an independent review into the Northern Ireland Policing Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the guidelines for his departmental officials in relation to time taken in replying to correspondence from elected representatives.
answer_text: The Department aims to respond to all correspondence from elected representative, received through my Private Office, within a maximum of 10 days.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will consult with the Minister for Infrastructure and school management to review pupil and parent road safety issues at Grange Primary School, Bangor.
answer_text: I have asked officials in my Department to liaise with the Department for Infrastructure to request a review of road traffic outside Grange Park Primary School and in the surrounding area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the amount of unpaid (I) domestic rates; and (ii) business rates in North Down in each of the last five years.
answer_text: ANSWERLand & Property Services produces annual statistics showing the amount of Rating Debt and the count of occupancies with Rating Debt at the end of each rating year, by District Council and Sector (Domestic/Non-Domestic).https://admin.opendatani.gov.uk/dataset/annual-end-of-year-rating-debt-by-district-council-and-sector

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, when a vehicle has received an exemption to drive on Northern Ireland roads without a current MOT certificate, provided a test appointment has been booked, whether it is acceptable to licensing authorities in Great Britain for the vehicle to be driven there.
answer_text: My answer to your question AQW 7805/22-27, outlines the steps the DVA has taken to ensure that motorists are still able to use their vehicles when they are unable to secure an MOT appointment before their current MOT expires and confirms these arrangements only apply in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department is considering resurfacing the Sydenham bypass, citybound between George Best City Airport and Dee Street, as part of the additional funding he announced for resurfacing projects.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Whilst the resurfacing programme for 2024/25 has yet to be developed it is my Department’s intention to include a scheme on Sydenham Bypass, subject to the availability of the necessary financial resource.In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline her contingency plans for managing an increase in the prison population beyond capacity.
answer_text: At the outset, I want to place on record my admiration of, and thanks to the staff of the Northern Ireland Prison Service.  The work of prison officers is challenging, complex, often overlooked, but critical to the safety and security of Northern Ireland and people in custody.On 1 March 2021, the total prison population in Northern Ireland was 1,401.  On 1 March this year, that population has risen to 1,879, a 34% increase.  Within that total, 684 prisoners are unsentenced – equating to over 36% of the prison population.The Prison Service is managing this issue by: Preparing all suitable accommodation at both Magilligan and Maghaberry Prisons for occupation;Closely monitoring and managing the balance of the population between Maghaberry and Magilligan; Maximising the safe use of cell sharing (‘doubling-up’) where possible; andDelivering the best prison regimes and services possible with the resources they have available, including through levering in support from other government partners and the voluntary and community sector. The Prison Service has also been actively recruiting and since January 2022, 275 officers have joined the Service.  It is anticipated that a further 27 new recruits will be in post by mid-March.Within the Department of Justice, we have also recently established a Prison Population Oversight Group.  This seeks to bring senior leaders from all relevant organisations – both with the Justice sector and beyond - together to take collective strategic responsibility for identifying and implementing measures to limit or mitigate the impact of the rise in the prison population. The Justice Permanent Secretary established a Prison Population Oversight Group during the latter part of last year.  In doing so, he is seeking to ensure that senior leaders across Justice take collective strategic responsibility to identify and oversee the implementation of measures to limit or mitigate the impact of the rise in the prison population. However to place the burden and responsibility solely on prisons would be grossly unfair.  Prison is the end of the line – much more needs to be done upstream.  Of course, issues such as speeding up justice and remand numbers fall to the wider justice system and that is something I am firmly focused on.However, drug and alcohol addiction, poor levels of education, additional educational needs and poor physical and mental health are all magnified in our prisons.  Imagine what could be achieved if we collectively focus on prevention rather than detention of people.  That is why I say prison issues are not just a justice responsibility – they go much wider and much deeper into our society.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of whether the recent visiting delegation from UEFA are satisfied that the Casement Park project will be completed; and (ii) whether they expressed any concerns about the project being completed in time to host games in the Euro 2028 finals.
answer_text: The UEFA delegates that visited Belfast at the end of February attended a technical presentation and site visit. UEFA has provided initial feedback and has committed to ongoing engagement through the design development for the Euros tournament. While acknowledging the amount of work to be undertaken on the site UEFA fully appreciate the importance of the event to the whole of Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education when he expects funding to become available for the suspended rebuilds for (i) Bangor Central Integrated Primary School; and (ii) Priory Integrated College, Holywood, to proceed.
answer_text: I have placed the 10 remaining former Fresh Start projects that are not yet in contract into my Department’s conventional major works programme to continue to advance in design and planning.  The projects will proceed to construction when planning is complete and affordable within the wider education budget context.All of the projects have been submitted as capital bids for 2024-25 to the Department of Finance and I trust all Executive parties will support these bids for capital funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Housing Supply Strategy 2022-2037.
answer_text: My Department developed a draft housing supply strategy, which was subject to a full public consultation, completing in early 2022. It was not however possible to finalise and publish the strategy at that time without an Executive in place.The primary aim of the draft strategy was to provide a long-term framework for the polices and actions needed to increase supply here. I have stated that housing is a key priority for me and I recognise the value of a long-term and strategic approach to ensuring the delivery of good quality, affordable and sustainable homes.I am currently in the process of reviewing the draft strategy document and considering the available options for progressing it.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether some schools are having to encourage parents to donate basic supplies to enable the school to function.
answer_text: Under current legislation, voluntary contributions are a matter for Boards of Governors.    While current legislation permits Boards of Governors to seek voluntary contributions from parents /guardians, any request must make it clear that:  there is no obligation to contribute; and registered pupils at the school will not be treated differently according to whether or not their parents/guardians have made a contribution. That said, I am acutely aware that many schools across Northern Ireland are facing difficult decisions, to enable them to live within their delegated budgets.  Clearly, this is of great concern to me, and I would urge any school facing financial difficulties to contact the Education Authority as funding authority for grant-aided schools.There can be no greater investment in our future than investment in education and I will continue to make the case for the additional resources, which our schools need from the Executive’s Budget 2024-25.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) to detail the extent of the accessibility of illegal drugs in (a) prisons; and (b) detention centres; and (ii) what measures her Department is taking to prevent such drugs entering the penal system.
answer_text: There is clear evidence that some individuals are determined to traffic illicit substances into prison.  It is also the case that medicines prescribed in prison can cause considerable harm if they are not used as intended, or if they are trafficked between prisoners.Prison officers work tirelessly to disrupt the flow of and reduce the harm caused by illicit substances in prisons.  This includes using search techniques and technology, drug detection dogs, acting upon information about individuals involved, and working closely with the Police Service of Northern Ireland.  Recent additional steps taken include an upgrade of staff searching within each establishment and the introduction of x-ray body scanners.The Northern Ireland Prison Service remains focused on delivering its Substance Misuse Strategy aimed at Restricting Supply, Reducing Demand and Building Recovery for those in prison custody.   Detention Centres are not the responsibility of the Department of Justice or Northern Ireland Prison Service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether vehicle owners with an expired MOT certificate can continue to use public roads in Northern Ireland, provided they have a pending MOT appointment booked; and (ii) whether the police services in GB are aware of exemptions, as a result of the MOT backlog.
answer_text: In 2022-2023, the Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) conducted over 1.15 million vehicle tests which is the highest number ever recorded in a year. This increase in capacity has been achieved by adopting a range of measures including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners, the most recent recruitment competition launched on 19 February 2024 to fill staff vacancies across the DVA’s network of test centres.  The DVA also uses overtime to provide cover for leave and they offer vehicle test appointments on Sundays and bank holidays, when testing is not normally available.  Disappointingly, the number of vehicles that failed to attend a booked appointment in 2022-2023 was also the highest on record at 50,772.The DVA appreciates that, in some cases, customers may not be able to have their vehicles tested before their current MOT certificate expires and has taken steps to ensure that motorists are still able to use their vehicles when they are unable to secure an MOT appointment before their current MOT expires. The latest information on MOTs is available on NI Direct at A guide to booking your MOT/ vehicle test | nidirect and is also included as an advice note with every MOT reminder letter issued to customers.The PSNI has agreed not to penalise drivers whose MOT has expired so long as:your vehicle is in a roadworthy and safe condition and doesn’t have a statutory off-road notification (SORN) declarationyour vehicle is properly insuredyou can prove (by showing an MOT appointment notification/ reminder, either in electronic or paper form) that a test appointment has been booked for your vehicleAll other vehicle roadworthiness and insurance offences will be dealt with in the usual way.These arrangements only apply in  the North. They do not cover other jurisdictions, where a valid MOT certificate is required.Driving an untaxed vehicle on a public road can result in enforcement action being taken by the Driver and Vehicle Licensing Agency (DVLA), Swansea.The DVA’s advice to vehicle owners who require an MOT to tax their vehicle and cannot secure a date before their MOT expires is to:•	book the earliest available appointment; •	keep checking the booking system for an appointment before their MOT expires. If they get to within five days of the expiry date of their vehicle tax and they have been unable to secure an appointment, or their vehicle tax has expired, they should contact the DVA Customer Services team at dva.customerservices@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk and they will do their best to get an urgent appointment for the vehicle.  To date, all customers who have contacted the DVA in these circumstances have been offered an earlier appointment.In addition, vehicle test appointments are released on a daily basis, when resources become available, so customers are encouraged to regularly check the booking system as they may be able to change their existing appointment to a different test centre and time if a slot becomes available.  Information on how a

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) what action she has taken to have Northern Ireland included in the UK legislation to overturn convictions of postmasters and postmistresses in the Horizon scandal; and (ii) to detail the case she made for Northern Ireland to be included in the UK legislation.
answer_text: On my return to the Department of Justice, I wrote to the Lord Chancellor and the Secretary for State for Business and Trade to advise that I would support the extension to Northern Ireland of the legislation to reverse the convictions of sub-postmasters affected by the Post Office Horizon scandal to Northern Ireland.  I expressed my view that this approach would be the best way forward, given that postal services are a reserved matter and the associated compensation schemes are UK-wide.  This would also have been the fastest legislative solution and avoided differential treatment between jurisdictions, including in terms of accessing compensation.  		I reiterated that position in my meeting with the Parliamentary Under Secretary of State for Business and Trade, Kevin Hollinrake MP, which preceded his public announcement that the legislation to overturn the convictions of sub-postmasters affected by the Post Office Horizon scandal will apply only to England and Wales.  		Following that announcement, I issued a joint letter, along with First Minister and deputy First Minister, calling on the UK Government to reconsider this decision.  		I have also written to the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland asking him to support my position.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many families are currently on the waiting list in North Down for a Housing Executive home.
answer_text: The number of applicants on the social housing waiting list in North Down is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.You should, therefore, direct your question to the Housing Executive at its dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) how many social housing units have been built in North Down in each of the last five years; and (ii) how many social housing units are planned to be built in North Down in each of the next five years.
answer_text: The information you have requested regarding (i) how many social housing units have been built in North Down in each of the last five years; and (ii) how many social housing units are planned to be built in North Down in each of the next five years is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.You should, therefore, direct your question to Northern Ireland Housing Executive at  Public.Affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the timeline to introduce legislation to overturn the convictions of postmasters and postmistresses in the Horizon scandal.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer I gave to AQW 7687/22-27.I remain of the view that Northern Ireland should be included within the scope of any Westminster Legislation, as this would be the fastest legislative solution and avoid differential treatment between jurisdictions, including in terms of accessing compensation.  	I have, however, asked my officials to review what other options may be available to provide a remedy for sub-postmasters in Northern Ireland, should the decision remain that the legislation will not be extended, though none of these are optimal.  These options include taking forward separate legislation through the Assembly to replicate the Westminster provisions, which would create additional delays, or requiring the individuals affected to appeal their convictions through the courts.		Given work is ongoing to identify a way forward, I do not have a timeframe available at present. However, I remain committed to ensuring that a swift and equitable remedy is delivered to Northern Ireland postmasters and postmistresses as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on potential revenue-raising measures currently being considered by her Department.
answer_text: The Executive recognises the need for fiscal sustainability and as part of a new fiscal framework will want to look at all options to deliver efficiencies, generate revenue, enhance borrowing powers and examine fiscal devolution.The main revenue raising measure which falls within my department’s remit is the Regional Rate. The Executive has agreed to recommend to the Assembly that the Regional Rate should increase by 4% in 2024-25.This should be seen as a clear demonstration that the Executive recognises the need to deliver high quality public services and to ensure Stormont’s finances are on a more sustainable footing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) what cost has been incurred in bringing the Bangor Central Integrated Primary School project to its current position; and (ii) what budget was used to meet these costs.
answer_text: The Bangor Central IPS scheme has incurred costs of £734k to date.  These costs have been met from the Fresh Start budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) what cost has been incurred in bringing the Priory Integrated College project to its current position; and (ii) what budget was used to meet these costs.
answer_text: The Priory IC scheme has incurred costs of £842k to date. These costs have been met from the Fresh Start budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) when the decision to suspend the rebuild of (a) Bangor Central Integrated Primary School; and (b) Priory Integrated College was conveyed to the school management; and (ii) what method was used to convey this decision.
answer_text: No decision has been taken to suspend the new build projects for Bangor Central Integrated Primary School or Priory Integrated College.  Both schools, like all those impacted, were advised by telephone and letter on Thursday 22 February that following the withdrawal of Fresh Start funding, I had agreed to place their project into DE’s Executive funded major capital works programme. This will allow the projects to continue to advance in planning and design.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on recruitment to fill gaps in Housing Executive, created by reported high resignation numbers.
answer_text: Matters relating to staff recruitment and retention within the Housing Executive are operational issues for the Housing Executive.You should, therefore, direct your question to the Housing Executive at its dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether the additional £8.1 million he has released from the 2023-24 budget to fund the structural road maintenance programme has any deadline for the (a) commencement; or (b) completion of projects to be undertaken; and (ii) what criteria will be applied to select projects.
answer_text: The additional £8.1m funding for my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme is to deliver additional resurfacing schemes and continue to address the poor condition of our road network. Work has started immediately on the planning, and delivery of these new schemes within the current financial year.My Department will prioritise the programme on the basis of a number of data factors including road usage and general surface condition surveys as well as information from maintenance inspections, public enquiries and public liability claims, as well as on the professional engineering judgements of local engineers. Programmes are subject to continual review and take into account a wide range of factors such as worsening condition of other roads, planned works by utilities, additional in year funding, and contractor capacity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how much was spent in settling vehicle damage compensation claims in North Down, in each year since 2019.
answer_text: The management information my Department holds in relation to claims can be found online at:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/road-asset-maintenance-directorate-management-information-roads-related-claims-period-20182019 Table 7 in the above link provides details of vehicle damage expenditure for the Ards and North Down Roads Section Office, which broadly aligns with the Ards and North Down Council area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 6124/22-27, whether he will now consider resurfacing the section of the Sydenham bypass, citybound from the entrance to the George Best Belfast City Airport to the Dee Street junction, given the additional budget he has made available for his Department's structural road maintenance programme.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Whilst the resurfacing programme for 2024/25 has yet to be developed it is my Department’s intention to include a scheme on Sydenham Bypass, subject to the availability of the necessary financial resource.In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will consider prioritising the resurfacing of Groomsport Road, Bangor, from Sheridan Drive to the Groomsport roundabout, as part of the additional funding available for resurfacing work.
answer_text: The additional £8.1m funding for my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme is to deliver additional resurfacing schemes and continue to address the poor condition of our road network. This funding has now been allocated across all four of my Department’s road divisions.Work has started on the planning and delivery of new schemes for delivery this financial year. While the development of the programmes is on-going, preparation of a scheme to resurface the Groomsport Road from Sheridan Drive to the Groomsport roundabout is not sufficiently advanced to allow for its delivery this financial year.  Details of the planned list of resurfacing schemes for the 2024/25 financial year in the North Down area will be included in the Department’s Annual Report to Ards and North Down Borough Council in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will consider including Silverbirch Road, Bangor, as part of the resurfacing work his Department will carry out, as a result of the additional £8.1 million funding.
answer_text: The additional £8.1m funding for my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme is to deliver additional resurfacing schemes and continue to address the poor condition of our road network. This funding has now been allocated across all four of my Department’s road divisions.Work has started on the planning and delivery of new schemes for delivery this financial year. While the development of the programmes is on-going, Silverbirch Road would not be considered for inclusion at this time, given its relative priority when compared to the many other roads and footways in greater need of investment. Details of the planned list of resurfacing schemes for the 2024/25 financial year in the North Down area will be included in the Department’s Annual Report to Ards and North Down Borough Council in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the contribution made by health charities in (i) raising public awareness and education of various conditions; and (ii) the support they offer to those, and the families, they represent.
answer_text: I recognise the vital contribution made by the voluntary and community sector (VCS) and the need for us to work in partnership to address the health and social care needs of the population of Northern Ireland.The VCS and health charities are key partners in delivering education, prevention, and early intervention services across a range of issues, and their role is vital in both raising awareness and providing services to individuals, communities, and families across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the proposed Casement Park stadium project is on target to be completed in time to host the European Football Championships in 2028.
answer_text: UEFA’s requirement for the EURO 2028 Tournament, is for construction to be completed by June 2027. Officials in my Department and wider tournament stakeholders are working to an integrated project programme that can meet this requirement.  This includes procurement of a new contractor for the main construction works using a UK Government Framework. An overall funding package will have to be secured and additional funding requirements will have to be agreed with partners.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many representations he received from (i) elected representatives; and (ii) members of the public, requesting the resurfacing of the Groomsport roundabout, Bangor.
answer_text: My Department does not maintain records of specific representations being made by either elected representatives or members of the public requesting the resurfacing of the Groomsport Road Roundabout. There are a number of factors used when determining the need for road resurfacing, not all of which are immediately evident, including road usage, general surface condition and structural deformation.  It was decided to resurface this roundabout because it was showing early signs of structural failure which is likely to have led to a rapid deterioration in surface condition. The work was planned for inclusion in the 2024/25 resurfacing programme subject to available resources; however, following a reallocation of capital funding it has been brought forward into this 2023/24 year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has raised any concerns in relation to the footpaths laid during the public realm work in Bangor city centre becoming slippery during wet weather conditions.
answer_text: My Department is investigating recent concerns that have been raised regarding the pavement installed during the Public Realm scheme in Bangor City Centre.This issue will be considered further by my officials and raised with the Council, who were responsible for the delivery of the scheme, if required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many claims his Department has received in relation to injuries to members of the public from slipping on footpaths in Bangor city centre, in the last five years.
answer_text: The Bangor City Advisory Group consider the following streets to comprise Bangor City Centre:  Main Street, High Street, King Street, Abbey Street, Queens Parade, Bridge Street, the top of Dufferin Avenue and the part of Hamilton Road from Main Street to Mills Road.Over the past five years, my Department has received two claims which cited slipping as a cause on these streets.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the new jobs Invest NI has helped to create in the North Down constituency, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Invest NI reports its performance on a financial year basis with 2022-23, ending 31st March 2023, being the most recent year available.  Information relating to 2023-24 is provisionally planned for release in June 2024.  During the period 2018-19 to 2022-23 Invest NI provided support to businesses which will lead to the creation of 398 new jobs in the North Down Parliamentary Constituency.  Jobs arising from these projects will be created over the next 3 to 5 years and will result in an increase in annual salaries of £11.72m.  The table below details these jobs in each of the last 5 years.Invest NI New Jobs in North Down Parliamentary Constituency (2018-19 to 2022-23)YearNew Jobs2018-19802019-20752020-211962021-22162022-2331Total398

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the final cost for the delivery of the Casement Park project.
answer_text: For commercial reasons, I will not be providing any details on final costs at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what inward investment Invest NI has attracted to the North Down constituency, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Invest NI reports its performance on a financial year basis with 2022-23, ending 31st March 2023, being the most recent year available.  Information relating to 2023-24 is provisionally planned for release in June 2024.  The table below details the number of offers that Invest NI has offered towards inward investment projects in the North Down Parliamentary Constituency between 2018-19 and 2022-23, the amount of financial assistance offered, and the amount of total planned investment that this support has secured for the area.  Total planned investment is the combination of business investment and assistance offered by Invest NI in support of a project. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an assessment of the Community of Lifesavers Education programme, including in relation to (i) funding; and (ii) interest from teachers.
answer_text: The Community of Lifesavers programme is a successful and well supported multi-agency approach to ensure, through the professional development of teachers, that every child has the opportunity to learn emergency life support skills including CPR & AED Awareness. Since 2022/23, 429 teachers have completed the programme and feedback from participants continues to be very positive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of applications for the Community of Lifesavers Teacher Professional Learning programme, broken down by (i) post- primary school; (ii) Special educational school; and (iii) EOTAS.
answer_text: The number of applications for the Community of Lifesavers Teacher Professional Learning programme is broken down as follows:2022/20232023/2024Post Primary217133Special3529EOTAS015Total Number of Participants252177

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail his Department's strategy to promote biodiversity.
answer_text: My Department is developing a Nature Recovery (Biodiversity) Strategy which includes objectives that support the promotion of biodiversity across government and society.  I plan to launch a consultation on the Strategy later this year, after which the objectives will be finalised for publication. The promotion of biodiversity across government and society will be supported by a number of other key plans and strategies that my Department is taking forward over the coming months. These include the Climate Action Plan, and the Environmental Improvement Plan.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) how many trees his Department has planted in each of the last five years; and (ii) what the target for new tree planting is for each of the next five years.
answer_text: Over the past 5 years, the Department has planted approximately 6.7 million trees on its estate and grant aided land owners to plant approximately 4.6 million trees. Table 1 below sets out the number of trees planted on the Department’s land to regenerate forest sites following harvesting and to create additional new woodland.Table 1Trees Planted (‘000)2018/192019/202020/212021/222022/23Forest regeneration1,4611,3361,3131,4171,108New woodland0411.5230Total1,4611,3771,3151,4401,108Table 2 below sets out the number of trees supported by forestry grant schemes and planted by land owners over the past five years.Table 2Trees Planted (‘000)2018/192019/202020/212021/222022/23Forest regeneration12018658324696New woodland476300566962902Total5964866241,2681,598The Department reviews and sets targets annually for planting new woodland on a per hectare basis, as part of its business planning processes. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of what effect the lowering of corporation tax would have on the Northern Ireland economy.
answer_text: The Independent Fiscal Commission NI in its Final Report of May 2022 recognised that a lower corporation tax in the North could be economically beneficial to the economy.It also noted that there would have to be agreement on how the block grant is adjusted, so that it would be affordable to the Executive in the short-term. This will be taken forward as part of negotiations on a new Fiscal Framework.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 5692/22-27, for an estimate of when such plans will be published to assist businesses that rely on east-west trade.
answer_text: The timeframe for this work is the responsibility of the British Government.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department has any plans to assist in lowering the rate of student poverty.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the financial pressures facing further and higher education students and I will be engaging with my officials to consider the support available and making announcements in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the impact the failure to reach the number of police officers recommended in the Patten Report has had on crime fighting strategies.
answer_text: Police officer numbers and the prioritisation of resources are a matter for the Chief Constable who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board.  You may therefore wish to address your question regarding the impact on crime fighting strategies directly to the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether temporary seating will be installed in Casement Park during the UEFA European Football Championship to meet UEFA safety regulations; and (ii) who will pay for this.
answer_text: Work is ongoing with all partners to deliver the stadium to meet UEFA technical specifications. A stadium configuration of 30,000 seats will be provided for the EURO 2028 tournament, which will be returned to the Gaelic games configuration thereafter.	Funding allocations are the subject of ongoing discussions between the EURO 2028 partners.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the impact the shortage of childcare support has on productivity.
answer_text: Increasing productivity is one of my four key priorities for the economy. Better childcare will help improve productivity and I am committed to working with other Departments on this important issue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what the final capacity will be in the proposed rebuild of Casement Park, broken down by (i) seated; and (ii) standing.
answer_text: The final capacity for the proposed rebuild of Casement Park will be 34,578. This is broken down as:(i) 26,403 seated(ii) 8,175 standing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the importance of high public uptake of all vaccines and screening programmes currently available.
answer_text: Vaccination has drastically reduced the burden of infectious diseases, and for the individual, vaccination means protection before exposure to fatal or life-changing diseases. Vaccination also protects against complications or secondary infections of those diseases and even if disease does occur, the severity is often reduced. Having a vaccine also benefits the whole community through "herd immunity". If enough people are vaccinated, it's harder for the disease to spread, including to those people who cannot have the vaccine.Unfortunately, Northern Ireland has experienced a drop in vaccination uptake in recent years, especially in childhood immunisations. For MMR specifically, the WHO recommend an uptake of 95% to ensure full protection for the individual and to ensure population immunity by preventing spread among communities. Uptake of the MMR vaccine in NI has dropped to 88% for the first dose, and 85% for the second, and we have recently seen the first case of measles being confirmed in NI since 2017. This demonstrates the importance of achieving a high uptake rate in vaccinations.Population screening programmes also have a key role to play in early detection of disease and there are currently eight programmes operating in Northern Ireland.  Screening programmes enable apparently healthy people who may be at increased risk of a disease or condition, to be identified. They can then be offered information, further tests and appropriate treatment to reduce their risk and/or any complications arising from the disease or condition.It is essential that we maximise uptake rates in our vaccination and screening programmes, in order to help minimise the impact of diseases or conditions on an individual, as well as reduce the burden on the health and social care system of dealing with these diseases and conditions at a later stage.  I would therefore encourage anyone who is eligible, to take up the offer of vaccination or screening, if invited to do so.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the percentage of vehicles that failed the MOT test in each of the years from 2019 to 2023, broken down by each MOT test centre.
answer_text: DVA’s National Statistics publication ‘DfI Driver, Vehicle, Operator and Enforcement Statistics’ details monthly and quarterly vehicle test pass rates by vehicle type and by test centre in Table 1.14 (‘Vehicle Test Pass Rates by Test Category and Test Centre’). The year-end, Quarter 4, publication details annual pass rates for the relevant financial year in Table 1.15 ‘Vehicle Test Pass Rates by Test Category and Test Centre – Annual’. These tables for each quarter from 2019 to 2023 can be found at:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/driver-vehicle-agency-activity-statistics.  Quarter 3 statistics for the period from 1 October to 31 December 2023 are provisionally due for publication in March 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) why his Department is taking the lead in the Casement Park project; and (ii) whether the same approach was taken in the Windsor Park and Ravenhill stadium projects.
answer_text: Following a review and assessment of the procurement options available to deliver the Casement Park project, my Department endorsed the use of a UK Government Framework for the procurement of the main construction works to ensure delivery in line with UEFA requirements for hosting the EURO 2028 in Northern Ireland.The same approach was not taken in the Windsor Park and Ravenhill stadium projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the circumstances surrounding the placement of boulders on a roadside section of Warren Road, Donaghadee; and (ii) the action taken by his Department in regard to this issue.
answer_text: The Department was made aware of the unauthorised placement of boulders on the roadside section of Warren Road, Donaghadee on 4th January 2024 and immediately referred the matter to the PSNI.  The boulders have subsequently been removed from the public road. My officials will carry out a safety assessment now that the boulders have been removed and will consider if any measures are required to assist in the management of parking in the area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure the percentage of vehicles that failed the MOT test in each of year from 2019 to 2023.
answer_text: DVA’s National Statistics publication ‘DfI Driver, Vehicle, Operator and Enforcement Statistics’ details monthly and quarterly vehicle test pass rates by vehicle type and by test centre in Table 1.14 (‘Vehicle Test Pass Rates by Test Category and Test Centre’). The year-end, Quarter 4, publication details annual pass rates for the relevant financial year in Table 1.15 ‘Vehicle Test Pass Rates by Test Category and Test Centre – Annual’. These tables for each quarter from 2019 to 2023 can be found at:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/driver-vehicle-agency-activity-statistics.  Quarter 3 statistics for the period from 1 October to 31 December 2023 are provisionally due for publication in March 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has any plans to upgrade the centre road markings on the B511 Bangor Road, Groomsport.
answer_text: My Department carries out regular inspections of all public roads and footways to ensure that essential response maintenance is identified and completed as necessary. During these inspections all defects are noted, including for example faded road markings.However, my Department’s resource budget, which is used to deliver day-to-day maintenance activities has been under significant pressure for a number of years. As a result, my Department has had to reduce its routine road maintenance activities to a limited service, which prioritises delivery of essential services for maintaining public safety. Details of the current road maintenance standards can be found at:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/road-maintenance-standards-for-safety-limited-service-in-2021-22-dem-160-21.pdf My officials will assess the centre road marking on the B511 Bangor Road, Groomsport in line with current policy and, if they meet the current intervention level, will add them to a future programme of work subject to budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 5694/22-27, (i) when officials last reviewed the speed limits on Orlock Road, Groomsport; and (ii) how it corresponds to his Department's policy on setting local speed limits.
answer_text: I can confirm that a review of the speed limit on Orlock Road in Groomsport was undertaken in early February 2024.When considering the appropriate speed limit for a road the Department’s policy and procedures guide entitled “Setting Local Speed Limits in Northern Ireland” is used to ensure speed limits are consistent and motorists are presented with the same speed limit on similar roads taking into account factors such as: the characteristic of the road; the speed of existing traffic; the degree of frontage development; and road traffic collision data, which is continuously monitored using data provided by the PSNI.The current speed limit on Orlock Road is in line with our current policy document and considered appropriate.  Therefore, I do not propose to implement any speed restrictions at this location at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are plans to introduce traffic control measures on the Newtownards Road roundabout, Bangor.
answer_text: My Department has previously introduced measures at this location which help to enhance driver awareness and reduce vehicle speeds on this busy part of the road network.  Although there are no plans to carry out additional works at this time, I have asked my officials to keep the location under review.All requests for improvements to the road network, including road improvement schemes, are assessed in line with my Department’s current policies and guidance and all works are subject to prioritisation within the funding available. Details of the planned list of local transport and safety measures for the 2023/24 financial year in the Ards and North Down area are included in the Department’s Annual Report for Ards and North Down which can be found at; https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/annual-report-ards-and-north-down-borough-council-20232024

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has plans to introduce a 20mph speed limit at Towerview Primary School, Bangor.
answer_text: Part-time 20mph limits near schools are implemented based on assessments considering collision history, speed, traffic volume, infrastructure, and site constraints.  Schools have been assessed in accordance with DfI’s policy ‘Road Safety at Schools’ which can be viewed at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/road-safety-schools-rsppg-e070 The program to date has been rolled out in tranches, with the most recent completed in November 2022.  Over the coming weeks, I will be considering my priorities, including the potential for a further tranche of the 20mph schools programme, in the context of the funding that is currently available to my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what action his Department has taken to prevent surface water running from landowners land on Springwell Road, Groomsport, onto the public road and contributing to the flooding of domestic premises.
answer_text: Road drainage systems are designed to cope with annual average daily rainfall for any given time of the year and cannot cope with the short duration high intensity rain events that have been occurring more frequently in recent times.Officials have visited the site several times during heavy rain, and each time the road drainage systems have been clear and running. However, some water run-off from areas of private land has been noted. My Department will take steps to identify the relevant landowner and take the necessary action to prevent further discharge of water onto the public road.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the York Street Interchange.
answer_text: Major Road schemes are an important part of the work that is delivered by my Department as we seek to reduce journey times, increase reliability and improve road safety.In October 2022 I published a Placemaking and Active Travel Review (PATR) report on the scheme and asked my officials to carry out further work on three scenarios recommended within it. This work is expected to conclude in Spring 2024. Details of the current status of the York Street Interchange project will continue to be available on the project webpage which can be viewed at: https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/york-street-interchange-overview

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline how much the consultation process on measures to support budget sustainability and raise additional revenue has cost to date.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to provide an update on the implementation of The Path to Net Zero Energy strategy.
To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of requests from HM Treasury to identify revenue raising streams.
answer_text: The Executive has already given a commitment to the British Government to develop a Sustainability Plan with a pre-requisite that the right level of funding is provided.The Government’s timescale requiring this to be published by May 2024 is completely unrealistic. It is essential that the Executive is afforded adequate time to give proper consideration to this important matter.The requirement that we raise £113m through locally generated revenue in the next 12 months is not consistent with a strategic Sustainability Plan. To expect this funding to be generated in such a short space of time can only serve to cause more harm to hard pressed families, households and businesses.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of requests from HM Treasury to identify revenue raising streams.
answer_text: We are clear that revenue raising cannot be used as a tool to punish people nor cause unfair or undue financial burden.  There is an indisputable case for needs based funding, based upon the independent advice of the Fiscal Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether the pending resurfacing of Groomsport roundabout, Bangor, was included in the Annual Report for 2023/24 presented to Ards and North Down Borough Council; (ii) whether the resurfacing project was included in budgets for 2023/24; and (iii) to detail the criteria used to prioritise this project.
answer_text: I can confirm that the Groomsport Road Roundabout, Bangor resurfacing scheme was not included in the Annual Report for 2023/24 as presented to Ards and North Down Borough Council. The Report for Ards and North Down which can be found at; https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/annual-report-ards-and-north-down-borough-council-2023.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether his Department has an inspection strategy for maintaining and replacing damaged road signs; and (ii) whether his Department recovers costs from vehicle owners who damage road signage.
answer_text: My Department carries out regular inspections of all public roads and footways to ensure that essential response maintenance is identified and completed as necessary. During these inspections all defects are noted, including for example defective signs and signs requiring cleaning to improve their visibility.However, my Department’s resource budget, which is used to deliver day-to-day maintenance activities has been under significant pressure for a number of years. As a result, my Department has had to reduce its routine road maintenance activities to a limited service, which prioritises delivery of essential services for maintaining public safety. Details of the current road maintenance standards can be found at:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/road-maintenance-standards-for-safety-limited-service-in-2021-22-dem-160-21.pdf Where my Department’s officials are able to identify vehicle owners responsible for damage to road signs, and where it is cost effective to do so, my Department may seek to recover the relevant repair costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for an update on schools identified in 2023 to have contained Reinforced Autoclaved Aerated Concrete; and (ii) whether any of these schools are still considered to be at risk.
answer_text: Reinforced Autoclaved Aerated Concrete (RAAC) has been identified in one school in Northern Ireland, Cairnshill Primary School.The safety of pupils and staff are the utmost priority for the Department. RAAC was identified in a first-floor roof of Cairnshill Primary School, resulting in the closure of an eight-classroom block within the school.  Temporary classrooms have been provided to ensure the safe and ongoing operation of the school while appropriate remedial works to the roof are planned and delivered.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the £1 million additional funding to address potholes will be divided equally between regional divisions or on the basis of local need.
answer_text: The much needed funding that I recently announced will be used between now and the end of the financial year to target areas of highest priority with small scale resurfacing schemes designed to repair roads that have suffered the most and where localised repairs are less effective. Given the widespread nature of this issue it will be distributed across all divisional areas. However, this is only the beginning of much needed investment in our road infrastructure, and I will continue to seek the support of Executive colleagues for further funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether delays in issuing planning permission presents obstacles to his Department in its efforts to encourage inward investment.
answer_text: My Department is aware of the need to improve the planning system. However, we are not aware of any specific impacts on efforts to encourage inward investment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for his assessment of reports that the average rent is rising faster than incomes; and (ii) whether his Department has any plans to address this.
answer_text: I am aware of an increase in rents in the private rented sector. Research carried out by my Department concluded the best ways of relieving pressure on affordability for renters is through increasing housing supply, and ensuring the benefit system properly takes into account the cost of housing.One of my key priorities is to ensure that people have access to safe, warm, and affordable homes. To help achieve this, I will continue to prioritise the building of new social homes and utilise new funding sources to support the delivery of intermediate housing, including a new intermediate rent product.  I am also currently reviewing a draft Housing Supply Strategy, aimed at unlocking supply across all tenures, and considering options on the way forward.In the shorter term, the increase in the Local Housing Allowance rate from April 2024 to the 30th percentile of local market rents, should go some way to assist many renters with affordability of rent costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of whether his Department's strategy for routine roadside gully cleaning had failed to prevent recent localised flooding incidents.
answer_text: Historically, my Department aimed to inspect and, where necessary, clean all gullies in urban areas twice each year and gullies in rural areas once per year. However, due to budgetary constraints, in recent years my Department aims to inspect and, where necessary, clean all gullies once annually.In addition, when severe weather warnings are received, gully emptiers are placed on standby, stocks of sandbags, pumps and signs are checked and replenished as necessary and gullies and screens at known flooding areas are inspected and cleaned, when required.This winter has seen flooding as the result of a number of storms and extreme weather events.During these storms, many road gullies and urban drainage systems were operating to capacity, and simply not able to cope with rainfall above the normal levels for which they are designed. Furthermore, despite everyone’s best efforts, road gullies can also become blocked during storm events with debris which adds to the overall risk of flooding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has a rolling programme in place for the cleaning of road signage.
answer_text: My Department carries out regular inspections of all public roads and footways to ensure that essential response maintenance is identified and completed as necessary. During these inspections all defects are noted, including for example defective signs and signs requiring cleaning to improve their visibility.However, my Department’s resource budget, which is used to deliver day-to-day maintenance activities has been under significant pressure for a number of years as a result of a decade of tory austerity and cuts . In response my Department has had to reduce its routine road maintenance activities to a limited service, which prioritises delivery of essential services for maintaining public safety. Details of the current road maintenance standards can be found at:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/road-maintenance-standards-for-safety-limited-service-in-2021-22-dem-160-21.pdf The importance of a well maintained road network cannot be understated but this requires investment. I will continue to seek the support of Executive colleagues to enable this to happen.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the relocation of Central Primary School, Bangor.
answer_text: This project is in the final stage of design with a planning application submitted in July 2022.  Procurement of a contractor is programmed to commence following receipt of planning permission.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she is giving any priority consideration to seeking a reduction of corporation tax.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer previously provided to Assembly Question, AQW 5424/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are plans to resurface any part of Groomsport Road, Bangor.
answer_text: Details of the planned list of resurfacing schemes for the 2023/24 financial year in the North Down area are included in the Department’s Annual Report for Ards and North Down which can be found at;https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/annual-report-ards-and-north-down-borough-council-2023.pdf. However, it has now been possible to include the resurfacing of the Groomsport Road Roundabout in Bangor and work is due to commence on 19 February 2024. The works will involve resurfacing of the roundabout along with associated footway and kerbing works.The estimated cost of the scheme is approximately £460,000 and represents as significant investment in the road infrastructure in North Down.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he can confirm that no mechanical compaction is currently deployed in the repair of potholes.
answer_text: My Department carries out the repair of surface defects using both internal and external contractors using a number of methods depending on the situation and repair type required.Permanent pothole repairs are completed with hot bituminous material and require mechanical compaction in line with the currently approved Codes of Practices and Specifications for the Reinstatement of Openings in Roads.My Department also deploys jet patching, which is normally confined to rural roads during spring/summer/autumn when road temperatures are appropriate and does not involve any mechanical compaction by design as the material is placed directly into the defect under pressure.In addition to this, temporary/emergency repairs are undertaken where high priority potholes/defects are made safe as part of incident response. This could be during or outside of normal working hours and can involve the use of cold lay bituminous material, compacted with a tamping bar.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has any plans to resurface the citybound carriageway of the Sydenham bypass, from the entrance to George Best City Airport to the Dee Street junction.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition.While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of the planned list of resurfacing schemes for the 2023/24 financial year are included in the Department’s Annual Report for Belfast City Council which can be found at; https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/dfi-roads-2023-annual-report-to-belfast-city-council.pdf  Work contained in the Annual Report is planned for completion in the current financial year subject to the ongoing availability of resources, capacity and subject to other events like severe weather that can impact programming.The programme for 2024/25 will be developed once future budgets are agreed.In the meantime, officials in my Department will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the Queens Parade redevelopment project in Bangor: and (ii) to confirm the date that enabling work will begin on site.
answer_text: My officials, in the Department’s Regional Development Office, along with Ards and North Down Borough Council, are continuing to work collaboratively with the developer, Bangor Marine, to progress the Queen’s Parade project and the broader regeneration of Bangor City.Planning Permission for the Queen’s Parade project was granted on 29th September 2022. Since then Bangor Marine has been working to finalise the scheme design, and work through the pre-commencement conditions that emerged from the planning approval. Bangor Marine and Ards and North Down Borough Council are also now working to secure the necessary approvals for the developer’s proposals that fall on the section of the site belonging to the Crown Estate. Officials in my Department’s Regional Development Office are working closely with Bangor Marine and Council teams to ensure this work progresses as quickly as possible. According to Bangor Marine’s latest plan, they will be on-site in the latter part of this year. Ards and North Down Borough Council is also currently developing its proposals for the regeneration of Bangor Waterfront, being funded by the NI Executive under the Belfast Region City Deal programme. Following the approval of the Outline Business Case and the issue of a Contract for Funding for the Council’s proposals, work is progressing well to appoint design teams and further develop the proposals to inform a Final Business Case.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether Northern Ireland Water will publish live data on spills from storm overflows, similar to that of the water companies in England and Wales.
answer_text: The issue you raised is an operational matter for NI Water.  You should therefore, direct your question to NI Water, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the pending work on Groomsport Roundabout, Bangor; and (ii) the estimated cost of this project.
answer_text: Details of the planned list of resurfacing schemes for the 2023/24 financial year in the North Down area are included in the Department’s Annual Report for Ards and North Down which can be found at;https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/annual-report-ards-and-north-down-borough-council-2023.pdf. However, it has now been possible to include the resurfacing of the Groomsport Road Roundabout in Bangor and work is due to commence on 19 February 2024. The works will involve resurfacing of the roundabout along with associated footway and kerbing works.The estimated cost of the scheme is approximately £460,000 and represents as significant investment in the road infrastructure in North Down.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether the Northern Ireland Environment Agency will publish live data on spills from storm overflows, similar to that of water companies in England and Wales.
answer_text: The NIEA are concerned about storm overflows and drives improvement through investment by NI Water.NIEA and NI Water are collectively moving through the commissioning process of storm-overflow monitors to ensure the data received is accurate and reliable. Whilst there is no statutory obligation for NIEA to publish the data, it is NIEA’s intention to publish annual performance reports once accurate and reliable data is provided by NI Water.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) the rationale for the recent tree felling near the entrance to Cairn Wood, Craigantlet; (ii) the number of trees felled; and (iii) the number of healthy trees felled.
answer_text: In keeping with duty of care, Forest Service undertakes risk based inspection programmes with a particular focus on trees at risk of causing injury or damage to the public, staff or infrastructure. Following inspections along the Craigantlet road, adjacent to Cairn Wood, twenty mature trees which displayed symptoms of ash dieback disease were felled for public safety reasons in the autumn of 2023. In addition, some hedgerow saplings were reduced in height at this time, as part of boundary management and maintenance of the area.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) when funding from the Sub Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer will be released; and (ii) whether the previously announced breakdown of how the funds will be distributed will be adhered to.
answer_text: I recognise and share the deep frustration of many that this programme has not been delivered. I have asked my officials to present me with proposals and options for consideration as a matter of priority. No previous announcements have been made regarding how funding will be disbursed as part of the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) what responsibility he has for water quality in Lough Neagh; and (ii) what part agricultural effluent has played in the recent pollution of the lough.
