answer_text: My Department has responsibility for protecting the aquatic environment, including that in Lough Neagh, by regulating water quality and hydrological processes whilst considering the needs of industry, agriculture and the protection of public health.(ii)	Nutrient surplus entering the aquatic environment has been identified as one of the key drivers of water quality issues that led to the prolific blue green algae blooms in Lough Neagh in 2023. The RePhoKUs report (The Agri-Food and BioSciences Institute (AFBI), October 2020) attributed the source of the excess phosphorus entering the water environment in Northern Ireland as 62% from agriculture, 24% from wastewater treatment works and 12% from septic tanks.  Officials are working with AFBI to agree a programme of further scientific work for Lough Neagh, including the development and validation of models to apportion nutrient loading from agricultural and non-agricultural sources. Once finalised, I will ensure the data is available to all interested parties.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether she has any plans to review the current Northern Ireland legislation in relation to dogs that attack humans or other animals.
answer_text: The Dogs (Northern Ireland) Order 1983 (the Dogs Order) is a key piece of legislation on dog control. It specifies that is an offence to allow a dog to attack a person, livestock, or other animal owned by a person. The definition of attack includes the dog behaving in a way that a person becomes fearful of being attacked.Under the Dogs Order, if a dog has attacked a person, livestock or someone else’s animal, a dog warden may issue a notice restricting that dog. Restrictions could include requiring that the dog is muzzled when in public and kept confined when not in public.The suitability and capability of all legislation, including the Dogs Order, is something I intend to consider as and when evidence is presented for change.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether she has any plans to introduce legislation in relation to the XL Bully dog breed, similar the current legislation in England and Wales.
answer_text: A proportionate and effective way forward to deal with potentially dangerous dogs is a key issue for me.I recognise the need to move quickly in this area and I am keen to engage with a wide range of stakeholders, to hear their views, so that any decision I take will be based on the best evidence available.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the Casement Park project; and (ii) for his assessment of whether the project will be completed in time to host the 2028 European Football Championship.
answer_text: The redevelopment of Casement Park is a flagship project for the Executive. UEFA’s requirement, to have the project completed in time to host the EURO 2028 tournament, is for construction to be completed by June 2027. Officials in my Department and wider tournament stakeholders are working to an integrated project programme that can meet this requirement. This includes procurement of a new contractor for the main construction works using a UK Government Framework. The expression of interest stage has now concluded, and a short list of contractors has been identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans he has to improve public transport links to George Best Belfast City Airport.
answer_text: The All-Island Strategic Rail Review (AISRR), is considering how the rail network on the island of Ireland can improve sustainable connectivity between major cities, enhance regional accessibility, support balanced regional development and contribute to decarbonisation.Enhancing the integration of rail with other transport modes is one of 13 objectives within the Review. In support of this objective, the Review recommends improving existing rail-airport connections at George Best Belfast City Airport. Following closure of the AISRR public consultation in September 2023, work is ongoing to finalise the Review and its recommendations. Once the Review is published, further work will be required to prioritise the recommendations and then to undertake feasibility studies and develop business cases to support future investment decisions, including those in relation to rail connectivity to George Best airport. I can confirm that Translink has commenced a Feasibility Study that is considering options for improving the accessibility and connectivity between the railway network and the George Best Belfast City Airport. The study is due to be completed by the end of 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will consider the removal of the national speed limit from Orlock Road, Groomsport, and replace it with a 30mph speed limit.
answer_text: When considering the appropriate speed limit for a road the Department’s policy and procedures guide entitled “Setting Local Speed Limits in Northern Ireland” is used to ensure speed limits are consistent and motorists are presented with the same speed limit on similar roads taking into account factors such as: the characteristic of the road; the speed of existing traffic; the degree of frontage development; and road traffic collision data, which is continuously monitored using data provided by the PSNI.The current speed limit on Orlock Road is in line with our current policy document and considered appropriate.  Therefore, I do not propose to implement any speed restrictions at this location at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what plans he has to assist local business that rely on East-West trade, following the introduction of the Windsor Framework.
answer_text: Now that businesses finally have clarity on our trading relationships my Department will develop plans to grow trade on both a north-south and an east-west basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what is the projected number of police officers that will be available to the Chief Constable in (i) 2025; (ii) 2026; and (iii) 2027.
answer_text: Police officer numbers are a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board. You may therefore wish to direct your question to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what plans he has to assist the farming community to deal with rising operating costs.
answer_text: In 2023 direct payments continued to be paid earlier than normal and with a 2.32% increase to the Basic Payment component.  My Department continues to bring forward new policies and programmes designed to help farm businesses improve productivity, resilience and environmental sustainability. CAFRE continues to offer relevant training and support also.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the measures her Department has put in place to function without Ministerial leadership after 28 October 2022.
answer_text: Work has been ongoing within the Department to make preparations, where possible, in anticipation of a Minister not being in place from 28 October.   The absence of a Minister, however, would significantly hamper decision making to advance a wide range of priorities within the Department of Justice.  That includes decisions that are specified in statute as well as decisions that would ordinarily be taken by a Minister, such as public appointments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the impact a vacancy in the ministerial office after 28 October 2022 will have on (i) the morale of the health and social care workforce; and (ii) patient care.
answer_text: HSC staff remain committed to delivering high quality services to patients and service users.  However, as I have said before, and repeat again today, the absence of an Executive can only make it more difficult for us to transform and improve our services to meet the needs of NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has any plans for remedial action to address repeated flooding in Bangor at (i) Balloo Avenue; (ii) West Circular Road; (iii) the section of the Newtownards Road facing the Bangor Fire Station; and (iv) Newtownards Roundabout, Belfast exit.
answer_text: Unfortunately my officials were unable to gather together the information sought by the member in the short amount of time I had remaining in office.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the measures his Department has put in place to function without Ministerial leadership after 28 October 2022.
answer_text: Unfortunately, due to there being no Minister in post after midnight on the 27th of October, my Department will be unable to answer the following questions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the measures his Department has put in place to function without Ministerial leadership after 28 October 2022.
answer_text: The Department of Finance made arrangements to record decisions made by senior officers of the Department under the NI (Executive Formation etc.) Act 2022.  Officials also arranged to brief parties' finance spokespersons.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the measures his Department has put in place to function without Ministerial leadership after 28 October 2022.
answer_text: This question was asked in the previous mandate and to the previous Minister for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for her assessment of the measures her Department has put in place to function without Ministerial leadership after 28 October 2022.
answer_text: As the Executive has been restored this matter is no longer relevant.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the measures his Department has put in place to function without Ministerial leadership after 28 October 2022.
answer_text: Unfortunately my officials were unable to gather together the information sought by the member in the short amount of time I had remaining in office.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the measures his Department has put in place to function without Ministerial leadership after 28 October 2022.
answer_text: I have not undertaken any assessment as such measures could only be put in place should the Department be without Ministerial leadership after 28 October 2022.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for her assessment of the measures her Department has put in place to function without Ministerial leadership after 28 October 2022.
answer_text: I can confirm that in the absence of a Minister, my Department’s functions were exercised in accordance with the provisions of the Northern Ireland (Executive Formation etc) Act 2022 and associated guidance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in the absence of a monitoring round in September 2022, what assessment has been made of the amount of (i) revenue; and (ii) capital that would have been returned by her Department to the Department of Finance for redistribution.
answer_text: In the absence of a monitoring round my Department assessed the financial position and identified £0.5m ring-fenced revenue funding that would have been returned to the Department of Finance in September 2022. This easement relating to the Office of the Police Ombudsman NI (OPONI) budget allocation for Legacy Historical Investigations, has occurred as the NI Troubles Legacy & Reconciliation Bill has impacted their ability to recruit staff in 2022-23.More widely the Department is currently facing pressures of £85m in respect of Non-ring-fenced Resource DEL and £3.4m relating to Ring-fenced Resource DEL.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in the absence of a monitoring round in September 2022, what assessment has been made of the amount of (i) revenue; and (ii) capital that would have been returned by his Department to the Department of Finance for redistribution.
answer_text: (i) My Department is facing unprecedented resource pressures in 2022-23, as a result of the increase in energy prices and inflation, which remain extremely volatile. This is assessed against the contingency planning envelopes provided by the Department of Finance in the absence of an Executive agreed budget. In previous years my Department has relied on additional funding through the in-year monitoring process to enable continued delivery of essential services to ensure public health and safety. (ii) In relation to capital my Department continues to maximise capital expenditure in line with the capital allocations I agreed for 2022-23 in the absence of an Executive agreed budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, pursuant to AQW 4258/22-27, would the same assurances be given with regard to supplies between Northern Ireland and Great Britain.
answer_text: According to agreed protocols, SONI, the electricity system operator for Northern Ireland, may provide support to Great Britain to allow for continuity of supply. However, this support would not be provided if it meant that Northern Ireland would enter an emergency state as a result.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance, in the absence of a monitoring round in September 2022, what assessment has been made of the amount of (i) revenue; and (ii) capital that would have been returned by his Department for redistribution.
answer_text: Based on my Department’s budget position at September 2022 no resource (revenue) or capital would have been returned for redistribution.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in the absence of a monitoring round in September 2022, what assessment has been made of the amount of (i) revenue; and (ii) capital that would have been returned by her Department to the Department of Finance for redistribution.
answer_text: The Secretary of State for Northern Ireland set the 2022-23 Budget for Northern Ireland on 24 November 2022.  In September 2022, in the absence of a budget, no monitoring round assessment by my Department was possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, in the absence of a monitoring round in September 2022, what assessment has been made of the amount of (i) revenue; and (ii) capital that would have been returned by his Department to the Department of Finance for redistribution.
answer_text: In the absence of a monitoring round, no formal assessment is carried out in that context. However, my Department has continued to monitor its budget and Officials submit an updated financial position to the Department of Finance through the monthly Forecast Outturn exercise.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in the absence of a monitoring round in September 2022, what assessment has been made of the amount of (i) revenue; and (ii) capital that would have been returned by his Department to the Department of Finance for redistribution.
answer_text: In the absence of a formal Monitoring Round in September 2022, my Department undertook an internal assessment at that time and no Resource DEL or Capital DEL would have been returned to the Department of Finance if there had been an exercise to do so.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in the absence of a monitoring round in September 2022, what assessment has been made of the amount of (i) revenue; and (ii) capital that would have been returned by his Department to the Department of Finance for redistribution.
answer_text: As you will be aware the Department of Health has yet to be provided a budget due to the lack of a functioning assembly and without a budget it is not possible to make an assessment of the budget position for a monitoring round in September 2022 without making several significant assumptions, as detailed below: (i) Regarding revenue, based on a number of assumptions such as the draft budget and contingency planning envelope to determine a revenue funding position, there would have been no funding available from my Department for redistribution by the Department of Finance. Indeed, it is likely that my Department would have bid for funding had there been a monitoring round in September 2022 given the significant funding gap facing the Health and Social Care system. (ii) As regards capital, £2.5m of ring-fenced capital funding would have been available from my Department for redistribution by the Department of Finance. This assessment is also based on the Draft Budget allocation and similarly my Department would have bid for additional capital funding and sought permission to retain the £2.5m of ring-fenced funding to progress the Department’s capital programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in the absence of a monitoring round in September 2022, what assessment has been made of the amount of (i) revenue; and (ii) capital that would have been returned by her Department to the Department of Finance for redistribution.
answer_text: Given the significant pressures faced by the Department at that time, had there been a monitoring round in September 2022, the Department would not have been in a position to return any funding to the Department of Finance for redistribution. Rather, the Department would have been bidding for additional resources. The Education sector has continued to face extensive unavoidable cost pressures and rising service demands associated with delivering statutory and policy obligations and prior to the agreement of the Financial package from HM Treasury, was facing a resource funding gap of c£300 million and a capital shortfall of c£43 million in 2023-24. Whilst these shortfalls for 2023-24 have now been addressed the budget position for 2024-25 has not yet been set and Education will require substantially more if we are to avoid a significant and detrimental impact on our children and young people.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to provide an update on his original answer to AQW 3395/17-22.
answer_text: Since 13 May 2020 my Department has received ongoing updates from the Utility Regulator (UR) at key stages of its review of SONI’s governance arrangements. These updates have included advance notification of consultation publication dates and associated reports of findings. Departmental records show that a meeting took place between the then newly appointed Chief Executive and the Department on 23 March 2021 and that the U provided an update on SONI Governance at that meeting.The Department has not been involved in any other discussions or negotiations with any of the listed groups in relation to SONI governance.The UR is an independent body responsible for regulating the electricity industry in Northern Ireland and its work on the review of SONI governance is a matter for it in line with its statutory duties. The Department does not have any powers to intervene in the role of SONI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he has (i) held discussions with; or (ii) made representations to Tourism NI in regard to their funding of the North West 200.
answer_text: This question was asked in the previous mandate and to the previous Minister for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department, or the Arms Length Bodies within his Department's focus, can provide the value and additional income that the North West 200 brings to the local economy.
answer_text: The most recent independent evaluation of the North West 200 event commissioned by Tourism NI was completed in 2017. This evaluation confirmed that the event makes an important contribution to the tourism economy in Northern Ireland. In 2017, the event generated 78,500 visitors, of which 26% were visitors from outside of Northern Ireland, as well as 35,000 commercial bed nights. The total economic impact of the event in Northern Ireland in that year was calculated to be £5.453m. Tourism NI also recognises that the North West 200 event also generates benefits and impacts which were not able to be captured for in the 2017 evaluation. These benefits include non-commercial overnight stays in campsites and caravans. More recent data is held by Tourism NI on the PR impact of the North West 200. In 2019, the North West 200 generated global exposure with a PR/Media value of £10.3m and a total of 14.5m social media views.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to provide an update to his original answer to AQW 3394/17-22, highlighting the changes in each section of his answer.
answer_text: This question was asked in the previous mandate and to the previous Minister for the Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he is preparing advice for domestic and non-domestic electricity users on what steps they should take in the event of planned or unplanned black-outs.
answer_text: As a responsible government department, DfE collaborates each year with the energy industry to ensure that robust contingency planning is in place.  An agreed and coordinated Communications Strategy forms part of this contingency planning. It details the messaging required in the unlikely event of supply disruption.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the money allocated for a Bangor to Holywood Greenway is contingent on the creation of a commuter route for cyclists.
answer_text: As the member may be aware, the development of greenways is the responsibility of local Councils rather than my Department.  However I am keen to support the development of greenways and have invited councils to submit applications for grant in support of greenways and active travel generally.  It is expected that all greenways should accommodate both walking and cycling.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, following reports of possible electricity blackouts in Great Britain and the Republic of Ireland, what reassurances can he give that Northern Ireland will not be affected.
answer_text: Security of supply is a key priority for my Department. DfE officials are in regular contact with the Utility Regulator, SONI and relevant energy industry stakeholders to seek reasurrances regarding NI’s position and possible impacts of a supply disruption elsewhere. 		I can provide reassurance that robust risk planning and winter preparedness procedures are in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what reassurances he can provide to (i) domestic; and (ii) non-domestic electricity users in Northern Ireland that they will not face either planned or unplanned blackouts this winter.
answer_text: Security of energy supply is a key priority for the Department. Each year DfE engages with energy industry stakeholders to identify potential risks to security of energy supply and to ensure sufficient mitigations are in place to protect both gas and electricity security of supply. This ensures that both domestic and non-domestic electricity users are sufficiently protected from supply disruption. I can provide reassurance that robust risk planning and winter preparedness procedures are in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy considering the current nature of the all-island electricity supply structure, both physical and managerial, what measures are in place to ensure that electricity supply in Northern Ireland will not be reduced to ensure the continuity of supply to Republic of Ireland customers and users.
answer_text: In the event of a supply emergency there are contingency protocols between the three electricity system operators in Northern Ireland, Republic of Ireland and Great Britain to provide support to one another. If this provision of support were to lead to a supply disruption within the supporting jurisdiction support would not be provided. In essence, Northern Ireland will not experience supply disruption in order to provide the Republic of Ireland with  continuity of supply.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when he has met with (i) electricity suppliers; (ii) the regulator; (iii) businesses; and (iv) the Consumer Council to discuss the contingencies that would be needed if there are electricity black-outs either planned or unplanned in Northern Ireland this winter.
answer_text: DfE is in regular contact with the Utility Regulator, SONI, (the electricity transmission system operator) and NIE Networks (the distribution system operator) to ensure that robust contingency protocols are in place in the event of both planned and unplanned disconnection. 		There are established procedures to ensure that the relevant wider energy industry including electricity suppliers, businesses and consumers are informed and engaged when a disconnection event takes place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what plans he has to support the hospitality sector in rural areas.
answer_text: I relation to support for the hospitality sector, I refer the Member to my previous response AQW 3529/22-27. More generally, in terms of my ambition for inclusive, regionally-balanced growth, nowhere is this more evident than Project Stratum. As part of the 2017 Conservative and DUP Confidence and Supply Agreement, £150m was secured to make Northern Ireland 100% fibre to the premises. The scheme rolling out the funding is called Project Stratum and is proving to be transformational for rural communities.Project Stratum is managed by my Department and is making gigabit capable broadband available to 85,000 primarily rural homes and businesses without access to a service of 30 Mbps. Where funding becomes available my Department would like to improve connectivity further, in line with the UK Government’s ambitions under Project Gigabit to ensure that every home and business across the UK has access to gigabit-capable infrastructure, regardless of location. It is expected that the majority of premises eligible to benefit from such an intervention would also be located in predominantly rural areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how much her Department has spent on Affordable Warmth Schemes so far this year, compared to this point in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has advised that the spend on the Affordable Warmth Scheme to 30 Sept 22, compared with the spend in the previous five years, is as follows: Affordable Warmth SchemeFull Year Expenditure(1st April to 31st March)Expenditure(1st April to 30th  Sept)£k£k2017/1818,2467,6602018/1914,5426,4112019/2012,3314,1172020/216,6771,3882021/2211,8165,8112022/23The indicative budget allocation for 2022/23 is £16M7,200

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for his assessment of the delays of rateable valuation reviews being conducted.
answer_text: LPS aims to complete requests received from ratepayers for reviews of rateable values within three months.  Regrettably, there has been significant disruption to normal working practices over the past two years as a result of the Covid-19 pandemic.  In addition, during 2022/23 LPS has had to prioritise its resources to completing the non-domestic revaluation, which will ensure businesses are paying rates from April 2023 on valuations which take account of the impact of the pandemic and other economic factors. This set of circumstances has resulted in it taking longer than LPS would wish for ratepayer requests for a review of their valuation to be completed.  LPS is working hard to deal with applications for reviews as quickly as possible

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what locations in North Down were in receipt of funding from the Rural Halls Refurbishment Scheme.
answer_text: The Rural Halls Refurbishment Scheme closed for applications in June 2021 with 214 applications received of which one was from the North Down electoral constituency.  To date from the 114 eligible applications, 73 Letters of Offer have issued offering grant totalling £2.483m.  Seven eligible applicants withdrew from the Scheme for varying reasons; therefore, it is anticipated that 34 further Letters of Offer to the value of £1.217m will issue before the end of this calendar year to the remaining eligible applicants. The one application located in the North Down constituency, Morning Star Masonic Lodge in Millisle, is anticipated to receive a Letter of Offer through this process.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for his assessment of the main factors behind the delays in the planning application process; and (ii) what his Department is doing to reduce these delays.
answer_text: There are a number of factors which impact the performance of the planning system for example; the quality of applications received, increasing complexities associated with environmental issues, delays around statutory consultation, and increased legal challenges are just some of the many issues which can cause delays.The impact of a decade of austerity on public services and staffing levels also has to be taken into consideration when looking at planning performance.My Department has been working on a number of fronts to improve the system, including the establishment of a cross-departmental Planning Forum to take forward recommendations from a report into the role of statutory consultees in the planning process. My Department has also undertaken a review of the implementation of the Planning Act (NI) 2011, which has identified areas where we could further improve performance of the system for all stakeholders – including councils, developers, and the wider public, and work on this will be progressing in the coming months. My Department and local government have also been working together to deliver a new regional planning IT system, which will also allow for the more efficient processing of applications and bring a range of benefits to the wider planning system.I recognise that improving the performance of the planning system is very challenging and I also recognise that more can be done collectively between key stakeholders, and I have therefore, asked my officials to bring forward an ambitious planning improvement programme, in partnership with local government, which will involve changes to legislation, policy and practice, to secure these improvements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, further to recent media reports, for his assessment of his Department’s spending on photography.
answer_text: Photography is an essential part of Government and Ministerial communications. My Department has a diverse and portfolio with responsibility for food, farming, environmental, fisheries, forestry and sustainable policy and the development of the rural sector across the whole of Northern Ireland. Each of these sectors has been impacted greatly by the pandemic, war in Ukraine and rising cost of living, food, supplies and fuel as well as the ongoing avian flu season. Following Covid-19, DAERA had a considerable backlog of engagements with a large number of stakeholders on a wide range of issues affecting their sectors. In my role, it is often necessary to visit rural locations throughout Northern Ireland. This means that photographers are required for extended periods of time. It is also only to be expected that these costs will be higher in the years when Ministers were in office.  Due to the wide-ranging scope of my Department, photographers are also required for a number of non-Ministerial events across all areas of DAERA. This includes promotion of education, knowledge transfer and innovation programmes at the College of Agriculture, Food and Rural Enterprise (CAFRE) and work of the Northern Ireland Environment Agency.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of why there has been a rise in the number of Northern Ireland students dropping out of university.
answer_text: Recent figures published by the Student Loans Company (SLC) show that student withdrawal notifications for academic year 2021/22 are higher than the previous 2 academic years. This recent increase in withdrawals applies across the UK and not just in Northern Ireland. However, the SLC report also indicates that while Northern Ireland student withdrawal notifications for 2021/22 are higher when compared with 2019/20 and 2020/21, they are broadly in line with the (pre-pandemic) figure for 2018/19. The number of Northern Ireland and other UK domiciled students dropping out of higher education courses during the pandemic was unusually low. The Higher Education Statistics Agency (HESA) suggests there are likely to be multi-faceted reasons underpinning this decrease, rather than a single, direct explanation for the drop. However, during periods of economic uncertainty, there is often a trend for increased higher education enrolments. The most recent increase is therefore likely to be a ‘normalising’ of the withdrawal rates following the pandemic.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) whether her Department has been able to to fill all vacancies left by the 357 workers who have resigned from their post in the Northern Ireland Housing Executive since 2018; and (ii) to detail if exit interviews revealed a trend in reasons for these resignations.
answer_text: The recruitment of staff to the Housing Executive is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. My Department is not responsible for filling vacancies in the Housing Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what financial assistance her Department will provide to schools facing rising costs in energy bills.
answer_text: I can assure you that I fully recognise the challenges that rising heating and electricity costs present for our schools, and my officials seek regular updates from the Education Authority (EA) in relation to this. The most recent update estimated an additional pressure of £13.3m relating to rises in natural gas, heating oil and electricity costs in the 2022-23 financial year. This was after factoring in the anticipated impact of the UK government’s recent announcement of an Energy Bill Relief Scheme for the period October 2022 to March 2023. These, and other pressures have been communicated to the Department of Finance in anticipation of an agreed budget for 2022-23 in due course. In addition to this, since August 2020, the EA has proactively sought to mitigate the impact of increasing utility and energy costs by engaging an independent energy advisor to provide advice on purchasing energy, tariff changes and hedging purchase prices for electricity and gas on behalf of our schools. Notwithstanding all this, if any school is facing financial difficultly, I would encourage them to contact the EA, as funding authority for grant aided schools, in the normal way.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 2876/22-27, to detail the options that are available to provide immediate interventions to address the cost of living crisis.
answer_text: I remain deeply frustrated that the continued absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget prevents additional measures being introduced. My Department will continue to do all it can to support those experiencing hardship, and all options are being explored to provide both immediate and medium to longer term interventions.My officials have worked collaboratively across departments to provide information on all current support available. This information is now published and available at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/campaigns/cost-living

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the impact that the recent Westminster mini-Budget will have in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The recent announcement by the Chancellor of the Exchequer marked a significant fiscal intervention by the UK Government.  A number of the measures proposed will be directly applicable in Northern Ireland, such as the reduction in the basic rate of income tax, the revisions to Corporation Tax rates, the changes to Stamp Duty and the reversal of the proposed increase in National Insurance.  As with all fiscal events, the full implications will take time to assess and I and my Officials will continue to review the detail and potential implications for the Northern Ireland economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what plans he has to assist farmers with the cost of living crisis and rising energy costs during this incoming winter period.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment of the current budgetary position of the PSNI; and (ii) to detail what actions she will take to address current budget shortfalls.
answer_text: The financial position for the justice system as a whole is extremely challenging. PSNI have provided my Department with regular updates of the in-year pressures they are currently facing which, as with all parts of the system, are significant. Those pressures are further exacerbated by the absence of an agreed Budget and of an Executive, without which there is no opportunity either to benefit from reallocation of resources during monitoring rounds or to disburse moneys held centrally as a result of Barnett consequentials to address in-year pressures, as would normally be the case. Both I and my Department have set out clearly to the Department of Finance and to the Executive parties, the serious consequences to policing and the wider justice system of the current shortfall in funding; namely, a direct impact on service delivery to the public.Ultimately, the prioritisation of policing resources is an operational matter for the Chief Constable who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. I am committed to respecting his operational independence and the role of the Policing Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment on the factors that have led to a freezing of PSNI recruitment; and (ii) what plans she has to address the impact of this freeze on the delivery of front line policing services.
answer_text: In the absence of an Executive and a budget, the financial position for the justice system as a whole, including PSNI, is extremely challenging.  I have been clear from the outset that I cannot support the 2022-25 draft budget, which will neither enable the retention of current police officer numbers nor meet the New Decade New Approach (NDNA) commitment.  I have made my views clear to my Executive colleagues on this matter, and repeatedly stressed that delivery of the NDNA commitment is dependent upon the availability of appropriate levels of funding.I am aware that the Chief Constable is having ongoing discussions with the Policing Board around the detail of the potential impact of reduced numbers upon service delivery.  I will continue to engage with the Chief Constable on a regular basis whilst respecting his operational independence and will continue to call for adequate funding for policing and the justice system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when the findings and advice from the Agri-food Rising Costs Industry Taskforce Autumn Survey will be released to help guide the agri-food sector during the winter months.
answer_text: The Autumn Survey developed by UFU and AgriSearch, supported by the Rising Costs Industry Taskforce closed on 16 September 2022.  This extensive survey was developed with input from Taskforce members with the aim of informing Knowledge Transfer (KT) messages over the coming months.  The results were presented to Taskforce members on Friday 30 September and CAFRE advisory messages will now be developed to address the main issues arising from the results.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what action his Department plans to take to assist takeaway restaurants in dealing with the pressures caused by the cost of fuel and the cost of living crisis.
answer_text: I am acutely aware of the pressure that businesses are facing across the board, primarily as a result of soaring wholesale energy costs associated with Russia’s invasion of Ukraine. In the last year, since September 2021, the wholesale cost of natural gas has trebled (+194%), while the cost of coal (+104%) and home heating oil (+107%) has doubled. Coupled with ongoing global supply chain disruptions, these unprecedented increases in energy costs are driving inflation across the economy, including input cost pressures for small businesses. I met recently with the Northern Ireland Takeaway Association to hear first-hand how the sector is managing these pressures, including through reduced opening hours and unfortunately in some more limited cases closure.As we are seeing across Europe and beyond, the magnitude of these global inflationary pressures means that it can only be tackled meaningfully by significant interventions at a national government level. That is why in recent weeks and months I have taken every opportunity to engage with UK Government to highlight the inflationary pressures being felt by households and businesses in Northern Ireland. Following these engagements, I welcome the UK Government’s commitment to deliver the much needed £400 Energy Support Payment Scheme in Northern Ireland. In addition, the announcement by the Prime Minister of a domestic Energy Price Guarantee and a non-domestic Energy Bill Relief Scheme – and her commitment that the same level of support will be made available in Northern Ireland via parallel schemes. This relief will give households and businesses a degree of certainty with their bills over the coming months.  My Department will continue to engage with the UK Government and others to ensure these support measures reach Northern Ireland households and businesses as soon as possible.The outlook remains challenging but I am hopeful for the future. I am investing in those areas where we can gain the best long-term outcomes, which will help us build greater economic resilience for the future.  For example, I have published an energy strategy which will deliver a step change in how we move away from our reliance on overseas, imported fossil fuels. Alongside our investment in skills this will support the delivery of our ambitious 10X Economic Vision for the benefit of all our people.In practical terms, Invest Northern Ireland’s Energy and Resource Efficiency Support for businesses provides a suite of support, including specialist advice and investment capital, to enable local businesses to become more efficient and resilient through green efficiencies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the impact that the proposed ferry service between Bangor and Belfast will have on road traffic between Bangor and Belfast.
answer_text: I fully support the concept of a 100% electric passenger ferry sailing between Belfast and Bangor on the Belfast Lough.  However, it is not clear at this time what impact the ferry would have on traffic between Belfast and Bangor and to enable this to be assessed and understood, more information around capacity and the frequency of the service, as well as terminal locations and parking capacity, would be required. This information would be required as part of any planning application process at which time my Department would become formally involved as a statutory consultee role to the Planning process. Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at: dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the Department for Work and Pension's extension to existing Personal Independence Payment awards that are approaching the end of their award date, will apply in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: My Department has measures in place to monitor PIP awards to ensure they are reviewed ahead of the award end date. As an additional safeguard, the Department will extend Personal Independence Payment awards that are approaching their end of award date.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department factored in the two major sporting events and national conference that were taking place in Belfast on 24 September 2022, when taking the decision to close a section of the Sydenham Bypass to facilitate resurfacing work.
answer_text: My Department carefully planned the road works and associated traffic management arrangements on Sydenham Bypass in order to minimise inconvenience to the public. There is no ideal time to undertake works of this scale and some disruption is always inevitable.  Throughout the calendar year, there are many major events held across Belfast and it would not be feasible to complete extensive resurfacing works on a road like Sydenham Bypass without impacting on some events.When works of this type are taking place it is important that road users allow extra time for their journey and follow the signed diversions in place.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at: dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the outworking of the traffic management plans that were put in place to facilitate the closure of part of the Sydenham Bypass for resurfacing work on 24 September 2022.
answer_text: Resurfacing is a valuable public service that in many cases requires a road closure to allow works to be completed safely – there is no ideal time to carry out works of this scale which will inevitably cause congestion given traffic volumes on this part of the network.The works were scheduled for the weekend and overnight when traffic volumes are much reduced in comparison to weekday traffic volumes. Traffic management arrangements were publicised on social media and through partner organisations such as Belfast City Airport.Given the disruption experienced by many road users over the first weekend of the works, my Department met with contractors to review the traffic management arrangements to see if improvements can be made for the weekends of 30 September - 3 October, 710 October, 1417 October and 2124 October when further works are planned. As a result, increased signage and additional cones will be used to encourage vehicles to use the planned diversion along the Sydenham Road instead of the Newtownards Road. My Department is also engaging with our contractor to see if would be possible to safely open access to the M3 bridge from the Westlink to the Sydenham Road for future phases and the public will be informed accordingly.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Eastern Division at: dfiroads.eastern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the impact that an ageing population will have on the economy of Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The latest NISRA population estimates expect 2027 to be first year that the number of people aged over 65 exceeds the number of children in the Northern Ireland population. This will of course have an impact on the relative size of the working age population - in 2020, 62% of Northern Ireland’s population was of working age (aged 16-64), and it is estimated that by 2045 this will have fallen to 59%. Meanwhile, those aged 65 and above will have increased as a proportion of the local population, from 17% to 25% of NI’s population, by 2045. An ageing population presents a range of challenges, including increased health and social care costs and an increase in the dependency ratio, as our economy is faced with a smaller pool of working age people supporting a greater number of people of non-working age. However, as highlighted in the 10X Economic Vision, our economy must prepare itself to take advantage of the opportunities these challenges present.  With an ageing and growing population, we can capitalise on the opportunity for increased demand for remote and digital medical solutions for an ageing population with more complex needs.  Northern Ireland has a strong base of market leading companies to build from, with a significant number of private enterprises associated with diagnostics, and our universities which are in the top 10 in the UK for bioscience research.In meeting the challenges of an aging population, skills are vital. It is important that we improve our rates of lifelong learning so that those within the working age population have the skills to take advantage of new working processes and technologies, as well as being able to move into new areas of work - older working age adults in NI tend to be lower qualified compared with their younger counterparts, and are also less likely to participate in lifelong learning to gain further qualifications.A range of free accredited training and qualifications have been made available via the DfE supported Skill Up programme which is available to all adults eligible to work in NI. There are up to 7,000 places available until the end of June 2023 with more to be confirmed to March 2024, offering an opportunity to train, re-train and re-skill. An ageing population presents us with economic challenges but also opportunities, opportunities which our economy needs to be ready to make the most of, as this global and local shift in population occurs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 2698/22-27, whether her Department has carried out an internal review of how funding for the Sub-Regional Stadia Progamme for Soccer will be allocated.
answer_text: I asked my Department’s officials to review the Programme to provide an up-to-date robust evidence base on the challenges, strategic priorities and both the current and the future needs for soccer at all levels.  The challenges created by not having a functioning Executive mean that I cannot bring proposals for endorsement and agreement as required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 3036/22-27, (i) what powers his Department has to remove unlawful items carrying advertising from public roads or property in the ownership of his Department; and (ii) how many occasions during the last year his Department has secured such removals, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: My Department has powers under Article 87 of the Roads (NI) Order 1993 to remove advertising signage erected illegally on Departmental property, including the surface of a road or upon any tree, structure or other works in or on a road. However, given the significant budgetary constraints my Department is operating under, this work is considered lower priority than other functions, many of which are road safety related, that are funded from my Department’s finite resource allocation. As such my Department currently only acts to remove items where they are positioned in such a way that it is considered to represent a risk to road safety.If my officials become aware of an advertising trailer creating an obstruction or road hazard, they seek to make contact with the owner of the trailer and request that it is removed or, depending on the circumstances, may notify the PSNI that it is creating an obstruction of the road under Article 88 of the Roads (NI) Order.My Department does not maintain formal records of occasions when such removals have been actioned.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 512/22-27, how many gullies have been inspected and remedial clearance carried out in North Down, in the last six months.
answer_text: My Department does not maintain records of the total number of gullies inspected and cleared on a responsive basis.  However, I can confirm that so far during the current financial year some 15,500 gullies have been cleaned on a scheduled basis within the Ards and North Down Council area. My Department does not collate or manage information on a constituency basis, rather information is managed on a Council or DfI Roads Section Office area basis. Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at: dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether the changes made to the Great Britain version of the Highway Code, introduced last year, have been made to the Northern Ireland version; and (ii) to detail what plans there are to incorporate the changes into the Northern Ireland Highway Code.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Highway Code is the official guide to using our roads safely and legally, and it is the responsibility of every road user to be aware of its contents for the safety of themselves and other road users. Road safety is a fully devolved matter, and my Department considers the implications of policy and legislative changes implemented elsewhere.  As Minister I take my responsibility for road safety very seriously, and I have to consider the best way to use available resources to meet the needs of all road users.  Possible updates to the Code first need to be considered to ensure they reflect local needs.  Any changes then need to be implemented safely, supported by clear communications to ensure they are understood by the wide range of road users, including pedestrians.  I am therefore unable to give a timescale for the update of the Highway Code at this point.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the main findings from the joint workshop to overcome challenges to delivering more homes.
answer_text: My draft Housing Supply Strategy recognises a number of interconnected barriers to delivery of the supply we need. The workshop jointly hosted by my Department and the Department for Infrastructure was held specifically to examine the issue of wastewater infrastructure. A range of key stakeholders and delivery partners attended the event and discussion was wide ranging, focusing on both potential strategic and tactical solutions. My officials are currently preparing a report for review by Minister O’Dowd and myself on the outcome of these discussions and to detail thinking on potential actions to be taken forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many traffic collisions have taken place at the junction of the Ballymiscaw Road and Whinney Hill, Craigantlet in the last twelve months and how many of these involved injuries.
answer_text: I can confirm that the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) is the authority responsible for gathering information on road traffic collisions.It shares periodically with my Department relevant data on collisions where injuries have been sustained and this is used to help identify viable engineering measures aimed at reducing the likelihood and impact of future collisions. However, as the information you have requested belongs to the PSNI, you should direct your request for information about collisions to its Freedom of Information team, who can be contacted at the following address:PSNI (Freedom of Information Team)Corporate Development65 Knock Road, BELFASTBT5 6LEEmail; foi@psni.pnn.police.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what redress is available to a claimant who has had a compensation claim for alleged damage to their vehicle by a road defect rejected by his Department.
answer_text: As set out at [G] of the Vehicle Damage Compensation Form and on the NI Direct website under the page titled “Complaining about how a roads-related claim was handled”, a review of the Department’s decision on a claim can be requested. In response, officials will review a decision to turn down a claim, however, new evidence should be provided to justify the review. If a claimant is still unhappy after the review and wishes to challenge the liability decision, the right of recourse is through legal proceedings in court, within appropriate time limits.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has any plans to regulate advertising trailers parked on public roads.
answer_text: Advertising on a public road without lawful authority is prohibited under Article 87 of the Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993 and my Department currently has no plans to introduce further regulation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment as to what extent the statistics from the Electricity Consumption Renewable Generation in Northern Ireland: Year Ending June 2022 Report put Northern Ireland on track to meet the set goals for renewable energy sources.
answer_text: The Energy Strategy for Northern Ireland, “A Path to Net Zero Energy”, published in December 2021, has set the policy direction for increasing renewable energy across Northern Ireland and achieving a target of at least 70% of electricity consumed coming from a diverse range of renewable sources by 2030 (since raised to 80% by the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022).		The latest report for the period July 2021 - June 2022, issued on 8 September by NISRA, states that for this 12 month period 47.1% of the total electricity consumed in Northern Ireland was generated from renewable sources in Northern Ireland. This represents a increase of 1.7 percentage points over the previous 12 month period (July 2020 – June 2021).	This is encouraging as my Department works towards delivering the Energy Strategy and replacing fossil fuels with renewable energy by growing our own indigenous renewable base. Whilst the 80% target by 2030 will be challenging, the current level of electricty generated from renewables is welcome news as we continue to move forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department has plans to support the setting up of further banking hubs in Northern Ireland to compensate for local branch closures in rural areas.
answer_text: I have been pressing the Treasury, the regulatory authorities and the local banks to ensure access to cash and local services are protected, particularly in the case of vulnerable citizens and those in rural areas.The recent announcement that a banking hub is planned for Kilkeel is welcome and I would urge the local banks to work together to ensure this is delivered as quickly as possible given the slow roll out of previously announced hubs in Britain.  The regulation of Financial Services is a reserved matter for the British Government and its regulatory authorities and any decisions around further banking hubs is a matter for the Cash Action Group who are responsible for rolling out the hubs across Britain and the North, based on independent assessments undertaken by LINK. I will continue to engage with local banks and stakeholders to encourage them to work together to ensure all communities in the North have access to the banking services that they need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department have plans to review the entry requirements for taxi drivers, which were introduced in 2013.
answer_text: The process of obtaining a taxi driver licence is prescribed in legislation. The Taxi Driver Theory Test was introduced for new drivers in October 2014, to help improve standards in the industry, increase road safety, provide additional assurance to passengers and raise the reputation of the industry as a provider of a quality service. There are currently no plans to review the entry requirements to become a taxi driver. However you may wish to note that officials are currently reviewing the available training material, with a view to helping candidates better prepare for the taxi driver theory test. The training manual, “All you need to know to be a Taxi Driver”, which is available on NIDirect, is under review to create a more streamlined, user-friendly version. As a result a revision aid has been developed, based on the information contained in the training manual.  This revision aid will soon be available through NIDirect and will help to support candidates in acquiring the knowledge needed for the taxi theory test.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) what plans his Department has to assess the need for additional road safety measures at the junction of the Ballymiscaw Road and Whinney Hill, Craigantlet; and (ii) whether his Department have plans to repaint the fading road markings at this junction.
answer_text: (i) My Department has implemented a range of measures at this location as part of a previous collision remedial scheme which included installation of enhanced warning and directional signs as well as laying stretches of high friction surfacing on the main approaches to the Craigantlet Crossroads, specifically Craigantlet Road, Holywood Road, Dunlady Road and Whinney Hill. These measures have enhanced driver awareness, helped to reduce driver speeds and improved overall road safety on this busy part of the road network.Any further substantial capital investment to upgrade the junction will be subject to consideration of competing priorities and also dependent on the capital budget allocation in future years.(ii) I can confirm that a scheme was recently completed involving a refresh of all of the road markings and high friction surfacing on the main approaches to Craigantlet Crossroads.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at: dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline what updates he has received on the rollout of the £400 Energy Bill Support Scheme in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The British Government has confirmed that it will deliver the £400 Energy Bill Support Scheme here in the North.I made it clear to the Chancellor and Secretary of State for Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy that any delay in issuing these payments to citizens here is completely unacceptable. Despite this, the British Government has yet to provide an update how or when people here will receive the payment.I have therefore written to the Chief Secretary to Treasury seeking clarification on both how and when households will receive not only the £400 payment, but also the additional energy support measures announced on 8 September.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the progress of the Casement Park project.
answer_text: The redevelopment of Casement Park is a flagship project for the Executive and the completion of the Regional Stadia Programme is a commitment in the New Decade New Approach. Following the outcome of the Judicial Review which upheld the Department for Infrastructure’s decision to approve Planning Permission for the redevelopment of Casement Park, my officials and I will continue to, Work with the UCGAA on the next steps for this New Decade New Approach commitment.Engage with UCGAA to progress the revised Full Business Case through the necessary approval processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the appointment of a permanent gully cleaning contractor in North Down.
answer_text: The procurement process to appoint a new Contractor for the Term Contract for Environmental Maintenance in the Newry, Mourne & Down, and North Down & Ards council areas is well advanced.  A contract award is currently expected in October 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the reasons why Northern Ireland is experiencing an increase in the cost of living.
answer_text: Like nations and regions across the world, Northern Ireland is experiencing unprecedented increases in the cost of living driven by a perfect storm of internationally-driven factors. The cumulative impact of disruption associated with the Covid pandemic and the Russian invasion of Ukraine are key drivers of the inflationary pressures being felt by housholds and businesses. 		Nowhere are these pressures being felt more accutely than in the soaring cost of energy. In the last year, since September 2021, the wholesale cost of natural gas has trebled (+194%), while the cost of coal (+104%) and home heating oil (+107%) has doubled. These unprecedented increases in energy costs are driving inflation across the economy.		In terms of spirling energy costs, which present a challenge across the board, I welcome the announcement by the Prime Minister of an Energy Price Guarantee and her commitment that the same level of support will be made available in Northern Ireland. This relief will give households and businesses a degree of certainty with their energy bills over the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the schools in North Down that have a 20mph speed limit in place; and (ii) his Department's plans to put such schemes in place at other school locations in North Down.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department has already introduced or is in the process of implementing part-time 20 mph speed limits that will benefit the following 17 schools within the Ards and North Down Council area:Tranche 1 – completed in 2021  Abbey PS (Newtownards); Andrews Memorial PS (Comber); Carrowdore PS (Newtownards); Kirkistown PS (Portavogie); Loughries PS (Newtownards); and St Patrick’s PS (Portaferry).Tranche 2 – in the process of being completed in 2022 Ballyvester PS (Donaghadee); Carrickmannon PS (Ballygowan); Clandeboye PS (Bangor); Donaghadee PS; Greyabbey PS; Millisle PS; Rathmore PS (Bangor); Priory College (Holywood); St Malachy’s PS (Bangor); St Patrick’s PS (Holywood); and Sullivan Upper PS (Holywood).When all of the tranche 2 sites are fully operational, my Department intends to carry out a review of this work.  Once this review is completed, I will then consider if a further roll out of 20mph speed limits at schools will be taken forward. Should you have any further questions on matter, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department is giving consideration to introducing residents parking permits in North Down.
answer_text: The Department has recently completed a review of residents’ parking schemes. I have had sight of the draft review report, and plan to make it public once it is finalised.  The review report will set out the lessons learnt from the Rugby Road scheme in Belfast, and the other schemes that did not make it to implementation, and will be used to establish the policy for such schemes going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what plans her Department has to assist the public with the cost of living crisis.
answer_text: The cost-of-living crisis is having major impact, particularly on those who are already struggling financially. I am deeply frustrated that the continued absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budget prevents additional measures being introduced.My Department will do all it can to support those experiencing hardship, and all options are being explored to provide both immediate and medium to longer term interventions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether her Department is considering a realignment of the previously agreed formula for sharing out the Sub-Regional Stadia Progamme for Soccer funding.
answer_text: The challenges created by not having a functioning Executive mean that I cannot bring proposals for endorsement and agreement as required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer funding will be paid out under the previously announced formula once Executive sign off is achieved.
answer_text: The challenges created by not having a functioning Executive mean that I cannot bring proposals for endorsement and agreement as required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) how many MOT tests have been carried out at each test centre in the first six months of 2022; and (ii) to detail how many available slots were not taken up during the same period at each test centre.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency is now conducting vehicle tests at similar volumes to pre-Covid levels and continues to work hard to increase its vehicle testing capacity.  Official vehicle testing statistics are published on a monthly basis and can be accessed at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/driver-and-vehicle-agency-monthly-tests-conducted-statistics The number of customers who had booked an available slot but failed to attend their appointment without prior cancellation is detailed in the published quarterly statistics at DfI Driver, Vehicle, Operator, and Enforcement Statistics – 2021-22 Q4 – Tables in tabs 1.8 and 1.9.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what percentage of her Departmental staff are currently working from home.
answer_text: Information on the percentage of staff working from home is not recorded.The change to guidance, on 6 June 2022, on working from home creates the circumstances for the implementation of the Northern Ireland Civil Service (NICS) Hybrid Working policy.  This will allow more staff to return to the workplace, with the potential for greater flexibility of location, where roles allow and should staff wish to do so, depending on business need and service delivery.My Department will progress the implementation of the policy over the coming weeks with a greater balance of office and home/remote working where this best meets the organisation’s needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what percentage of his departmental staff are currently working from home.
answer_text: I would refer you to my answer to AQW 534/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what percentage of her Departmental staff are currently working from home.
answer_text: Staff within the Department of Justice (DoJ) continue to work in line with the prevailing guidance.  A significant number of DoJ staff are required to be in the workplace to provide front-line services that cannot be delivered remotely.  The Department does not routinely capture information on the number of staff working from home as that changes on a daily basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what percentage of his departmental staff are currently working from home.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what percentage of his Departmental staff are currently working from home.
answer_text: As was the case throughout the pandemic, many staff within the Department continue to deliver front line services which cannot, by their nature, be delivered from home.  Following the lifting of the Executive’s guidance on the 6th June 2022, the Department, along with all other NICS departments, has commenced the implementation of the NICS Hybrid Working policy for roles which are suited to this way of working. This will be undertaken in a phased approach, over the coming months.   While line managers closely monitor the location of staff, the Department does not hold information on the number of staff who are working from home on any given day.  This information could not be obtained without disproportionate costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department has any plans to introduce financial incentives for households to switch to lower carbon heating.
answer_text: My Department intends to launch a pilot domestic and small business support scheme for low carbon heat in 2022/23 which will then lead to a wider rollout of support in the future.Further information on our pilot support scheme will be published at the earliest opportunity.
