question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail how many times food intended for consumption in Northern Ireland has been delayed or rejected because of complications during protocol checks.
answer_text: Under the terms of the Protocol, Northern Ireland (NI) is required to apply Sanitary and Phytosanitary (SPS) checks on agri-food goods entering from GB, in line with those checks prescribed for Third Countries as currently set out in EU law.  There are three types of SPS checks that must be performed (and completed satisfactorily), dependent on the nature of the consignment.  These include:		Documentary Checks: Examination of official certifications, attestations and other commercial documents that are required to accompany a consignment. Documentary checks are completed remotely and online in advance of arrival of the consignment at the NI Point of Entry (POE), a process that facilitates (and generally achieves) the timely resolution of any deficiencies detected during checks.  Failure to resolve documentary issues in advance of arrival will inevitably lead to delays at NI POE and potential rejection of the consignment in the event of failure.  Regarding documentary checks, records are maintained for associated rejections only, and not delays encountered by importers due to issues detected during the documentary check process.		Identity Checks: Visual inspection of a consignment in order to verify its content and labelling corresponds to the information provided in accompanying documentation.  To minimise delays, identity checks are delivered through a seal checking process at the Great Britain (GB) port of departure with those failing checks subjected to an identity checks on arrival in NI.  Consignments, which ultimately fail the identity check, will be refused entry to NI.  Similar to that with documentary checks, records are maintained of identity check associated rejections and not delays associated with the completion of such checks. 		Physical Checks: Physical inspection of the goods to verify that they are compliant with the SPS import requirements contained within EU law.  Physical checks are completed on a risk-based frequency at NI POE, records of which are maintained along with associated rejections.  Delays associated with completion of physical inspections vary with the complexity of the consignment and can range from 30 -120 minutes.		Authorised traders (supermarkets and their trusted suppliers) are able to avail of the ‘Scheme for Temporary Agri-Food Movements to Northern Ireland’ (STAMNI) for the movement of retail goods from GB to NI.  This simplified scheme, otherwise known as the ‘retail grace period’ accounts for approximately 55% of all agri-food consignments moving between GB and NI.  Such consignments are subjected to a simplified documentary and identity check procedures and no physical inspections provided adherence to the scheme requirements are met. 		Between 01 Jan 2021 and 30 June 2022, 215,060 consignments of agri-food goods entered NI from GB, with approximately 15% of these, destined for the Republic of Ireland. A total of 208,128 documentary checks, 202,752 identity checks and 40,140 physical checks were complet
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, following the end of the ban on poultry gatherings on 1 June 2022, whether his Department has any concerns regarding the prevalence of Avian Flu.
answer_text: Following a Veterinary Risk Assessment that indicated a decreasing level of risk the Avian Influenza Prevention Zone (AIPZ) was lifted in Northern Ireland (NI) on 1 June 2022. Poultry gatherings, shows and sales are now permitted under general licence.  There have been no further cases of Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza (HPAI) in poultry in NI since early February 2022 when a sixth infected premises was confirmed in County Fermanagh.  There have been a small number of wild bird submissions in NI since April 2022 when the wild bird survey recommenced, with two testing positive for HPAI H5N1. I am also aware of the increase in mortality of seabirds on Rathlin Island where testing has confirmed that HPAI is present. Therefore, given that there is still an ongoing low level risk of an AI incursion into poultry flocks, and in considering the risk still posed by wild birds, the biosecurity requirements set out in the AIPZ are still considered good practice and should be adhered to as much as practically possible by flock keepers. My Department continues to communicate this message widely to industry stakeholders and the general public through a range of communication channels.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment on bringing forward legislation, for planning applications and the implementation of Policy FLD 5, in line with the rest of the United Kingdom.
answer_text: The Department continues to progress further commencement and implementation of the Reservoirs Act (NI) 2015, which should assist with the implementation of Planning Policy FLD5, Development in Proximity to Reservoirs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many planning applications were refused due to the implementation of Policy FLD 5 during (i) 2019; (ii) 2020; and (iii) 2021.
answer_text: Whilst my Department publishes statistics on planning activity and performance on a quarterly and annual basis, the disaggregation being sought in the Member’s question is not available through existing statistical databases.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many planning applications have been delayed due to the implementation of Policy FLD 5 during (i) 2019; (ii) 2020; and (iii) 2021.
answer_text: Whilst my Department publishes statistics on planning activity and performance on a quarterly and annual basis, the disaggregation being sought in the Member’s question is not available through existing statistical databases.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the effectiveness of recycling facilities in North Down in meeting the needs of the area.
answer_text: The effectiveness of recycling facilities in North Down meeting the needs of the area is a matter for Ards & North Down Borough Council to assess as part of its obligation to prepare a Waste Management Plan. It is my understanding that the council’s Plan is being reviewed. In due course I will determine whether this Plan has properly taken account of the NI Waste Management Strategy, which will be revised over the coming months. The latest Northern Ireland local authority collected municipal waste management statistics, published on 28 April 2022, for Q3 of 2021/22 show an average twelve month rolling recycling rate of 50.2% across the eleven local councils. Recycling facilities within Ards & North Down Borough Council have contributed to this percentage with a recycling rate of 48.2%.I will continue to promote policies across Northern Ireland which meet local needs and improve our collective circular economy and climate change goals.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 1792/22-27, to outline the steps taken in relation to public safety when dealing with development considerations within the inundation zones of controlled reservoirs.
answer_text: The Reservoirs Act (NI) 2015, once fully commenced, will provide for the regulation of reservoir safety. As a statutory consultee to the planning process, my Department’s Rivers’ Directorate advise Planning Authorities in relation to flood risk in accordance with Revised PPS 15 ‘Planning and Flood Risk’ including policy FLD 5 - ‘Development in Proximity to Reservoirs’. This policy requires a developer to provide ‘sufficient assurance regarding reservoir safety, so as to enable the development to proceed. However, difficulties have been encountered when, due to the absence of the full commencement of the Act, developers have been unable to provide sufficient assurance regarding reservoir safety. In order to assist with this issue my officials and Local Planning Authorities have developed pragmatic short-term approaches to help deal with development considerations within the inundation zones of controlled reservoirs. These are based on the principle of the avoidance of any additional risk to public safety when new development in the flood inundation area of reservoirs is being considered by Planning Authorities.  However, these approaches are not a substitute for the full commencement of the Reservoirs Act.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for her assessment of the findings from the Landscape Review of the Education Authority.
answer_text: The Review of the Education Authority (EA) was undertaken on behalf of the Department by the consultancy firm Baker Tilly Mooney Moore. Its purpose was to consider the overall operation and governance of the EA, and the extent to which it is effectively able to deliver against stated Executive and Departmental priorities.In commissioning this review, my Department’s priority was to ensure the delivery of the best possible services for our children and young people throughout the education system. While the Report identifies that some progress has been made – including enhancement to governance, examples of good practice and emerging innovation, all set against the backdrop of the organisation’s relatively recent existence, and other challenges such as the COVID-19 pandemic and the increasing and diverse needs of our children and young people – it is clear that more needs to be done.  The Report makes thirteen recommendations, which are focused on the themes of:the future form, function and financing of the EA;improvements required to support organisational effectiveness; andgovernance, accountability and relationships which will support future EA effectiveness.Key issues highlighted in the report include the need for EA to improve communications and responsiveness; to better meet the expectations of the EA’s many stakeholders; and the importance of making education services more child-centric.My Department is fully committed to working with the EA to progress the issues identified in the report, and to shape the continuing improvement of the organisation in delivering for our children and young people.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for her assessment of the recent statement from the Ulster Teachers Union that the need for GCSE's is now hard to justify.
answer_text: GCSEs are important qualifications that provide access to a range of education, training and employment opportunities.  They have currency and portability across the UK and beyond.Alternative awarding arrangements that were put in place in the past two years, in response to the unprecedented public health situation, were effective in ensuring that young people were awarded their qualifications. However, these arrangements presented a range of challenges, including ensuring consistency across the education system and the requirement for school leaders and teachers to carry out assessments.As we emerge from the pandemic, it is right that we reflect on the lessons learned from that experience, including arrangements for the assessment of learners, particularly at the end of compulsory education at age 16.Reviews of both general and vocational qualifications will be taken forward within the context of 14-19 Framework, which Minister Lyons and I launched on 21 June 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of PSNI officers assaulted whilst on duty in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The recording of information on assaults against on-duty police officers is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.The Police Service of Northern Ireland maintain their own statistics on crimes against police officers. You may, therefore, wish to direct any question relating to the number of PSNI officers assaulted whilst on duty to the PSNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for her assessment of the impact that the absence of a set budget is having on the roll out of Attendance Allowance.
answer_text: The absence of a functioning Executive and agreed Executive budged is resulting in serious challenges to all Departments across many areas.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the impact that the absence of an agreed budget will have on the number of police officers over the next three years.
answer_text: In my previous response to the Member (AQW 598/22-27), I set out the impact of the current budgetary constraints on recruitment to the PSNI.  The PSNI has advised my Department that in the absence of clarity of in-year and future funding, police officer numbers are expected to reduce significantly by approximately 700 police officers (over three financial years) by 31 March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how much available public funding he is able to distribute in the absence of an Executive.
answer_text: Whilst a Budget cannot be put in place, my department has provided departments with contingency planning envelopes to allow them to make decisions on the allocation of resources until such times as an Executive establishes a Budget.  The contingency planning envelopes represent the minimum funding departments would expect to get in 2022-23 and in totality are less than the overall amount allocated to departments in the Draft Budget.There is also a further £435 million available for allocation in 2022-23, above the Draft Budget position which cannot be allocated in the absence of an Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, further to recent media reports, to confirm whether (i) 133 newly trained PSNI officers are not able to be deployed as a result of budgetary issues; and (ii) those officers are being paid in the interim period.
answer_text: In my response to the Member (AQW 1438/22-27), I set out the funding position and its impact on PSNI police officer numbers, including the target as set out in the New Decade New Approach document. In relation to the Member’s questions, PSNI recruitment and deployment, including training, are operational matters for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  As I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board, I would suggest that the Member contacts the PSNI directly for a response to his questions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what impact the cost of living crisis and the lack of a set budget will have on his ability to address the welfare of HSC staff.
To ask the Minister for the Economy what plans are in place to improve the telecommunications infrastructure in North Down.
answer_text: Telecommunications policy is a reserved matter, led by the Department for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport (DCMS) across the UK. The Department for the Economy (DfE) maintains regular contact with DCMS on all telecommunications matters and ensures that all local considerations are front and centre of any policy decisions taken regarding the improvement of mobile connectivity in Northern Ireland.Project Stratum, funded under the DUP/Conservative Confidence and Supply agreement, represents a total investment of almost £200million that is transforming the broadband connectivity bringing gigabit capable service to 85,000 premises in rural areas right across Northern Ireland. The total number of premises in the North Down Constituency that will benefit from Project Stratum is 874. There are also several ongoing initiatives which will help improve mobile connectivity throughout Northern Ireland. The Shared Rural Network (SRN), is a project developed by the UK’s four mobile network operators (BT(EE), Vodafone, O2 and Three) and UK Government, it will deliver 4G coverage to 95% of the UK, enabling rural businesses and communities to thrive.The Shared Rural Network (SRN), when implemented as planned, will make 4G mobile broadband available to rural communities across Northern Ireland. As part of the legally binding commitments set out within the SRN, mobile coverage in Northern Ireland will reach 98% from at least one operator, and 85% from all four operators compared to 97% and 79% respectively today.DfE understands the many challenges faced by our rural communities in terms of digital connectivity and indeed the issues faced by MNOs when they attempt to roll out telecommunications infrastructure.To help address these challenges and issues, my officials led on the development of the recently published cross Departmental Mobile Action Plan for Northern Ireland (MAP NI). Amongst other things, the MAP NI identifies the main issues that impact on the roll-out of mobile technologies and provides solutions that aim to remove these barriers, allowing us, amongst other things, to maximise the benefits of the SRN and other mobile technologies for citizens, businesses and visitors across Northern Ireland. You can view MAP NI at www.economy-ni.gov.uk/publications/mobile-action-plan-northern-ireland-map-ni.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) for her assessment of the shortfall in special school places for the new academic year; (ii) to detail the extent of the shortfall; and (iii) to detail what plans her Department has to address this issue.
answer_text: Securing appropriate provision for children with statements of Special Educational Needs (SEN) remains a key priority for me and is being taken forward by the Education Authority (EA) supported by the Department and key partners, to ensure children are placed appropriately and in line with their needs.  The EA has advised me that the shortfall in special school places for this new academic year is due to a number of factors including (but not limited to) a 34% increase in pupils with a statement of SEN in the last five years, and the need to provide appropriate placements to meet the individual needs of children including those with increasingly complex needs.  Other factors include the impact of dual day placements (two classes in one day using the same classroom) for Early Year’s children progressing into primary school provision, and the requirement for more classrooms.As of 27 June 2022, the EA advised that 228 children still require their allocated place to be confirmed by schools.  Currently all children with a statement of SEN at Stage 3, who are known to the EA have been mapped to an available suitable placement.  The statutory consultation process is ongoing with schools and parents to secure placements for these children. The EA established a cross-directorate planning group to address this issue, which is tasked to ensure that there is an appropriate place in a special school or specialist provision in a mainstream school, for every child with a statement who needs one.  Senior officials in my Department attend these meetings and hold regular oversight meetings with senior EA staff to monitor progress.Solutions implemented for September 2022 include additional places being provided in special schools with further capacity being delivered for September 2023 and beyond, and additional or new Specialist Provision classes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the reasons behind the recent increase in the cost for consumers of food items, including milk and bread products.
answer_text: A number of factors have contributed to the recent increase in costs for consumers of food items.  Input costs were on an upward trajectory during the latter part of 2021, with tight global grain markets driving increases in feed costs.  In addition, rising oil prices following the easing of lockdown restrictions exerted upward pressure on fuel and fertiliser prices during the same period.  Russia’s invasion of Ukraine in February 2022 has intensified market conditions.  Ukraine and Russia are important contributors to global grain and oilseed markets, particularly, wheat, maize, barley, sunflower seed and rapeseed markets. The disruption to exports and impact on 2022 production has led to sharp increases in the prices of these specific commodities and also other feed ingredients due to knock-on impacts.  The war also disrupted energy markets, which has further increased oil, fertiliser and fuel prices.  Oil prices have risen from approximately US$50 per barrel at the start of 2021 to over US$110 per barrel in April 2022.  The impact in the UK has been exacerbated in recent months by the fall in the value of Sterling compared to the US dollar.  The rise in costs of productions incurred by farmers has had a knock-on impact on agricultural commodity prices, which has fed through to higher costs to consumers.  The latest data for the UK consumer price inflation index shows that prices for food and non-alcoholic beverages rose by 8.7% between May 2021 and May 2022.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when roadway gullies were last cleaned in (i) Bangor; (ii) Groomsport; (iii) Holywood; and (iv) Millisle.
answer_text: The road gullies in Bangor and Holywood were last cleaned in May 2021 and in Groomsport in January 2021 as part of a scheduled programme delivered by an external contractor under a contract which expired in November 2021. Procurement of a replacement contract is ongoing and in the interim the Department is carrying out any reactive gully cleaning as required using its internal contractor resources. The road gullies in Millisle were last cleaned in July 2021 by my Department’s internal contractor and are scheduled to be cleaned again in July 2022. Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at: dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether the Housing Executive has halted inspections of properties that have residents wishing to avail of the Home Repair Assistance Grant.
answer_text: The three discretionary private sector grants, including the Home Repair Assistance Grant, administered by the Housing Executive are awarded in exceptional circumstances and where the applicant meets the relevant eligibility criteria which can be found here:https://www.nihe.gov.uk/Housing-Help/Grants/Types-of-grants-available.In the ongoing absence of an Executive and an agreed 2022-23 Budget, my Department is operating within a constrained Contingency Planning Envelope.As such currently Grant Technical Officers are not completing inspections or issuing any schedules of works or approvals for any discretionary grants unless there is a need for such works to be processed to facilitate the completion of mandatory Disabled Facilities Grants.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his officials routinely inspect the sites of completed work carried out by contractors on behalf of his Department, to ensure the timely removal of excess materials and road signage used during the work.
answer_text: Our contractors are responsible for health and safety on their live construction sites, and my officials routinely visit these sites to monitor progress and to ensure that disruption to surrounding properties and road users is being properly managed. Contractors expected to remove any excess material and signage in a timely manner once all the works have been completed.Issues with the road network, including at roadworks, can be reported using the links on NI Direct Problems with roads and streets | nidirect.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the projected budget required to facilitate the relocation of Central Primary School, Bangor.
answer_text: The Business Case for the new-build school for Bangor Central Integrated Primary School was approved in July 2020 at a cost of just over £9.4 million.  The projected costs for the project will be closely monitored as the design develops.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for her assessment of the help available to homeless people and those who sleep rough.
answer_text: The Housing Executive, has recently published the Homelessness Strategy 2022-27, which outlines its overall approach to addressing homelessness. This is supported by annual action plans with the Year 1 Action Plan detailing 44 actions to help those who are homeless or threatened with homelessness. The Homelessness Strategy 2022-27 is intrinsically linked to the draft Strategic Action Plan for Temporary Accommodation, which, was subject to public consultation in early 2022.  A final version of the Action Plan is due for publication in the coming months and will outline the Housing Executive’s efforts to support those households who avail of temporary accommodation. Any households who present as homeless to the Housing Executive will be provided with support and assistance through Housing Solutions and Support Teams who will seek to provide person centred services which are tailored to meet the needs of each household and support them to achieve sustainable housing solutions.  This will include the identification of any support needs and subsequent engagement with other localised services in order to provide as far as possible wrap around support.  This support can include floating support to prevent homelessness or temporary accommodation in cases where emergency accommodation is required.In addition to funding services which support the delivery of statutory duties, the Housing Executive also provides funding to a number of Voluntary Sector Organisations, which cover a range of support services including floating support, advice, and multidisciplinary services to assist those in danger of homelessness or in Temporary Accommodation.  The Housing Executive has also continued to resource a homelessness prevention grant to assist community organisations in both the prevention of homelessness and to help raise the level of homeless awareness within communities.  The prevention of homelessness remains a priority for the Housing Executive, as outlined in the Homelessness Strategy 2022-27.The Housing Executive works closely on a daily basis with outreach services to move people from the street into appropriate temporary accommodation and along with homeless providers will continue to ensure that any person wherever possible is engaging with support services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the plans to relocate Central Primary School in Bangor.
answer_text: Bangor Central Integrated Primary School has been selected for a major capital investment project to improve or replace its existing accommodation.  The project will cater for the school’s current approved enrolment of 618 pupils.The new design proposal for the school is progressing well.  A Proposal of Application Notice (PAN) was submitted to Ards and North Down Council planning service on 16 February 2022 commencing the statutory 12 week period prior to making a planning application.  As part of the PAN process a pre-application community consultation was undertaken via a public website from 18 March to 8 April, including a public event at the school on 30 March.The design team is now collating information from this process and preparing to make a full planning application in summer 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether her Department has any plans to increase awareness of benefits available to older people.
answer_text: My Department provides a Make the Call Service which aims to ensure that all individuals and their families are receiving the benefits, supports and services they are entitled to claim. This Service offers free and confidential needs assessments for individuals to check for entitlement to social security benefits, additional supports and services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail what plans his Department has for the provision of on-street car charging points within Donaghadee Town Centre.
answer_text: The current electric vehicle public charge point network is owned by the Electricity Supply Board (ESB) and is operated on a commercial basis. ESB announced in November 2021 that it had been awarded £3.27 million from the Levelling Up Fund to expand and enhance the EV charging network in the North. ESB can be contacted directly, regarding its plans for the provision of on-street car charging points within Donaghadee and Holywood Town Centre, at ecars@esb.ie or enquiries can be sent directly to ESB, Two Gateway, East Wall Road, DUBLIN 3, D03 A995. The market is also open to other commercial operators who would wish to provide charging infrastructure. I am pleased that my Department has been able to support the FASTER Project, funded through the INTERREG VA Territorial Co-operation Programme which is managed by the Special EU Programmes Body (SEUPB). The project has received funding to install a total of 73 EV Rapid charging points across the island of Ireland and the West of Scotland by 31 May 2023. My Department will be contributing €513,945 (£455,371) of Match Funding towards the FASTER project. The responsibility for the implementation of the project rests with SEUPB and it has agreed a delivery schedule with the project partners, led by the East Border Region Group Limited (EBR). It is expected that 20-30 of these will be located across the North and EBR is best placed to advise on charge point locations.	Funding opportunities for the installation of charge point infrastructure in GB-NI are provided by the Office for Zero Emission Vehicles (OZEV). My Department has made match-funding available of circa £350k, subject to public expenditure processes, from the Department’s Blue Green Fund to assist local councils making an application to the On-street Residential Charge Point Scheme (ORCS). A local council consortium, led by Derry City and Strabane District Council, submitted a joint application to the Energy Saving Trust which manages the fund on behalf of the Office for Zero Emission Vehicles (OZEV) on 30 March 2022. You may wish to contact your local council to enquire about its application and plans for the provision of on-street car charging points within Donaghadee and Holywood Town Centre.		My Department has also set up an EV Infrastructure Task-Force which brings together representatives from government, consumers, energy providers, industry and EV drivers to consider our EV Infrastructure requirements and to help set out a clear action plan by autumn 2022 to deliver a fit for purpose, modern EV charging network

The project has received funding to install a total of 73 EV Rapid charging points across the island of Ireland and the West of Scotland by 31 May 2023. My Department will be contributing €513,945 (£455,371) of Match Funding towards the FASTER project. The responsibility for the implementation of the project rests with SEUPB and it has agreed a delivery schedule with the project partners, led by the East Border Region Group Limited (EBR). It is expected that 20-30 of these will be located across the North and EBR is best placed to advise on charge point locations.	Funding opportunities for the installation of charge point infrastructure in GB-NI are provided by the Office for Zero Emission Vehicles (OZEV). My Department has made match-funding available of circa £350k, subject to public expenditure processes, from the Department’s Blue Green Fund to assist local councils making an application to the On-street Residential Charge Point Scheme (ORCS). A local council consortium, led by Derry City and Strabane District Council, submitted a joint application to the Energy Saving Trust which manages the fund on behalf of the Office for Zero Emission Vehicles (OZEV) on 30 March 2022. You may wish to contact your local council to enquire about its application and plans for the provision of on-street car charging points within Donaghadee and Holywood Town Centre.		My Department has also set up an EV Infrastructure Task-Force which brings together representatives from government, consumers, energy providers, industry and EV drivers to consider our EV Infrastructure requirements and to help set out a clear action plan by autumn 2022 to deliver a fit for purpose, modern EV charging network

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has plans to resurface sections of the Warren Road, Donaghadee.
answer_text: I can confirm that the resurfacing programmes for the Ards and North Down area are now, as in other areas, being finalised following recent confirmation of the Department’s 2022/23 contingency funding allocation.Details of works programmes, including resurfacing schemes, for the 2022/23 financial year will be included in my Department’s Report to Ards and North Down Council, which will, in due course, be presented to Council, published on the Department’s website and placed in the Assembly library.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has any plans to mitigate or change how inundations from reservoirs and private bodies of water are currently dealt with under Policy FLD5.
answer_text: As a statutory consultee to the planning process, my Department’s Rivers Directorate advise planning authorities in relation to flood risk in accordance with Revised PPS 15 ‘Planning and Flood Risk’ including policy FLD 5 - ‘Development in Proximity to Reservoirs’There are no plans at present to change how development in proximity to reservoirs is considered or administered under Revised PPS 15. In the absence of the full commencement of the Reservoirs Act, my Department and local planning authorities have developed and implemented pragmatic short-term approaches that take into account public safety, when dealing with development considerations within the inundation zones of controlled reservoirs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether all (i) post-primary; and (ii) primary school pupils in North Down have secured a school place for the incoming school year.
answer_text: Of those children from North Down who had an application submitted for a Year 8 place through this year’s post-primary admissions process, four remain to secure a place.No child from North Down who had an application submitted for a Year 1 place through this year’s primary admissions process remains to secure a place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of hotel bed capacity in the Greater Belfast area.
answer_text: There were 144 hotels with 9,563 rooms and 21,548 bed-spaces in Northern Ireland at the end of May 2022. One-quarter of Northern Ireland hotels are located in the Belfast City Council area, with these hotels accounting for approximately half of available rooms and bed-spaces.There were 36 hotels located in the Belfast City Council area at the end of May 2022, providing 4,646 rooms and 10,050 bed-spaces.A breakdown of the current stock of hotel accommodation within the Belfast City Council area is presented in Table 1 below. Currently, 22 of the 36 hotels within the Belfast Council area participate in Tourism Northern Ireland’s voluntary Quality Grading Scheme.Table 1: Hotel Stock in Belfast City Council Area by ClassificationCategoryGradingBed SpacesRooms/ UnitsPremisesHotel 10,0504,64636 Three Star9754736 Four Star4,7712,27514 Five Star4182082 Ungraded3,7441,61613 Awaiting Grading142741Over the past decade or so, the hotel landscape in Northern Ireland has changed dramatically, with the introduction of a number of budget branded hotels, the arrival of international brands as well as the upgrading of existing hotels.The number of available hotel rooms in the Belfast City Council area at the end of May 2022 increased by over one third compared with capacity at the end of December 2015, with bed-spaces increasing by more than two-fifths.Belfast welcomed the opening of six new hotels between 2016 and 2019, which collectively delivered an additional 1,097 rooms and 2,266 bed-spaces, with the majority of new rooms falling into the 4-star classification.Data released by the Northern Ireland Statistics & Research Agency (NISRA) suggests that, after a slow start to 2022, Northern Ireland hotel performance is beginning to show encouraging signs.Belfast hotel room occupancy for the January-April 2022 period was 57%, representing a 5 percentage point decrease compared to the same pre-pandemic period in 2019, with bed-space occupancy down by 7 percentage points. These declines are slightly greater than those witnessed for Northern Ireland overall – see Table 2.Table 2: Hotel Performance (Comparison Jan-April 2019 and 2022)Occupancy 20192022Change NI Room Occupancy58%55%-3pps*NI Bed-space occupancy47%42%-5ppsBelfast Council Room occupancy62%57%-5ppsBelfast Council Bed-space occupancy49%43%-7ppsSources:  NISRA.   *pps = percentage points – change calculated on unrounded figures Tourism Northern Ireland is aware of several hotels in the Greater Belfast area that are temporarily not operating as normal e.g. use by the Home Office and Councils to house asylum seekers or refugees. This temporary use will likely distort any published data on occupancy.  Looking ahead, there are currently three new hotels in progress in the Greater Belfast area totalling approximately 570 new bedrooms, with brands such as Moxy by Marriott and Motto by Hilton being proposed. There are also a further three known hotel projects at the planning stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what is the timescale for road surface defects, identified and marked by his Departmental staff, to be repaired.
answer_text: Article 8 of the Roads (Northern Ireland) Order 1993 places a duty on my Department to maintain all public roads in reasonable condition. We fulfil this duty by complying with a set of Maintenance Standards for Safety. These standards are designed to ensure a consistent service level across the network and safe highways for all road users.My Department utilises a system of regular inspections of the road network. Inspection frequencies vary between daily cycles for motorways to six monthly for carriageways carrying lower volumes of traffic. Our current standards specify a range of response times for the repair of defects, depending on factors such as their severity and the volume of traffic using the road. Response times range from one calendar day for the most serious defects to periods of between five working days and four weeks for less serious defects. However, if it becomes apparent the relevant response time cannot be met, my Department has the option of installing signs to warn road users of a possible danger.Currently, under our reduced level of service, my Department continues to repair the highest priority road defects across the entire road network. Safety related defects of greater than 50mm depth on all roads are being repaired and on high traffic roads in both urban and rural locations, defects of greater than 20mm are also being repaired. Defects of less than 50mm on more lightly trafficked roads are not currently being repaired but are reviewed again during subsequent inspections and will be prioritised for repair if their condition has deteriorated beyond the minimum intervention criteria of 50mm.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has plans to introduce traffic lights on the Newtownards Road Roundabout, Bangor.
answer_text: My Department has previously introduced measures at this location which serve to enhance driver awareness and reduce vehicle speeds on this busy part of the road network. Although there are no plans to carry out additional works at this time, I have asked my officials to keep the location under review.Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how much budget has been earmarked for the Forestry Grant Scheme in the current financial year.
answer_text: The Department of Finance (DoF) has provided Contingency Planning Envelopes to departments for budget planning purposes. Funding identified within this process to meet forestry grant commitments and new woodland grant applications for the current financial year is £3.4 million.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what steps his Department is taking to address the backlog in MOT testing.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) has steadily increased its vehicle testing capacity to pre-Covid levels by adopting a range of measures including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners, the use of overtime to provide cover for leave and by offering vehicle test appointments on a Sunday and bank holidays, when testing is not normally available.Official vehicle testing statistics are published on a monthly basis and can be accessed at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/driver-and-vehicle-agency-monthly-tests-conducted-statistics.  The DVA continues to experience significant demand for vehicle testing services and in some cases customers may not be able to have their vehicles tested before their current MOT certificate expires. In such cases, customers must book the earliest available test appointment for their vehicle, which may mean travelling to another test centre instead of their preferred location. The DVA releases vehicle test appointments on a daily basis and customers are encouraged to regularly check the booking system as they may be able to change their existing appointment to a different test centre and time if a slot becomes available. The latest information on MOTs is available on NI Direct at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/book-view-change-or-cancel-mot-vehicle-test and is also included as an advice note with every MOT Reminder Letter issued to their customers.	Vehicle testing services have been significantly impacted by the Covid-19 pandemic.  Whilst the DVA reinstated normal vehicle testing capacity from 26 July 2021 it has had to carefully manage its resources to mitigate against high transmission levels of the virus.   To minimise late cancellations due to the non-availability of staff, the DVA is offering more short notice appointments to ensure testing can be delivered when staffing availability is confirmed. This is allowing many customers to bring their MOT appointments forward, as new appointment slots are released. 	MOT testing is conducted in compliance with The Road Traffic (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 and the Motor Vehicle Testing Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2003 and the Goods Vehicle Testing Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2003, and this existing legislative framework does not provide for vehicle tests to be conducted at private garages.  There are no plans to consider changing this legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline the advice his Department offers to vehicle owners whose road tax renewal is due before they have been able to obtain an MOT appointment.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency’s (DVA) advice to vehicle owners who require an MOT to tax their vehicle and cannot secure a date before their MOT expires is to:book the earliest available appointment; andkeep checking the booking system for an earlier appointment.If they get to within five days of the expiry date of their vehicle tax and they have been unable to secure an MOT appointment or their vehicle tax has expired, they should contact the DVA Customer Services team at dva.customerservices@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk, and they will do their best to get an urgent appointment for the vehicle.  To date, all customers who have contacted the DVA in these circumstances have been offered an earlier appointment slot.The latest information on MOTs is available on NI Direct at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/book-view-change-or-cancel-mot-vehicle-test  and is also included as an advice note with every MOT Reminder Letter issued to their customers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what groups in North Down have been granted awards from the Rural Community Pollinator Grants Scheme since it was established.
answer_text: In the North Down area, six groups were granted awards under the Rural Community Pollinator Scheme.  The groups included:	Friends of St Patrick’s Ballygalget	Kilmood Playgroup;	Kircubbin Integrated Primary School;	Kircubbin Community Nursery School;	Millisle Youth Forum; and	St Columbas College.In addition, 32 projects were granted awards in the North Down area as part of the Schools Pollinator Garden Scheme.  The groups included:Ballyholme Primary School;Ballymagee Primary School;Ballyvester PS;Bangor Academy;Bangor Central Nursery School;Carrickmannon Primary School;Castle Gardens Primary School;Comber Primary School;Crawfordsburn Primary School;Glastry College;Glenlola Collegiate School;Grange Park Primary School;Groomsport ISLU;Holywood Primary School;Holywood Steiner School;Killard House School;Kilmaine Primary School;Millisle Primary School;Newtownards Nursery School;Portaferry Integrated Primary School;Priory College;Rathmore Primary School;St. Anne’s P.S.;St Finian’s Primary School;St Malachy’s PS;St Mary’s Primary School, Comber;St. Mary’s Primary School, Kircubbin;Saint Mary’s Primary School and Nursery Unit;Sullivan Upper School;Towerview Primary School;Victoria Primary School; andVictoria Primary School Ballyhalbert.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how much additional funding the PSNI requires in the current financial year to (i) maintain its current number of officers; and (ii) increase the number of officers to meet the target as set out in New Decade New Approach.
answer_text: In 2021-22, the PSNI received additional Executive funding to increase police officer numbers to approximately 7100, towards the target of 7500 set out in the New Decade New Approach. However, based on the assessment made of the impact of the 2022-25 draft budget for PSNI, the Chief Constable decided on a precautionary basis to defer the March 2022 PSNI recruitment intake and therefore the target of 7,100 officers by end of the 2021-22 was not achieved.The most recent assessment of the additional funding required by the PSNI in the current financial year to reach 7100 police officers is estimated at £24.3m; however, it is unlikely that 7100 could be achieved in the current financial year, due to the slowing-down of recruitment. However, should additional funding become available in the 2022-23 financial year, the PSNI  will focus  on increasing officer numbers back towards the previously planned level of 7,100, if considered affordable in the longer term.Regarding the Member’s second question, increasing police officer numbers to meet the NDNA target of 7500, the target of 7500 cannot be achieved in the current financial year, due to the slowing-down of recruitment. Furthermore, unless further funding is made available in future year budgets, the NDNA target of 7500 officers will not be met.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department plan to remove grass cuttings on the verges and central reservation along Bangor Ring Road.
answer_text: The Department undertakes grass cutting for safety reasons and to prevent overgrowth of carriageways and footway surfaces and the obstruction of sight lines and traffic signs, however, it is not undertaken for aesthetic or amenity purposes.Grass cuttings are not collected across the road network, including along the verges and central reservation on Bangor Ring Road, and my Department currently has no plans to change this arrangement.Should you have any further questions on this issue, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many faults have been reported regarding the traffic lights at the junction of South Circular Road and Balloo Drive, Bangor, during the last six months.
answer_text: I have been advised there have been five faults reported at the traffic lights at the junction of South Circular Road and Balloo Drive in Bangor over the last six months. In each instance repairs were completed within established timescales.  Should you have any further questions on this issue, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the arrangement between his Department and the local council for the maintenance of roundabouts in the Bangor area.
answer_text: Within the Ards and North Down Borough Council area there has been a long standing arrangement with my Department that the Council maintains a number of roundabouts. Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether any accommodation provision has been made for Bright Sparks Pre-School in the plans for the rebuild of Crawfordsburn Primary School.
answer_text: Due to its status as a voluntary provider, the Bright Sparks Pre-School is not eligible for capital funding. The mobile building the playgroup rents from the Education Authority sits outside the major works project for Crawfordsburn Primary School.However, the business case for Crawfordsburn Primary School included adequate site area to accommodate the playgroup. Consequently, I am pleased to confirm the Bright Sparks unit will be retained on the existing school site.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how many social housing properties still have inflammable cladding.
answer_text: The Housing Executive has three Tower Blocks on which a rain-screen cladding system has been installed.  This system does not contain any form of Aluminium Composite Material (ACM), Metal Composite Material (MCM) or High Pressure Laminate (HPL) which are deemed combustible cladding.  It was the subject of a full scale fire test through BRE and complied with Building Regulations at the time of installation.  The Housing Executive is currently reviewing these cladding installations against the new regulatory standards.The Housing Executive is also currently reviewing the rest of its stock to identify any properties that have cladding systems on external facades.  All Registered Housing Associations have confirmed that none of their social housing properties have inflammable cladding.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission what progress has been made in allocating office space in Parliament Buildings; and when a final allocation will be announced.
answer_text: The allocation of accommodation following each Assembly seeks to minimise disruption to new and returning Members and their staff. As far as possible, the accommodation plan tries to ensure an equitable share of available accommodation (in terms of floor area) based on the number of Members per party and given the variety of room sizes, types and locations that are available.On this occasion, the greater than normal amount of change to the relative strengths of the parties meant that this was not easy to achieve, resulting in more disruption to the layout than normal. Some concerns have been raised about the allocation of rooms and their location and there have been minor anomalies in the percentage floor areas available to some Parties. The plan that is currently being finalised tries to take account of these issues. The majority of accommodation has been allocated to Members but a small number of issues have still to be resolved and the Assembly Commission is expediting this as quickly as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether her Department has any plans to upgrade or replace the existing premises used by Bright Sparks Pre-School at Crawfordsburn Primary School.
answer_text: The Education Authority does not have any plans to upgrade or replace the existing premises used by Bright Sparks Pre-School at Crawfordsburn Primary School. Bright Sparks Pre-School is not a statutory provision, therefore the playgroup is not eligible for capital funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has any plans to erect a road sign indicating at what point the one way traffic system ends at Whitehill Pass, Bangor.
answer_text: My officials advise that a warning sign indicating a change from ‘one way’ to ‘two way’ traffic will be sited on the back of the existing ‘no entry’ sign.  This sign will be readily visible to drivers on the left hand side of the road when travelling along Whitehill Pass. This work is scheduled to take place in the coming weeks.Should you have any further questions on this issue, please contact DfI Roads Southern Division at dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when a permanent gully cleaning contractor will be operational in North Down.
answer_text: The procurement process to appoint a new Term Contractor for Environmental Maintenance in Newry, Mourne and Down, North Down and Ards is ongoing.  A contract award is expected by September 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the proportion of the livestock consumed in Northern Ireland that is imported.
answer_text: The Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs does not hold data on the amount of meat consumed in NI, nor from where domestically consumed meat has originated.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans she has to help families impacted by the rapid rise in food prices.
answer_text: I am deeply frustrated that the lack of a functioning Executive and agreed budget prevents any further cost of living support measures being introduced. However, I remain determined that my Department will continue to deliver support for those who are in need and I have tasked my officials to fully explore and expedite all options to address both the immediate needs of, and medium to longer term solutions to, the cost of living crisis. My Department has been running a Social Supermarket Pilot Programme at five sites since October 2017. A Social Supermarket provides holistic support through the provision of food alongside wraparound services to address the root causes of poverty. This includes, but is not limited to, debt and benefits advice, budgeting, healthy eating, cooking on a budget and training and volunteering opportunities to enhance future employability.Following a positive evaluation of the pilots, I have approved the expansion of the social supermarket approach and a budget of £1.5m has been allocated to support this work in 2022/23. This is being taken forward through a co-design process with each Council and their local stakeholders to design a model that best meets local need.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to provide an update on the success to date of the 10X Economy strategy for Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The 10x Economic Vision provided the outline of how we will use a decade of innovation to improve our economic, social and environmental wellbeing. Our strategy was widely endorsed by industry bodies, business leaders and academics including Ann McGregor of NI Chamber of Commerce, Michael Ryan of Spirit AeroSystems and Professor John McCanny from Queens University Belfast.The 10x Economic Vision sets out a strategic framework for economic policy over the next decade and was therefore the first step in setting out our priorities. It provides a focus for subsequent strategies and policies, ensuring alignment to our overarching vision. The vision has and will be supported by a series of publications to outline, in more detail, how we will transform our economy. My Department has already launched the Skills Strategy for Northern Ireland ‘Skills for a 10x Economy’, which outlines how we will develop our skills system to 2030.  We have also launched ‘Trade and Investment for a 10x Economy’, which sets out how we will harness trade and investment as a catalyst for inclusive, green growth in Northern Ireland.My officials are currently undertaking work to develop policies and interventions that will deliver a more innovative, inclusive and sustainable economy. Work will culminate in the publication of a 10x Implementation Plan, due to be launched later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the benefits that the technology sector contributes to the Northern Ireland economy.
answer_text: The Technology Sector - defined as “businesses that sell goods and services in electronics, software, computers, artificial intelligence, and other industries related to information technology” - is crucial to the Northern Ireland Economy. The 2021 UK Tech Nation Report states that the total number of Technology Sector businesses in Northern Ireland is 2,195.  In 2019 the Technology Sector contributed around 3.5% of total NI GVA or £1.4 billion. In comparison, twenty years ago, the Technology Sector contributed just 0.5% of NI GVA.There has been a steady increase in employment in the sector to Q4 2021, when the number stood at 22,710, having seen over 7,000 new jobs created in the preceding decade. These are typically higher value jobs. It is estimated that those working in the Information & Communication sector in NI earn on average £753.70 per week, compared to the Northern Ireland average of £531.60 for all industries. In 2021, there were 22 software & IT FDI projects in NI representing an investment of £127 million. In 2020, there were 23 FDI projects in the sector, a spend of £124 million. This suggests growth in the sector has not been hampered by the Covid pandemic as investment in these two years outperformed the FDI attracted by the sector in the two years prior to the pandemic. Beyond its own growth, the Technology Sector also serves to drive productivity across the wider economy, providing innovations and technology based services to almost every other sector. This was never more evident than during the Covid-19 pandemic when the Technology Sector kept much of our business, academia and vital public services functioning effectively.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he received advice to no longer answer operational questions relating to NI Water.
answer_text: I received advice from my officials that NI Water is best placed to directly answer questions which relate to wholly operational matters.  NI Water has a well-established customer service function, with a dedicated MLA response team.  It is significantly more efficient, therefore, that questions of an operational nature, such as the performance of NI Water’s assets and the management and delivery of its services are referred directly to NI Water to answer.Pursuant to AQW 599/22-27, the delivery of water and sewerage services is the responsibility of NI Water’s CEO and the NI Water Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the CEO and the role of the NI Water Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether any fines were issued to people who refused to participate in the 2021 Census.
answer_text: Census Office instigated formal non-compliance procedures for a number of households where the householder had explicitly refused to complete a Census return. Due to ongoing court action we cannot provide any further details at this time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his oversight responsibilities for NI Water.
answer_text: NI Water is a Government Owned Company and a Non Departmental Public Body, operating at arm’s length from central government.  My Department is the shareholder, appoints the NI Water Board of Directors and is responsible for overall policy and legislation and for setting strategic objectives through social and environmental guidance.  The actual delivery of water and sewerage services is the responsibility of NI Water and the NI Water Board, and is subject to independent regulatory oversight and monitoring by the NI Authority for Utility Regulation and environmental regulators.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 599/22-27, whether NI Water were consulted on the final response provided.
answer_text: NI Water was consulted on the final response provided and agreed that this was an operational matter within its remit to answer.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 599/22-27, why he did not answer the question, which related to matters within NI Water, when his Department has commented on such matters previously.
answer_text: I received advice from my officials that NI Water is best placed to directly answer questions which relate to wholly operational matters.  NI Water has a well-established customer service function, with a dedicated MLA response team.  It is significantly more efficient, therefore, that questions of an operational nature, such as the performance of NI Water’s assets and the management and delivery of its services are referred directly to NI Water to answer.Pursuant to AQW 599/22-27, the delivery of water and sewerage services is the responsibility of NI Water’s CEO and the NI Water Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the CEO and the role of the NI Water Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the economic benefits Northern Ireland received from the Queen's Platinum Jubilee celebrations.
answer_text: My Department has not undertaken an assessment of the economic benefits of the Queens Platinum Jubilee celebrations. However, after the Covid related economic challenges of the past two years, the Jubilee weekend was certainly a welcome boost to our hospitality, retail and tourism sectors.The Platinum Jubilee was a truly historic moment, the first time any British monarch has reached this milestone. In time, we will better understand the economic impact of the celebrations. The benefits are likely to have extended far beyond the economy and the Jubilee weekend can be expected to have boosted civic pride and social cohesion.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department plans to address concerns expressed by the hospitality industry around the lack of late night public transport in Belfast.
answer_text: Translink are currently running their full bus and train timetable as published. For larger events they also provide additional later evening bus and train services to cater for the increased demand.  Translink aim to provide further additional later evening capacity as we build back post Covid-19 to support the night-time economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Queens Parade project in Bangor.
answer_text: The Developer’s proposals for the Queen’s Parade project are currently being considered by the Ards and North Down Borough Council’s planning service. My officials, in the Department’s Regional Development Office, along with the Ards and North Down Borough Council, are continuing to work closely with the Bangor Marine team to progress this scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he has plans to further decentralise higher education facilities to areas outside Belfast.
answer_text: Northern Ireland’s universities are autonomous bodies and my Department does not dictate where campuses are located nor where specific courses are offered. Ulster University offers a range of subjects across its campuses in Belfast, Coleraine and Londonderry. In terms of expansion of provision outside of Belfast, Ulster University welcomed its first cohort of students to its Graduate Entry Medical School in August 2021, and will enrol a further intake for Academic Year 2022/23 as well as relocating students on its undergraduate Health Sciences programme from the Jordanstown campus.My Department is in discussion with Ulster University in relation to its strategic objectives for growth across all its campuses.  Additionally, the Open University offers a wide range of distance-learning higher education qualifications which students can access anywhere in Northern Ireland.It is also important to note the key role of the six Further Education colleges funded by my Department in delivering a range of higher education courses and opportunities.  Courses are available on a full and part time basis and are delivered across approximately 23 campuses.  Campuses delivering higher education provision are spread across Northern Ireland, including Enniskillen, Omagh, Londonderry, Limavady, Newtownards, Bangor, Newry, Newcastle, Cookstown and Magherafelt. There are approximately 19 further education campus locations outside of Belfast where higher education provision is available.  There are a variety of higher education qualifications and subject areas delivered at these locations, with qualifications including Foundation Degrees, Higher National Certificates and Diplomas and full degrees.  All Foundation Degrees and the majority of degrees available at the further education colleges are developed in partnership with and awarded by our three local universities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether she is currently in a position to sign off on any funding for the Casement Park project.
answer_text: I welcome the recent outcome of the Judicial Review which upheld the decision to approve Planning Permission for the redevelopment of Casement ParkThe Executive has agreed Casement Park as a priority under the flagship stadia programme, and is an NDNA commitment.The next stage of the project will be to consider the judgement in full, complete the business case and finalise overall costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how her Department plans to address the shortfall in funding for the Casement Park project.
answer_text: The Executive has agreed Casement Park as a priority under the flagship stadia programme, and is an NDNA commitment.  The next stage of the project will be to consider the judgement in full, complete the business case and finalise overall costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she was aware of the issues raised by the BBC Spotlight programme, prior to its broadcast on 31 May 2022.
answer_text: During one of my regular stocktake meetings with the Chief Constable on 31 May 2022, I became aware of some of the issues that were likely to be raised by the BBC Spotlight programme on 31 May.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the BBC Spotlight programme on 31 May 2022 following its investigation into issues raised by PSNI whistleblowers.
answer_text: As the Member is aware, PSNI investigations relating to whistleblowing are ultimately an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  As I have stated in my answer to AQW 898/22-27, I am committed to respecting this operational independence and the role of Northern Ireland Policing Board. The Chief Constable is aware of the allegations made on BBC Spotlight Programme and I understand that investigations are ongoing.  It is important that I respect due process and the existing accountability and oversight mechanisms in place.  Therefore, it would not be appropriate for me to comment any further at this stage.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the length of time taken by the PSNI to investigate allegations against the whistleblowers who participated in the BBC Spotlight programme on 31 May 2022.
answer_text: As the member is aware, PSNI investigations relating to the whistleblowing are ultimately an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting this operational independence and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board. Accordingly, you should direct your question to the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the implementation of the Executive's Tackling Paramilitarism, Criminality and Organised Crime programme.
answer_text: The Executive’s Programme to Tackle Paramilitary Activity, Criminality, and Organised Crime is dealing with a vast complex, intergenerational problem through a whole of government approach. Although the Programme Team is hosted in the Department of Justice, the Programme itself is cross-sectoral and cross-Executive in nature, reflecting the complex nature of paramilitarism and the need for a multi-faceted response to it. Currently, nearly 80 projects and bespoke interventions are being delivered collaboratively through a network of 7 NI Civil Service (NICS) Departments, 22 statutory agencies, and over 50 public sector and 180 community/voluntary sector organisations. In each of its four annual reports, the Independent Reporting Commission has pointed to significant progress towards addressing issues of concern. Since the start of the Programme, significant numbers of people, including thousands of young people, have been supported. Capacity is being built in communities; and wider systems change is helping to ensure that existing supports are more joined up and better able to address the many manifestations of paramilitary harm. Extensive trauma-training is helping frontline delivery partners to manage complex challenges. Early interventions are reducing pressures and cost elsewhere in the system. The Programme’s public awareness campaigns have generated debate and are playing their part in addressing attitudinal change.  Addressing paramilitarism requires sustained effort, and the Programme is using a data-driven and outcomes based approach (Benefits Realisation) to help focus effort and facilitate reporting on longer-term outcomes. Projects are subject to evaluation and progress is being measured by collecting and analysing short and longer-term qualitative and quantitative data. The Programme also monitors the NI Life and Times Survey results to understand the wider impact of paramilitarism at a population level. There is no quick fix for tackling these complex, cross cutting and challenging issues; they require our continuing cross-Executive focus.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there have been any logistical issues encountered with phasing in zero emission buses into the public transport network.
answer_text: In the absence of an Executive a Budget has not been agreed for 2022-23 meaning that departments have no certainty upon which to plan. To assist with this DOF has informed departments of Contingency Planning envelopes which provide an understanding of funding for which there may be a reasonable degree of certainty that it will be contained in any Budget position brought to the Executive for consideration. However, Ministers have also been advised that they should not cease expenditure where there is a reasonable expectation that the department will receive further funding later in the financial year once a Budget is put in place. This is particularly important where other organisations are dependent upon funding from departments.In terms of funding available, I can confirm a further £435 million has become available since the Draft Budget was published. This funding cannot be allocated in the absence of an Executive. It is not possible to quantify the level of funding which is currently going unspent as this depends on decisions taken by individual Ministers. Furthermore, the available funding can be allocated later in the year once an Executive is formed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of meetings, including dates, she has held with officials of the Police Federation since her appointment.
answer_text: Since taking office as Justice Minister I have met with the Police Federation seven times, either virtually, in person or by telephone, on the following dates;8 April 202010 December 202026 May 202116 June 20216 July 20218 December 202125 May 2022

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline plans for the development of Ulster-Scots culture and heritage in schools across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The NI curriculum provides significant scope for the teaching of the Ulster-Scots culture and heritage in schools across Northern Ireland. These can be delivered through areas such as Language & Literacy; History; Music and the Arts more broadly; Mutual Understanding in the Local and Wider Community; and Local and Global Citizenship within Learning for Life and Work, where young people are able to explore cultural identity, heritage, language and diversity.To support schools in the delivery of the curriculum the Council for the Curriculum, Examinations and Assessment (CCEA) produces a wide range of curriculum resources for schools.  CCEA has a Curriculum Connections area on their website which has several resources dedicated to learning about Ulster-Scots and shared cultural heritage. The CCEA Northern Ireland @ 100 microsite also features relevant presentations and resources on language in Northern Ireland and pupils are given the opportunities to explore significant contributions of key people and events to Ulster-Scots language and culture.CCEA has also supported the Ulster-Scots Agency with the development of a learning resource called ‘Land of Promise’ and engaged with the Ulster Scots Education Project to develop fifteen units of work for post-primary schools, and with the Ulster-Scots sector to develop additional resources designed for Key Stage 2 pupils. There are also a range of qualifications at GCSE and A-level which enable students to learn about the origins of Ulster-Scots; the actions and contributions of Ulster-Scots in the 18th, 19th and early 20th centuries; local artists and designers; variations in spoken and written language; cultural identity, heritage and diversity; and the diversity of heritage music.The Department also provides annual funding to the Bready District Ulster Scots Development Association (BDUSDA) to deliver highland dance and bagpipe tuition to a number of schools in the Derry and Strabane area. Last year the Department provided additional funding to enable BDUSDA to grow their programme by increasing their geographical remit and tutor hours and to include the tuition of the B Flat Flute.  BDUSDA are also delivering new Ulster Scots Cultural Awareness Sessions to 2400 pupils.
