answer_text: In response to the first part of your question the Housing Executive has provided the following table detailing the number of applicant households, broken down by composition, on the waiting list as at 31 March 2022, in the North Down Parliamentary Constituency.Table 1: Waiting List (WL) as at 31/03/22 at North Down Parliamentary Constituency by Household Type.Parliamentary  ConstituencyHousehold CompositionNo. of Applicant HouseholdsNorth DownElderly557Large Adult42Large Family112Single729Small Adult117Small Family392Total1949In response to the second part of the question the Housing Executive has provided the following table detailing the allocations between 01/04/17 and 31/03/22 for the North Down Parliamentary Constituency, by Household Type and the Median months on the Waiting List at the Point of Allocation.Parliamentary ConstituencyHousehold CompositionNo. of AllocationsMedian Months on the WL at the Point of AllocationNorth DownElderly3499.0Large Adult2213.5Large Family12514.0Single68911.0Small Adult7815.0Small Family45810.0Unknown<10Total1721*11.0Notes:Waiting List data is a snapshot of the waiting list as it was on the last day of the specified quarter, in this case 31/03/22.Allocations data is provided over 5 years from 01/04/17 - 31/03/22.Allocations data includes allocations to both Housing Executive and housing association properties.<10 are cases where numbers are less than 10.* Totals minus cases where there are less than 10.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department has formally closed the High Street Scheme.
answer_text: The High Street Scheme was a huge success and achieved exactly what it set out to do. The Scheme was designed to provide an economic boost to the local economy and injected over £136.5 million into the local economy leading to an improved level of consumer confidence and increased levels of public spending.		The High Street Scheme closed at midnight on 19 December 2021.  It re-opened on 24 December 2021 for a two week period for a limited number of individuals whose cards were not delivered by 19 December 2021 to give them additional time to spend on their card.		In recognition of the fact that some verified applicants did not receive a card while some others encountered difficulties activating and using their card, I announced that I would be making remedy payments to eligible applicants. My Department continues to identify and process payments for those eligible to receive remedy payments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the impact that rising energy costs will have on food producers and food processors.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of the work of the National Trust in protecting the integrity of the North Down and Ards coastlines.
answer_text: My Department has made no formal assessment of the National Trust’s work in protecting the integrity of the North Down and Ards coastlines.  However I am aware they take a natural-based, sustainable approach in managing coastal change, and are the landowner of 22% of the Northern Ireland coastline. Works within the intertidal area are subject to the marine licensing requirements of my Department.My Department also co-chairs a Coastal Forum, along with DfI.  The membership includes the Chief Executives of coastal councils and the NI Director of National Trust. I am appreciative of the role National Trust plays in the Coastal Forum, as a key stakeholder and source of technical expertise on coastal matters. The Coastal Forum and its Working Group is taking a partnership approach to improving how we manage coastal change in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what criteria was used to select the initial 150 grassroots organisations that would benefit from the £1500 Energy Payment Grant.
answer_text: I refer the member to my reply to AQW 768/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department has any plans to encourage local councils to make more land available for use as allotments.
answer_text: My Department does not have any plans or funding schemes in place regarding the allocation of land by local councils for use as allotments. My Department engages on projects with local councils, many of which are funded through rural development grant schemes.  These include Council-led projects on the Department’s forest land under well-established partnership arrangements. Forest Service promotes and supports the establishment of new woodland areas on Council land through its forestry grant schemes.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the Rural Community Pollinator Scheme.
answer_text: A total of £1.5m of grant funding has been awarded to 178 projects under the Rural Community Pollinator Scheme. Under the Schools Pollinator Garden Scheme I have approved expenditure of £2.4m across 301 school projects in both rural and urban areas. In addition following an open competition, I recently presented an award of £100,000 to Magheralin Parish Church Community Group to develop a Platinum Jubilee Pollinator garden of reflection.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of (i) the impact of current budgetary constraints on recruitment to the PSNI; and (ii) how rising fuel costs will impact on PSNI transport demands.
answer_text: In relation to the Member’s first query, in the absence of an Executive, a budget for 2022-23 cannot be agreed. In order to assist with decision-making, the Department of Finance has provided each department with a Contingency Planning Envelope for 2022-23 for both Resource and Capital DEL, to allow the continuation of service delivery. The Contingency Planning Envelope for PSNI did not contain specific funding for police numbers and is insufficient to maintain, let alone increase, police numbers.The proposed draft Budget for 2022-25 is insufficient to maintain the police officer numbers at 7,100 and further progress to increase police officer numbers toward the New Decade New Approach (NDNA) target of 7500 will not be possible unless further funding is made available. I have raised concerns regarding the Police Budget with the Finance Minister and Executive colleagues on several occasions since the publication of the draft Budget 2022-25.The 2022-23 financial position for the Department looks extremely challenging at this stage and that, if confirmed that would require difficult decisions to be made.  There have been a number of changes to the funding position for 2022-23 from the announcement of the Draft Budget, and there is now a significant amount of additional resource funding available for allocation in 2022-23. In the absence of an Executive, this funding cannot be allocated. The reality is there is no Executive or Assembly in place to approve a Budget.   It is imperative that we come back together as an Executive and work collectively to ensure that all available funds are effectively utilised.The prioritisation of policing resources, including recruitment, is ultimately an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. I am committed to respecting his operational independence and the role of the Policing Board. In relation to the Member’s second query, matters relating to transport demand is an operational matter for the PSNI. I appreciate that rising fuel costs is a concern and my Department will continue to engage with the PSNI on funding matters.I remain committed to continue working with the Minister of Finance and the Chief Constable, to ensure that we have a police service that is properly resourced in all aspects for the challenges it faces.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the distribution of the £100,000 funding for women's football announced in March 2022.
answer_text: The Department for Communities provided a letter of offer for a grant of £100,000 on 2 March 2022 to the Irish FA to fund projects under the Women’s Football Legacy Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether measures his Department currently has in place will prevent any water shortages during coming months.
answer_text: Management of measures to prevent water shortages in the coming months across the North is an operational matter for NI Water. You should therefore, direct your question to NI Water at waterline@niwater.com.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his officials and officers from Ards and North Down Borough Council will meet with residents of The Point, Groomsport, to discuss possible solutions to the roadway at this location to enable it to be brought up to adoption standard.
answer_text: The basis for adopting private roads or laneways is similar to that which applies to streets within new housing developments, in that they firstly must be brought up to the required standard before they can adopted and my Department takes on maintenance responsibilities.In order for my Department to agree to the adoption of The Point, significant improvements would need to be carried out by, or on behalf of, the frontagers, which would include full road construction with a footpath on one side, provision of a turning head, road drainage and upgraded street lighting.Subject to these works being satisfactorily completed, my Department would adopt the road providing:the majority of frontagers consent to and request in writing that the road should become a public road; and.appropriate planning permission through Council Planning is in place with any associated costs in bringing The Point up to adoption standard, including planning, design and construction costs being borne by the frontagers.I can confirm my officials are always willing to meet with or provide whatever guidance and advice is required to assist frontagers who wish to take forward projects of this type and can be contacted via the following email address: dfiroads.southern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans his Department has in place to improve road safety.
answer_text: My Department has a statutory duty to promote road safety and this is a responsibility that I take very seriously. My Department does this through a range of road safety educational activities, engineering and other initiatives. My Department remains focussed on ensuring the road network is safe for road users, from an engineering perspective.  Our work maintaining and making improvements to the network has an underlying safety aim, with activities ranging from renewing road markings and signage, to major resurfacing works or indeed delivery of major schemes.As well as providing measures to help people move safely, such as the provision of traffic signs and pedestrian crossings, we will also continue to provide schemes aimed specifically at improving road safety. Such schemes include collision remedial schemes which target known problem areas, and traffic calming measures that are primarily aimed at slowing traffic movements through areas.Our street lighting, traffic control and motorway communications systems also continue to play an important role in providing information to help road users navigate our road network safely and successfully.Excessive speed continues to be a problem in some areas and we will continue to work with our policing partners to set speed limits that are appropriate for the local conditions.  We are also planning to undertake a review of the recent initiative that saw the introduction of part-time 20mph speed limits outside 219 schools over the past two financial years. My Department has a rolling programme of educational activities, including the road safety public information campaigns.  Regrettably over 95% of road traffic collisions where someone dies or is seriously injured are due to human error. Therefore, in addition to making proven engineering interventions, my Department also challenges driver behaviour and through the road safety campaigns, reminds motorists of their responsibility to themselves and other road users.  In the coming months I will consider if more can be done to positively influence road user behaviour.I am also currently considering a draft new Road Safety Strategy to 2030 for the North.   The content of the final Strategy will require Executive approval, which is not possible at this time due to the ongoing absence of the Executive.  We will continue working with others including PSNI, Fire and Rescue Service and the Ambulance Service on road safety issues to further reduce deaths and serious injuries on our roads.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission what plans it has to facilitate translation of contributions made in Irish or Ulster Scots in the Assembly Chamber, for the benefit of those in the public gallery.
answer_text: A wireless headphone system has been installed for use in the public gallery. When entering the public gallery, members of the public can collect a headset, through which they will hear the audio from the Chamber, including the translation of any contributions in Irish or Ulster Scots.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has any plans to clear the blocked roadside gutters in Main Street, Groomsport.
answer_text: Regrettably the external contractor who had been engaged by the Department to undertake gully emptying work in this area withdrew from the contract at the end of last year reducing our capacity to provide gully emptying services within the Bangor and Groomsport area. I can confirm that a competition for a replacement contract is ongoing and it is anticipated a contract will be awarded in the near future. In the meantime, I have asked officials to continue to keep this area under review and if any specific issues with the drainage system are identified, to make arrangements to undertake any necessary remedial works.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the impact of rising energy costs on local (i) Small and Medium Enterprises; and (ii) retail outlets.
answer_text: Significant increases and ongoing volatility in international wholesale fuel prices for oil products and natural gas are impacting adversely on the price we are paying for electricity, natural gas, heating oil and transport fuels. We, in Northern Ireland, have no control on the wholesale cost of the fossil fuels which we import – we are a ‘price taker’.Unfortunately, current forecasts are that the high prices are set to continue for some time. Obviously, the rising energy costs are putting pressure on the net incomes of local businesses, which they are seeking to offset through increasing prices, where this might be possible. Obviously, this in turn puts upward pressure on the retail prices which we, as the consumer, are paying for our products and services.My department continues to work with the Utility Regulator and the Consumer Council to ensure that small and medium size businesses and retail outlets are protected as much as possible through the regulated tariff regime in Northern Ireland. One of the key objectives of the new Energy Strategy is to ensure that, in the medium term, all businesses have access to affordable energy to help maintain competitiveness.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of the reported disclosure of protected personal data by the PSNI to out of state law enforcement agencies.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board (NIPB). I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the NIPB. You should, therefore, direct your question to the Chief Constable.I note that the Police Service of Northern Ireland have advised that they are taking this issue extremely seriously and referred themselves to the Office of the Police Ombudsman and informed the Information Commissioner’s Office (ICO).  I understand that they have also kept the NIPB and ICO updated.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 will reduce the number of additional places available to post-primary controlled schools.
answer_text: When a school seeks additional places through the Temporary Variation (TV) process it is the number of alternative places available in that school sector only that are considered.  For the purposes of considering TV requests, four school sectors are recognised, namely denominational; non-denominational; Integrated; and Irish-medium.  Similarly, the Development Proposal (DP) process considers availability within school sectors.This will continue to be the case even when the Integrated Education Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 comes into operation on 26 October 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what assistance is available to families to help with the cost of school uniforms.
answer_text: Uniform Grants are available to families who meet certain qualifying criteria: https://www.eani.org.uk/financial-help/free-school-meals-uniform-grants/free-school-meals-uniform-grant-eligibilityThe Department has commenced a Review of Free School Meals and Uniform Grant eligibility criteria.  The review is considering a number of options for free school meals and uniform grants, including the options for universal free school meal provision for certain year groups or all pupils and the removal/adjustment to current thresholds for Universal Credit/Working Tax Credit as well as the potential to legislate on uniform policy.   My Department continues to work to develop costed options and a consultation exercise will take place later in the year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) how many Special Police Service duties the PSNI has carried out in the last three years; and (ii) to detail the costs involved.
answer_text: Matters relating to the policing duties carried out by the PSNI and associated costs are ultimately an operational matter for the Chief Constable of the PSNI.  The Chief Constable is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board (NIPB). I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the NIPB.You should, therefore, direct your question to the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department has any plans to increase the employability prospects of those with disabilities.
answer_text: The Department for Communities (DfC) has lead responsibility for employment policy, including for those with disabilities, and is currently developing a Disability Employment Strategy for Northern Ireland.  My officials are working with their counterparts in DfC to contribute to the draft strategy, primarily in relation to skills issues.  A range of practical, financial and other support is provided for people with disabilities across the further and higher education sectors, as well as for those participating on the vocational training programmes funded by the Department.  Examples of the type of support include the Disabled Students Allowance for Higher Education students, a focus on disability through the Widening Access and Participation Plans, the Additional Support Fund in Further Education, extended eligibility and flexibilities on the Skills for Life and Work programme, specialist support through the Disability Support Service contract.  Despite this, however, we are aware that more remains to be done to impact on the disability employment gap that currently exists in Northern Ireland.   The recently published Skills Strategy is providing a key platform for driving forward the response to these issues in my Department.  A Skills Equality, Diversity and Inclusion (SEDI) sub-committee of the Northern Ireland Skills Council has been established to look at access and participation for those in under-represented or disadvantaged groups, with the aim of identifying barriers and advising on policy solutions and actions to help drive changes to remove these obstacles.  Disability and access to skills is one of the first substantive issues being considered by the SEDI sub-committee.  	I trust this answer is helpful in relation to my Department’s commitment in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the proposed badger cull.
answer_text: I launched the new bovine Tuberculosis Strategy (bTB) for Northern Ireland on the 24 March 2022. As part of Strategy I have agreed to a programme of badger removal closely modelled on that deployed in England of a farmer led and paid intervention using controlled shooting of free roaming badgers supplemented by cage trapping and shooting.In coming to a final position on this element of the Strategy, my Department considered a range of relevant scientific information, the recommendations of a comprehensive business case, operational experience of other jurisdictions, comprehensive policy and veterinary advice, and feedback from the extensive public consultations on the matter. My officials are now seeking to develop the necessary processes and guidance for a badger removal programme in Northern Ireland.  In order for an intervention to take place legislation must also be laid at the Assembly.  This would only be done once all other necessary actions have been taken by both the Department and by industry.I want to see all of the actions within the Strategy implemented as soon as practically possible and this includes badger removal in specific intervention areas where there is a high density of badger and high incidence of bTB in herds. It should be noted, however, that the specific proposals relating to this element of the Strategy are currently subject to ongoing judicial review proceedings which will be required to progress to a conclusion prior to implementation of badger removal on the ground.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer; and whether she has any further talks planned with stakeholders.
answer_text: I have received legal advice in respect to the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer and this confirms that I have a responsibility to bring my proposals to the Executive for the necessary approval.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Casement Park project.
answer_text: I welcome the recent outcome of the Judicial Review which upheld the decision to approve Planning Permission for the redevelopment of Casement ParkThe Executive has agreed Casement Park as a priority under the flagship stadia programme, and is an NDNA commitment. The next stage of the project will be to consider the judgement in full, complete the business case and finalise overall costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will consider placing Give Way signage on the roadway for traffic entering Bridge Street from Moat Street, Donaghadee.
answer_text: My officials advise there is no need for Give Way signage for drivers entering Bridge Street from Moat Street.  Vehicles travelling along Moat Street and High Street each have their own continuous lane flowing into Bridge Street with those wishing to merge or swap lanes as the two lanes run alongside each other along Bridge Street should do so carefully and courteously and in accordance with the Highway Code.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department will consider placing Give Way signage on the roadway for traffic entering New Street from The Parade in Donaghadee.
answer_text: I have been advised by officials that there is no need for Give Way signage to advise drivers entering New Street from The Parade.  Vehicles travelling along The Parade from both directions each have their own continuous lane flowing into New Street.  Vehicles wishing to merge or swap lanes as the two lanes flow beside each other along New Street should do so with care, courteously and in accordance with the Highway Code.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update regarding a number of homes at Cranley Grove, Bangor, where there is a dispute about responsibility for the sewage system serving those homes.
answer_text: The management of sewerage infrastructure at Cranley Grove, Bangor and across the North is an operational matter for NI Water.  You should therefore, direct your question to NI Water at waterline@niwater.com .

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) what plans his Department have for investing in electric vehicle charging infrastructure in North Down; and (ii) how much has been spent on this in North Down in the last three years.
answer_text: I am pleased that my Department has been able to support the FASTER Project, funded through the INTERREG VA Territorial Co-operation Programme which is managed by the Special EU Programmes Body (SEUPB). The project has received funding to install a total of 73 EV Rapid charging points across the island of Ireland and the West of Scotland by 31 May 2023. My Department will be contributing €513,945 (£455,371) of Match Funding towards the FASTER project. The responsibility for the implementation of the project rests with SEUPB and it has agreed a delivery schedule with the project partners, led by the East Border Region Group Limited (EBR). It is expected that 20-30 of these will be located in the North and EBR is best placed to advise on the locations of these charge points.Funding opportunities for the installation of charge point infrastructure in GB-NI are provided by the Office for Zero Emission Vehicles (OZEV). The British Government has made £20 million in grant funding available to local authorities/councils in GB-NI during 2021-22 to provide charge points for residents without off-street parking. My Department has made match-funding available of circa £350k, subject to public expenditure processes, from the Department’s Blue Green Fund to assist local councils making an application to the On-street Residential Charge Point Scheme (ORCS). A local council consortium submitted a joint application to the Energy Saving Trust which manages the fund on behalf of the Office for Zero Emission Vehicles (OZEV) on 30 March 2022. You may wish to contact Ards and North Down Borough Council about its ORCS application.The current electric vehicle public charge point network is owned by the Electricity Supply Board (ESB) and is operated on a commercial basis. ESB announced in November 2021 that it had been awarded £3.27 million from the Levelling Up Fund to expand and enhance the EV charging network in the North. ESB is best placed to advise where it intends to locate charge points and it can be contacted directly at ecars@esb.ie or enquiries can be sent directly to ESB, Two Gateway, East Wall Road, DUBLIN 3, D03 A995. The market is also open to other commercial operators who would wish to provide charging infrastructure. My Department has also set up an EV Infrastructure Task-Force which brings together representatives from government, consumers, energy providers, industry and EV drivers to consider our EV Infrastructure requirements and to help set out a clear action plan by autumn 2022 to deliver a fit for purpose, modern EV charging network.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when his Department plans to resurface sections of Groomsport Road, Bangor.
answer_text: Works programmes, including resurfacing schemes, for the 2022/23 financial year cannot be finalised until my Department’s annual budget has been confirmed. Once confirmed, details of the agreed work programme for the Ards and North Down Council area will be included within the Department’s Council Report which will, in due course, be presented to Council, published on the Department’s website and also placed in the Assembly library.I can confirm that my officials are aware of the condition of Groomsport Road, Bangor and and its potential inclusion in a future resurfacing programme is under consideration.I can also advise that the previous resurfacing contract for the Ards and North Down area expired in November 2021 and following legal challenges to the award of new resurfacing contracts in spring 2021, the Department has been working at pace to develop and implement a new contract strategy. My Department is currently aiming to have a replacement contract available by late 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on plans to resurface sections of the city-bound carriageway on the Sydenham Bypass, Belfast.
answer_text: My officials are presently in the early stages of developing two tender packages of resurfacing works for the Sydenham Bypass.Package 1 includes those sections of the Bangor bound carriageway still to be resurfaced and the A55 Parkway towards Holywood Road, whilst Package 2 includes the Belfast bound side of Sydenham By-Pass, from Tillysburn to the M3 motorway.My Department operates a rolling programme of prioritised carriageway and footway resurfacing schemes and the intention is to implement Package 1 during the 2022/23 financial year. Package 2 is being planned for delivery in the 2023/24 financial year.  Progression of both of these schemes is subject to the necessary funding being available at the appropriate time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of how the recent award of city status to Bangor will help the local economy.
answer_text: Bangor has a rich history as a vibrant seaside town, and a burgeoning arts and creative community. With the new City status, it can firmly re-establish its identity as a key destination for cultural tourism, and a gateway to the rest of the stunning Ards Peninsula and wider borough.  		Bangor has been positioning itself to secure dual investment totalling over £124M, that will enable it to embark on its most ambitious regeneration journey for many years. The award of City Status gives further momentum to this. 		The Bangor Waterfront development is due to receive circa £40M of funding from Belfast Region City Deal, a bespoke package of funding from the UK Government, with the remaining investment coming from both the Council and the private sector.  		Tourism NI is providing advice and support to Ards & North Down council on elements of these tourism related projects. 		By securing these two exciting investments, the Council’s ultimate aim is to help deliver on a vision for Bangor agreed by local stakeholders in 2018 – to make Bangor a ‘destination of choice’ to live, work, study and invest in.		Bangor’s attractiveness to potential investors is growing via its easy access to Belfast, the Republic of Ireland and Great Britain; excellent digital and transport connectivity; highly educated workforce and outstanding natural environment. Key sectors operating in Bangor include Creative and Digital; Hospitality and Leisure; Business and Professional Services and Advance Manufacturing and Aerospace.		Although the awarding of city status doesn’t provide any direct local economic benefit, it would be my expectation that the increased prestige and pride associated with Bangor becoming a city, alongside the city’s Belfast Region City Deal projects, will provide further opportunities for investment into Bangor. 		Ultimately, I would hope that Bangor becomes an increasingly attractive destination to visit, for living, for businesses to locate and invest in, and for innovation – aligning with the economic transformation envisaged in my Department’s Vision for a 10x Economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department has had any discussions with the major banks regarding the closure of some of their branches.
answer_text: The regulation of financial services is a reserved matter.  In Northern Ireland, the Minister of Finance has the lead role for engaging with local banks, UK Finance, Treasury, and regulatory authorities, on issues around local banking and access to services.My officials have not held any discussions with the banks on this issue; however, the Consumer Council for Northern Ireland (CCNI) continues to engage with local banks from a consumer perspective, and useful Bank Branch Closure Advice is available on the CCNI website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of patients that have benefited to date from the Republic of Ireland Reimbursement Scheme established in June 2021.
answer_text: The ROI Reimbursement Scheme was established on 1 July 2021 for a 12 month period. Patients approved under the ROI scheme have approximately 9 months from the date of approval to arrange treatment and submit their claim for reimbursement of the HSC equivalent cost of treatment. Therefore, there is a lag between approval and reimbursement. The table below provides the number of ROI Reimbursement Scheme applications received and approved in the period 1 July 2021 to 30 April 2022.  It also provides the number of claims submitted and the number of payments authorised for the same period. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the current budget for cleaning road signage in North Down; (ii) the budget during the last five years for this purpose; and (iii) how much was spent during that period on an annual basis.
answer_text: My Department does not allocate a specific budget for cleaning of road signage but it is included within the overall budget allocation for traffic sign maintenance for each council area. The budget for traffic sign maintenance for the 2022-23 financial year cannot be confirmed until my Department’s overall budget is agreed.ii) & iii)  	As there is no specific budget for cleaning of signs my Department does not maintain details of its annual expenditure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the York Street Interchange project.
answer_text: The Placemaking and Active Travel Review, which aimed to maximise the social, economic and environmental benefits of the York Street Interchange project and its contribution to the future development of Belfast, has now been completed. The report identified a number of options to enhance placemaking and improve active travel provision. Work is ongoing to refine the shortlisted options and consider factors relating to their potential implementation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether she has any plans to support students eligible for free school meals, but who are not on school premises to take advantage of this service.
answer_text: The provision of food in schools is covered by the Arrangements for the provision of milk, meals and related facilities Under the Provisions of Articles 58 and 59 of the Education & Libraries (Northern Ireland) Order 1986.  An addendum to these arrangements was created on 25 March 2022 to advise on food provision to pupils entitled to free school meals unable to attend school due to covid-19.This addendum states that the Education Authority may provide food parcels for children, determined by the Department, who are required to attend school but, owing to Coronavirus, are or will be prevented from being in attendance for a period of 5 calendar days or more.https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/publications/milk-and-meals-arrangements-education-authority-voluntary-grammar-and-grant-maintained

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the rollout of mega clinics.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) when his Department plans to commence footpath and roadside weed spraying in North Down; and (ii) how many times will this exercise be carried out this year.
answer_text: Weed control within the Ards and North Down area is scheduled to commence before the end of May 2022, however progress will be dependent on favourable weather conditions.The Department usually undertakes an initial treatment in May/June when weeds have sufficient leaf for the spray to be effective.  Subsequent treatments are undertaken throughout the season as deemed necessary.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether contributions made in the Assembly in Irish or Ulster Scots will, going forward, have an English translation recorded in the Official Report.
answer_text: Yes, an English translation of contributions made in the Assembly in Irish or Ulster Scots will be included in the Official Report (Hansard). The Editor of Debates is currently considering the modalities around the content and presentation of such translations and will put appropriate arrangements in place as soon as possible (including for the Official Report of the sitting on Friday 13 May 2022).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many patients benefited from the decision to allocate £90m in 2021-22 to waiting list initiatives.
answer_text: A total investment of £90m non-recurrent funding was made available to the HSC in 2021/22 to help reduce waiting times for assessment and treatment across a range of services.  This funding has been used to secure additional capacity, both in-house and in the independent sector, across a range of elective specialties. This funding has been targeted at those patients with the highest clinical priority. At the end of February 2022 (month 11) approximately 216,000 Outpatient/AHP assessments, diagnostic tests and IP/DC procedures had been delivered using this funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the progress to reduce cataract in-patient day case waiting times; and for his assessment of the impact successful cataract surgical procedures can have on improving the overall quality of life for patients.
answer_text: Strenuous efforts are being made across the HSC to reduce the current long waiting times, including rebuilding activity in cataract day procedure centres and securing additional capacity by working with independent sector providers. I am pleased to note that these efforts are having a positive effect, with waiting lists and times for both routine and urgent cataract treatments starting to reduce.NICE Quality Adjusted Life Year (QALY) impacts are strong, and NICE recommends appropriate first and second eye intervention to improve patient’s quality of life and promote independent living. This aligns surgical intervention for cataracts with Programme for Government outcomes, particularly, our citizens having long, healthy, active lives.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) when will the central reservations and verges along Bangor Ring Road have the first cut carried out; and (ii) how many times does his Department plan to cut them during the current growing season.
answer_text: I can confirm that the first cut of grass along the central reservation and verges of Bangor Ring Road is planned for early June 2022.Under my Department’s current policy, it is planned to cut these areas twice this year subject to the availability of funding.
